observe the "current distribution" approaching the "target"; once all in place the collection becomes searchable.

Segment and shard assignment: who searches which piece

A big collection often has hundreds or thousands of segments and several shards, while there are many QueryNodes — so the core question is: which segment, which shard goes to which QueryNode? QueryCoord does two kinds of assignment. One is segment assignment: spreading historical, flushed segments (Lesson 12's Flushed segments) across nodes' memory, each node handling local retrieval over its share. The other is shard (channel) assignment: each shard maps to a vchannel, hosted by a delegator (shard proxy / shard leader) on some QueryNode. The delegator is that shard's "face": it both coordinates queries over all segments under the shard and takes on the shard's latest, not-yet-flushed incremental data (growing segments).

Why distinguish the "segment" and "shard/delegator" layers? Because one query must see both historical data (flushed/sealed segments) and the latest data (growing segments still flowing in memory). The delegator is the key role stitching the two together: representing one shard, it fans out queries over historical segments to the QueryNodes holding them, also queries the shard's latest increments, and finally reduces them into that shard's complete result. So the Proxy need only send queries to each shard's delegator to get an answer that misses neither "history nor latest." The details of this mechanism (cross-shard reduce) go deep in Lesson 29; here just grasp the role division.

The delegator role also explains a common question: why can Milvus make "data just written immediately searchable" (under a suitable consistency level)? Because the latest growing increments are held directly by the delegator and participate in queries — no need to wait for flush, no need to wait to be loaded — when a query reaches the delegator, it merges on the spot the "results from flushed historical segments" with the "latest in-memory increment results it holds," so neither old nor new data is missed. This is exactly the seamless relay between the write side (DataCoord's segment transitions) and the read side (QueryCoord's delegator) on the same shard: on the write side data travels from growing to flushed, while on the read side the delegator always watches this stream, ensuring queries always see a complete present.

Segment assignment

Spread flushed historical segments across QueryNodes, each doing local topK over part of the segments. The focus: spread the existing data evenly so retrieval compute scales out.

Delegator assignment

Each shard is hosted by a delegator that stitches the shard's historical-segment queries with its latest growing increments, serving as the shard's query face. The focus: ensure every data stream is covered up to the latest.

The balancer: keeping load impartial

Assignment isn't one-off. Nodes come and go, collections are added and dropped, each node's load gradually skews — so a balancer is needed to continually re-spread segments and shards. Its goal is plain yet important: don't let one QueryNode be hot and crammed while another sits idle, because overall query latency is often dragged down by the busiest one. When a new node joins, the balancer must migrate some segments over to share load; when a node leaves, its segments must be taken over elsewhere; when load stays uneven, it must proactively move things to correct it.

Milvus's balancer has more than one strategy. The simplest is round-robin: spread segments evenly purely by count, regardless of each segment's actual size or heat — simple to implement, fit for relatively uniform distributions. The finer one is the score-based strategy: scoring by a node's "real load" — segment count, row count, memory usage — and placing segments on lighter-loaded nodes. The two embody different trade-offs: round-robin for simplicity and even counts, score-based for precision and even real pressure. Both implement the same Balance interface, chosen by QueryCoord as needed.

Balancing also carries a subtle cost to weigh: migration itself isn't free. Moving a segment from node A to node B means B must re-pull the segment from object storage, reload it into memory, and rebuild necessary state, all while being careful not to disturb in-flight queries. So the balancer can't frantically shuffle at the slightest imbalance — that would throw the cluster into "constant churn with negative net gain" thrash. A good balancing strategy knows to set thresholds, leave margins, and converge slowly: it acts only when the imbalance exceeds some bar and the gain clearly outweighs the migration cost. This "restrained diligence" is exactly where distributed balancing design most tests one's skill.

internal/querycoordv2/balance/balance.goThe Balance interface: produce segment and channel move plans
type Balance interface {
    // balance segments and channels for a replica; return "how to move" plans
    BalanceReplica(ctx context.Context, replica *meta.Replica) (
        []assign.SegmentAssignPlan, []assign.ChannelAssignPlan)

    // fetch the underlying assign policy (round-robin / score-based)
    GetAssignPolicy() assign.AssignPolicy
}

// Round-robin balancer: spread segments/channels evenly by count
type RoundRobinBalancer struct { /* ... */ }

Replicas: trading redundancy for HA and throughput

When loading a collection, you can give it multiple replicas — that is, load the same collection's segments once each on different QueryNodes. Replicas buy two good things at once. First, high availability (HA): if a node holding one replica dies, another replica is still serving fine elsewhere, queries uninterrupted, availability preserved. Second, high throughput: multiple replicas can share query traffic in parallel; for the same collection, two replicas can in theory handle nearly twice the concurrent queries. Redundancy here is no waste but the source of steadiness and speed.

one collection
loaded with N replicas
load once each
replica 1 (nodes A·B)
one copy of the same segments
replica 2 (nodes C·D)
another copy, serving independently
share in parallel
HA + throughput
one dies, another takes over; ~2× queries

Of course replicas aren't free: N replicas cost N times the query memory. So replica count is a knob the user weighs by "how important this collection is, how heavy its query load" — core, ultra-concurrent collections get more replicas, obscure ones need just one. QueryCoord's ReplicaManager uniformly manages these replicas: which nodes are in each replica and which segments each carries are all kept on its ledger. This echoes Lesson 9's "coordinators hold the truth, nodes execute" division — how replicas are laid out is decided and booked by QueryCoord, while QueryNodes just load and search the segments assigned to them.

A commonly overlooked detail hides here: replicas are not simple "exact mirrors"; each independently carries the collection's full data and independently balances. The benefit: migration and scaling within one replica don't entangle another replica — faults and load are isolated from each other. When a query arrives, the Proxy picks one available replica to execute on (usually by load or proximity), so traffic is naturally spread across replicas. You could say a replica is both a "vertical safety cushion" (a backup if one dies) and a "horizontal amplifier" (concurrency magnified) — which is why we rank it the read side's most important availability and scaling lever.

Distribution vs target: the ever-running correction

Before diving into this correction mechanism, let's tabulate the core concepts of this lesson to clarify each one's role — they are the most important columns in QueryCoordV2's "ledger":

ConceptWhat it isRole
distributionthe current actual loaded staterecords "which segment is really on which node right now"
targetthe desired ideal layoutdeclares "what it should be loaded as," the reference for correction
replicaan online copy of a collectionbuys HA and throughput; N copies = N× memory
delegatora shard's query facestitches historical segments with the latest growing increments
balancerthe load re-balancercontinually spreads segments/shards evenly, avoiding hotspots

The most essential design in this "V2" architecture is that it splits state into two views. One is the distribution: what it actually looks like now — which segment is really loaded on which node at this moment, where each shard's delegator currently is. The other is the target: what it ought to look like — the desired layout computed from the collection's load request, replica count, and balancing strategy. There is almost always a gap between them: a node just died, a segment just migrated, a load just started... reality forever chasing the ideal.

QueryCoord's core loop is a swarm of observers constantly comparing these two and, on finding a gap, generating actions to close it: a segment the target wants but the distribution lacks gets scheduled to load; one the distribution has but the target no longer needs gets released; move what should move, fill what should fill. This "declare the target → continually correct the actual toward it" design is exactly the robust paradigm modern distributed systems (like Kubernetes' reconcile) acknowledge: instead of writing a long, brittle "do this step then that step" procedure, you only declare "the end state I want" and let the system converge round by round. The target itself further splits into current target and next target, to transition smoothly from old layout to new without interrupting in-flight queries. Grasp this "distribution approaching target" loop and you hold the soul that distinguishes QueryCoordV2 from the old architecture.

Declare the end state, keep correcting: QueryCoordV2 splits state into distribution (actual) and target (desired); an observer keeps comparing them, and on a gap (segC missing) emits load / release actions so the distribution converges to the target — exactly like Kubernetes reconcile: you declare the end state, the system iterates toward it, never stopping.

Why split "current" and "next" targets? Imagine a large-scale reload or rebalance: swapping the target to a brand-new layout in one step could leave queries running on the old layout suddenly unable to find segments. The two-stage target lets the system prepare the next target first, then switch smoothly once the new layout is ready, letting old queries finish and new queries use the new layout, the whole process invisible to users. This "double-buffered" switch resolves the tension of "must change, yet must not stop" cleanly. With that, from load to assignment, to balancing, to replicas, and on to this distribution-approaching-target loop, QueryCoordV2's whole picture as the read side's brain is clear — it and last lesson's DataCoord, one writing and one reading, together hold up Milvus's two ends at the distributed layer: "store soundly, search swiftly."

user load
declare the collection to search
→
QueryCoord
target·assign·balance
→
QueryNode
load segments·be delegator
↺
observer
distribution → target
🌍 The big picture
QueryCoordV2 is the read side's master scheduler. Its world starts at load: stored doesn't mean searchable — a collection's segments and indexes must be loaded into QueryNode memory to be retrievable. It does two kinds of assignment — spread historical segments across nodes, and give each shard a delegator (stitching historical segments with the latest growing increments); it uses a balancer (round-robin / score-based, one Balance interface) to keep load even; it uses replicas to buy HA and throughput. Most essential is "distribution vs target": observers keep converging "actual loaded state" toward "desired layout" — declare the end state, continually correct — the root of its robustness.
📌 Key points
  • load / release: stored ≠ searchable; a collection's segments and indexes must be loaded into QueryNode memory to be retrievable; release returns the memory.
  • Two assignments: segment assignment (spread historical segments across nodes) + shard assignment (each shard gets a delegator stitching historical segments with the latest growing increments).
  • Balancer: continually re-spreads segments/shards to avoid hotspots; has round-robin and score-based strategies, both implementing the Balance interface.
  • Replicas: the same collection loaded once each on different nodes, buying HA (a replica survives a node death) and throughput (parallel query sharing), at the cost of N× memory.
  • Distribution vs target: distribution = actual current state, target = desired layout; observers continually correct the actual toward the target (with current/next target for smooth transition).

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. QueryCoordV2's world starts at 'load'. Why does 'stored' not equal 'searchable'?
  1. Because object storage forbids reads by default
  2. Because stored segments are auto-encrypted and need decryption to query
  3. Because queries must first be approved by DataCoord
  4. Segments and indexes must be loaded into QueryNode memory to be retrievable; load brings data into memory, release returns it
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: D. Search happens in QueryNode memory. Flushing only persists; to be searchable, segments and indexes must be loaded into memory. load/release toggles this 'searchability' and is the starting point of QueryCoord's scheduling
2. The core of QueryCoordV2's robustness is 'distribution converges to target'. What does this most precisely mean?
  1. target = desired layout, distribution = actual loaded state; observers continually compare and correct so the actual keeps approaching the desired - declare the end state, keep correcting
  2. Load all segments onto one node at once
  3. distribution is a config file, target is a runtime log
  4. The two must always be exactly equal or the cluster halts
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: A. This is declarative scheduling: you describe the desired end state (target), and the system observes the actual state (distribution) and continually corrects. Node crashes and skew are auto-healed by convergence, not one-shot imperative commands
3. What do 'replicas' buy QueryCoordV2, and at what cost?
  1. The same collection loaded once each on different nodes, buying HA (one dies, others survive) and throughput (parallel query sharing), at the cost of N times the memory
  2. Replicas are backup-only and never serve queries
  3. Replicas reduce total memory usage
  4. Compresses data to save memory but lowers recall
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: A. Replicas trade memory for availability and throughput: each replica loads independently on different nodes, so a node death is survived and queries can be shared in parallel; the cost is memory growing linearly with replica count
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • In your cluster one QueryNode keeps becoming a query hotspot (CPU pinned) while others sit idle. Using 'balancer + distribution/target', explain how QueryCoordV2 auto-mitigates this, and when it might still be powerless (needing human intervention).

Metadata & coordination

The last five lessons introduced the control/data planes and the four roles: Proxy, RootCoord, DataCoord, QueryCoordV2. But the "global map" each one holds — a collection's schema, where segments live, who loaded what, which node is still alive — where is it stored, and how do they stay in sync? This lesson is the glue that binds the whole architecture: etcd as the metadata store, sessions for service discovery, the metastore/Catalog abstraction over metadata I/O, the kv layer for primitives, and watch so coordinators assign work and nodes pick it up.

🔌 Analogy
Think of the metadata system as a building's front desk + archive room. etcd is the front desk: every resident (component) signs the register on move-in — "who I am, which unit, am I still here" — and gets a keycard that must be periodically renewed (a lease); forget to renew and the desk knows you've moved out. The archive room (object storage) holds the genuinely bulky things — furniture, equipment (data and index files); the desk only notes "the piano belongs to unit 7, shelf 3 in storeroom" — it never drags the piano to the front desk. And the Catalog is the archivist: everyone who reads or writes archives goes through that one standard window, never rummaging the storeroom themselves.

The whole picture: metadata from coordinators down to etcd

Stack what this lesson covers and you won't get lost. At the top are the coordinators — RootCoord/DataCoord/QueryCoord — the producers and consumers of metadata: creating a collection writes a schema, segmenting records a segment, loading saves a replica. They never hand-assemble etcd keys; they call the next layer's metastore Catalog interface, reading/writing metadata with "business verbs" like CreateCollection, AddSegment, SaveReplica. Below Catalog sits the kv layer — serializing "a Collection object" into "a series of key/values" and calling primitives like Save/MultiSave/LoadWithPrefix. At the very bottom, kv actually writes bytes into etcd (small, critical metadata) or hands bulk data to object storage.

produce/consumeCoordinators
RootCoord/DataCoord/QueryCoord read/write metadata while deciding: schema, segments, indexes, replicas, load state. They speak only "business verbs," never touch storage details.
abstract interfacemetastore · Catalog
RootCoordCatalog / DataCoordCatalog / QueryCoordCatalog: abstract "persist collection/segment/replica metadata" into interfaces, hiding whether the backend is etcd or something else.
primitiveskv layer
BaseKV / TxnKV / MetaKv / WatchKV (pkg/kv/kv.go): Save/Load/Remove, transactions, leases, watchable. Implementations in internal/kv/etcd.
where it landsetcd · object storage
Small, critical metadata goes to etcd (strongly consistent, watchable); bulky vector/index files go to object storage, with metadata keeping only a reference path.
1

Receive DDL

RootCoord gets CreateCollection, validates the schema, allocates a collectionID.

2

Call a business verb

calls Catalog.CreateCollection — it only says "create a collection", untouched by storage details.

3

Lower to kv primitives

Catalog serializes the object into key/values and calls kv.Save/MultiSave.

4

Real landing

kv writes the bytes into etcd (strongly consistent, watchable); bulky files go to object storage, keeping only a reference.

The essence of this picture is one line: coordinators speak "business language," Catalog translates to "key-value language," and kv writes it into etcd. Each layer down is more generic and cares less about business meaning. Because Catalog is that translator, RootCoord never needs to know "how many keys a collection is split into in etcd"; and when the storage backend is swapped (say etcd for TiKV), the upper layers change not a single line. That is layered decoupling applied to the metadata system.

Walk "create a collection" end to end to feel the pipeline. The client sends CreateCollection, Proxy forwards it to RootCoord; RootCoord first allocates a global timestamp and some IDs from itself (Lesson 11), assembles the schema into a model.Collection domain object, then calls RootCoordCatalog.CreateCollection(ctx, coll, ts). At Catalog the object is decomposed: one key for the collection, one per field, one per partition... then kv.MultiSave writes that batch of key/values into etcd atomically in one transaction. Across the whole chain RootCoord only said "create a collection"; "into how many keys, and how to keep it atomic" was quietly absorbed by the two layers below. That is the hallmark of good abstraction: the upper intent is simple, the lower complexity is encapsulated.

etcd: the cluster's coordination hub: coordinators write authoritative metadata by transaction/CAS and watch for changes; nodes register a leased session (kept alive by keepalive, auto-deleted on crash). But the real search/insert traffic is point-to-point between nodes and never touches etcd — control through etcd, data direct: a divide that runs through all of Milvus.

etcd and sessions: how components discover each other

Metadata must "persist," but components must also "find" each other. Lesson 9 mentioned: at startup each component calls Register(), writing a leased session into etcd — "I am querynode-3, my gRPC address is here, I'm alive." This lives in internal/util/sessionutil/session_util.go: a Session struct holds a LeaseID, and a background goroutine keeps renewing the lease (keepalive); once the process crashes and the heartbeat stops, the lease expires and etcd auto-deletes the session record.

node Register()
write a leased session: who/address/alive
keepalive
etcd session dir
alive→renew; crashed→lease expires, auto-deleted
WatchServices
coordinator
gets up/down events → holds the live-node list

On the other end, coordinators call WatchServices to watch the prefix directory of these sessions: a node comes up and an "added" event fires instantly; a node drops (lease expires) and a "deleted" event fires. So the control plane always holds a live roster of nodes, using it to assign segments, balance load, and reassign work on failure. This "register → renew → watch" is Milvus's service discovery — it shares the same etcd as metadata, but carries the transient state of "who's here, where," not the persistent metadata of "what a collection looks like."

Reiterate that red line from Lesson 9: a session stores an "address," not "data." On this path etcd is an "address book + heartbeat board"; it tells you "who's here, where," but real search/insert traffic never passes through etcd — that would instantly overwhelm it. Once a component has the peer's address, it sends requests over direct point-to-point gRPC. Control info via etcd, data traffic direct — a divide that runs through all of Milvus.

Why insist on "lease + renewal" instead of simply writing an alive=true flag? Because the hardest thing in a distributed system is not "graceful exit" but "sudden disappearance" — a process gets kill -9'd, a machine loses power, a cable is yanked. In those cases nobody gets a chance to flip the flag to false. The beauty of a lease is that it makes "being alive" something you must keep paying to maintain: the moment the heartbeat stops, etcd itself purges the record when the lease expires, with no cleanup by anyone. This is a "failure is the default" design — the system assumes a node may vanish at any time, and being alive is the exception that must be continually proven. Grasp this and you grasp the same philosophy behind nearly every distributed heartbeat mechanism.

One practical detail worth noting: all these keys (metadata and sessions alike) hang under a unified metaRoot prefix (config-driven, e.g. by-dev/meta). This lets one etcd cluster isolate multiple Milvus instances — each uses its own prefix, never crossing wires. It's also why LoadWithPrefix and WatchWithPrefix appear everywhere in the source: in etcd's flat key/value space, a prefix is a namespace, the basic device by which Milvus organizes its whole metadata graph.

metastore and Catalog: the "archive interface" for metadata

Now the core abstraction. internal/metastore/catalog.go defines several interfaces split by coordinator: RootCoordCatalog owns databases/collections/partitions/aliases/credentials/roles; DataCoordCatalog owns segments (ListSegments/AddSegment/DropSegment), channels, indexes; QueryCoordCatalog owns load info (SaveCollection/SaveReplica/SaveCollectionTargets). Every method is a business verb, and its arguments are domain objects like model.Collection, datapb.SegmentInfo, querypb.Replica — not raw bytes.

Notice the symmetry: the three catalog interfaces map exactly onto the three coordinators from Lessons 11–13. RootCoord owns "what exists" (schema/DDL), DataCoord owns "where data sits" (segments/channels), QueryCoord owns "what's loaded" (replicas/targets). Each coordinator persists only its own slice of the world through its own catalog, and the slices barely overlap. That clean partition is why the three roles can be reasoned about — and packaged into one MixCoord process — without their metadata stepping on each other. The Catalog split is the storage-layer mirror of the responsibility split you already learned.

Note that many RootCoordCatalog methods carry a ts typeutil.Timestamp argument — because RootCoord's metadata is multi-versioned: DDL operations hang on a timestamp, so "the schema as seen at a given instant" can be reproduced exactly. Historically this was done by a "snapshot kv" layer — storing different versions of the same key as multiple records keyed by timestamp, reading the latest "no later than instant T." It makes consistency levels and time-travel reads possible. Today Catalog's concrete impl (internal/metastore/kv/rootcoord) holds a kv.TxnKV and splits domain objects into key/value pairs.

Funneling metadata I/O through the Catalog interface has another big engineering payoff: testability. To test RootCoord's create-collection logic you needn't spin up a real etcd — hand it a mock Catalog, or an in-memory kv (internal/kv/mem), and tests run fast and stable. This is the same move as Lesson 9's "interfaces as pluggable seams": wherever code touches the outside world (storage, network), insulate it behind an interface, so production wires in real etcd while tests wire in a fake. The many mock_*.go files in the Milvus source are largely auto-generated for exactly these Catalog/kv interfaces. Abstraction is not only for elegance, but to be independently verifiable.

internal/metastore/catalog.goCatalog interfaces (excerpt)
// RootCoord metadata: db/collection/partition; methods carry ts (multi-version)
type RootCoordCatalog interface {
    CreateCollection(ctx, coll *model.Collection, ts Timestamp) error
    GetCollectionByID(ctx, dbID, ts Timestamp, id UniqueID) (*model.Collection, error)
    DropCollection(ctx, coll *model.Collection, ts Timestamp) error
    // ... partitions, aliases, credentials, roles ...
}

// DataCoord metadata: segments and channels
type DataCoordCatalog interface {
    ListSegments(ctx, collectionID int64) ([]*datapb.SegmentInfo, error)
    AddSegment(ctx, segment *datapb.SegmentInfo) error
    DropSegment(ctx, segment *datapb.SegmentInfo) error
}

The kv layer: the primitives beneath Catalog

Below Catalog is a set of incrementally stacked interfaces in pkg/kv/kv.go. At the bottom is BaseKV: the plainest create/read/delete — Save/Load/Remove/MultiSave/LoadWithPrefix. Above it, TxnKV adds transactions (MultiSaveAndRemove — multiple writes and deletes all succeed or all fail, crucial for "alter schema while deleting old keys"). Above that, MetaKv adds CompareVersionAndSwap (version-based optimistic locking). At the top, WatchKV adds Watch — the very foundation of service discovery and the "assign work" mechanism.

This "interface-on-interface" stacking is the same engineering aesthetic as Component in Lesson 9: depend on exactly the layer whose capabilities you need. A module that only reads config depends on BaseKV; a Catalog that needs transactions depends on TxnKV; service discovery that watches for changes depends on WatchKV. Implementations live in internal/kv/etcd (the etcd version) and internal/kv/mem (an in-memory version for tests). Here is the contract stacking from BaseKV all the way up to WatchKV:

pkg/kv/kv.goBaseKV → TxnKV → MetaKv → WatchKV
// the plainest create/read/delete
type BaseKV interface {
    Load(ctx, key) (string, error)
    Save(ctx, key, value) error
    Remove(ctx, key) error
    LoadWithPrefix(ctx, key) ([]string, []string, error)
    // ... MultiSave / MultiRemove / Has ...
}

type TxnKV  interface { BaseKV; MultiSaveAndRemove(...) }   // +transactions
type MetaKv interface { TxnKV; CompareVersionAndSwap(...) }  // +CAS (version)
type WatchKV interface { MetaKv; Watch(ctx, key) WatchChan } // +watch

That CompareVersionAndSwap (compare-then-swap, i.e. CAS) on MetaKv deserves a callout: it is the basis of optimistic locking. Imagine two coordinator replicas both wanting to edit the same metadata; if each writes blindly, the later write silently overwrites the earlier — a lost update. CAS instead "writes carrying the version I saw" — the write succeeds only if etcd's version still matches what I read; if someone changed it meanwhile (version moved), the write fails and the caller must re-read and retry. This blocks the read-then-write race at the storage layer, sparing upper layers from rolling their own distributed lock. Transactions guarantee atomicity of a group of writes; CAS guarantees one write isn't overwritten — together they uphold metadata correctness under concurrency.

What goes where: etcd vs object storage

A common question: with both etcd and object storage, what goes in which? The answer is a single line drawn on size and role — small, critical "metadata" that needs strong consistency and watching goes to etcd; bulky "data and indexes" that only need reliable storage go to object storage. Metadata keeps only a reference path into object storage. This keeps etcd forever "thin," so watch and consistency hold up; object storage grows linearly with data but never participates in consistency coordination — it's cheap and stackable without limit.

This divide is not fastidiousness but is forced by etcd's hard constraints. etcd is a Raft-based strongly consistent store; every write must reach consensus across a majority of replicas — which makes it reliable but also "expensive": it imposes conservative caps on single-value size and total data volume, with throughput far below object storage. If someone took a shortcut and stuffed a few-hundred-MB vector binlog straight into etcd, at best it drags down watch and leader election, at worst it paralyzes the cluster's whole metadata layer. So "bulk data always sinks to object storage; etcd keeps only the path" is not optional but a red line. Remember it and you'll instantly grasp the crux of any "metadata vs data" layering: spend strong consistency where it matters most, and don't make it pay for massive data.

ContentWhereWhy
collection schema, partitions, aliasesetcdsmall, critical, needs strong consistency + versions
segment metadata (SegmentInfo, binlog paths)etcdrecords "where a segment is"; tiny itself
load info, replicas, targetsetcdQueryCoord's scheduling basis; needs watch
discovery session (address + lease)etcdtransient state, auto-expires via lease
vector binlogs, index filesobject storagebulky; only needs reliable storage; metadata keeps a reference
write-ahead log (WAL)message queuethe carrier for Lesson 7's "log-as-data"

While here, nail down the three external dependencies once more from the metadata angle: etcd owns "state and coordination," object storage owns "bulk data," the message queue owns "the write pipeline." Lesson 8 met them from a deployment angle; here you see their roles in the metadata path. Each guards its own turf without trespassing — the underlying support that lets Milvus cleanly split "store" from "compute," and "control" from "data."

watch: coordinators assign, nodes pick up

The final piece is how the control plane pushes decisions down to the data plane. The mechanism is still etcd watch, but reversed: instead of nodes registering and coordinators watching, coordinators write the "assignment plan" into etcd and nodes watch their own slice. For instance, DataCoord decides "vchannel-A is handled by datanode-2" and writes that channel assignment into etcd; datanode-2 watches the key belonging to it and starts consuming that channel. QueryCoord assigns segments similarly — which is exactly the previous lesson's "distribution converging to target" realized at the storage layer: the target is written into etcd, and nodes watch and act on it.

node Register()
writes leased session to etcd
→
coord WatchServices
learns "who's alive, where"
→
coord writes assignment
channel/segment plan into etcd
→
node watches, picks up
sees its own key, starts executing

Collapse the whole chain to one line: etcd is both the "filing cabinet" (stores metadata) and the "bulletin board" (publishes assignments, drives discovery). Components rarely shout directly to fix state; instead they cooperate indirectly via "write etcd / watch etcd" — the physical realization of Lesson 9's discipline "cross-plane communication only via interfaces and etcd." Once you understand where metadata lives, how components discover each other, and how coordinators assign work, you hold the last key that runs through the entire distributed part.

One last reality: "watch is not as simple as it looks." Networks jitter, connections drop, and once a watch disconnects and reconnects, the few events in between may be missed. Milvus's answer: remember a revision (a version watermark) when watching, and after a reconnect resume from that watermark rather than starting over; the sturdier pattern is "full pull once, then incremental watch" — first take a current snapshot with GetSessions/LoadWithPrefix to align state, then open the watch carrying that snapshot's revision, so you neither miss events nor double-process them. You'll notice WatchServices returns a revision to the caller for exactly this. The reminder is: watch is a "notification," not the "truth" — the truth is always the current state in etcd; the notification just saves you from polling. Read any "watch for changes" code with this mindset and you won't be fooled by the illusion that "events are never lost."

🌍 The big picture
In one line: coordinators speak business language → Catalog translates to key-value language → kv writes it into etcd (small, critical metadata) or object storage (bulky data/index). In parallel, components Register() a leased session and coordinators WatchServices for discovery; assignment goes the other way — coordinators write the plan into etcd and nodes watch to pick it up. So etcd plays both "filing cabinet + bulletin board": it persists state and lets the two planes cooperate indirectly. That is the glue binding all five earlier roles together.
📌 Key points
  • Three layers down: coordinators (business verbs) → metastore's Catalog interfaces → kv primitives → etcd / object storage. Each layer down is more generic, less business-aware.
  • Catalog abstraction: internal/metastore/catalog.go splits by coordinator into RootCoordCatalog / DataCoordCatalog / QueryCoordCatalog, hiding the backend; RootCoord's methods carry ts (multi-version/snapshot).
  • kv layer: pkg/kv/kv.go stacks BaseKV → TxnKV → MetaKv → WatchKV (CRUD → transactions → CAS/version → watch), implemented in internal/kv/etcd.
  • What goes where: small, critical metadata needing strong consistency + watch goes to etcd; bulky vectors/indexes go to object storage, with metadata keeping only a reference path.
  • Discovery + assignment: components Register() a leased session and coordinators WatchServices for up/down; assignment is reversed — coordinators write plans into etcd and nodes watch to pick up.

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. From coordinator down to etcd, which layers does a CreateCollection traverse, and what is each layer's role?
  1. The coordinator hand-assembles etcd keys and writes with no middle layer
  2. Write object storage first, then etcd syncs asynchronously
  3. The Proxy writes SQL straight into etcd
  4. coordinator (business verb) → metastore's Catalog interface (translate to key-value language) → kv primitives (Save/MultiSave) → etcd / object storage; each layer down is more generic, less business-aware
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: D. Three layers: coordinators speak business language, Catalog translates and decomposes objects, kv offers Save/MultiSave primitives, finally writing etcd. Each layer is more generic, so swapping the backend leaves upper layers untouched - layered decoupling
2. Why must vector binlogs and index files go to object storage rather than being stuffed into etcd?
  1. etcd cannot store binary, only text
  2. etcd is a Raft strongly-consistent store with conservative caps on value size and total volume; stuffing bulk data drags down watch and leader election. Bulk data should sink to object storage, with etcd keeping only a path reference
  3. Because index files must be SQL-queryable
  4. Object storage is more strongly consistent than etcd, so bulk goes there
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. The divide is by size and role: small, critical metadata needing strong consistency and watch goes to etcd; bulky data/index needing only reliable storage goes to object storage. Spend strong consistency where it matters, don't make it pay for massive data
3. Components register a leased session in etcd instead of writing an alive=true flag. What is the key benefit of this 'lease + renewal' design?
  1. It requires every node to manually deregister before exit
  2. It makes 'alive' a state that must be continually renewed; on kill-9 or power loss the heartbeat stops, the lease expires, and etcd auto-purges the record with no cleanup by anyone
  3. It stores query data inside the session to speed things up
  4. It routes search traffic through etcd
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. 'Failure is the default': the system assumes a node may vanish suddenly with no chance to flip a flag. A lease makes being alive an exception that must be continually proven; stop the heartbeat and the record auto-vanishes. A session stores an address, not data; traffic still goes direct
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • 'Watch is a notification, not the truth.' Explain this: when a coordinator's watch drops and reconnects on a network jitter and misses some events, what mechanisms (revision; full-pull-then-incremental) keep nodes eventually getting the correct assignment without 'missing work'.

Insert via Proxy

The first three parts toured the whole cluster and every coordinator. From here we follow one row of data on its journey — from a client's insert call all the way to a searchable segment in object storage. The first stop is our old friend the Proxy, but this time we don't watch it gate or schedule; we focus on what "processing" it does on the write path. By the end you'll see the Proxy's write-path role is more like a workshop turning raw material into a semi-finished part: in comes a messy batch of rows, out go sharded, timestamped, ordered messages ready to drop straight into the WAL.

📮 Analogy
Picture an insert as mailing a batch of parcels nationwide. You hand boxes of parcels (a batch of rows) to the post-office counter (the Proxy): the clerk first checks the labels (schema validation: right fields, right types), assigns a tracking number to any parcel missing one (primary-key auto-fill), then sorts parcels into chutes by postcode (hashing rows by primary key to vchannels). Once sorted, the whole batch gets one common postmark timestamp and rides the conveyor together (appended to the WAL). The counter delivers nothing and warehouses nothing, yet the batch is all-or-nothing — that is the write's "atomic batch." This whole routine — check label, assign number, sort, postmark, onto the conveyor — is the Proxy's entire job on the write path; it is fast, light, and replicable, leaving the real "delivery and warehousing" to later roles.

Gate one: schema validation and the primary key

Once an insert reaches the Proxy it is wrapped as an insertTask and first runs data-correctness checks in PreExecute. The core step is checkPrimaryFieldData: it confirms the batch's primary-key column is valid and consistent with the schema. If the collection uses auto-ID, the client never sends a primary key, so this step assigns a globally unique key to every row and fills it in; with a user-supplied key it checks non-null, uniqueness, and type. Only after this does the batch's primary-key IDs become settled.

1

Wrap into an insertTask

the insert enters the Proxy; PreExecute runs data-correctness checks.

2

Validate the PK column

checkPrimaryFieldData: is the primary key valid and consistent with the schema?

3

autoID? allocate : validate

auto → allocate a globally unique PK per row and fill it in; user-supplied → check non-null/unique/type.

4

PK IDs settled

with PKs fixed, they can route to a shard (the next gate).

Why settle the primary key so early? Because the next step — "which row goes to which shard" — depends entirely on it. The key is not just an identity card, it is the routing basis; without a settled key, no later step can proceed. Think of it as "stamp a unique tracking number on every parcel before you can sort by it." It is also the Proxy's last semantic validation of client data: once a batch is admitted into the write pipeline, the backend trusts it is "well-formed with complete keys" and never re-validates, saving precious compute for the actual persistence.

A design trade-off worth unpacking hides here: who generates the primary key. With auto-ID, Milvus assigns the key uniformly at the Proxy layer — it asks the coordinator for a range of globally unique IDs and slices it across this batch. The upside: clients never worry about uniqueness; the downside: you cannot use a business-meaningful value (say, an order number) as the key. Conversely, a user-supplied key hands generation to the application — flexible, but you must guarantee no duplicates. Either way, validation must finish before the write: once data enters the WAL, the key becomes that record's "indelible identity," and all later updates, deletes, and dedup align by it. That is why Milvus would rather spend a little extra validation up front than let a batch with a "possibly-illegal key" into the log — a textbook case of front-loading validation to keep the backend simple.

Note one easily-missed detail too: this step also aligns the client-supplied fields with the schema. The client may have omitted a field with a default, written columns out of order, or carried a dynamic field not in the schema (Lesson 6). checkPrimaryFieldData and its surrounding checks normalize all of this: fill defaults, reorder by schema, fold legal dynamic fields into $meta. Once this gate is passed, what flows into the write pipeline is a "clean, normalized, fully-keyed" batch, and the backend never has to fuss over format again.

internal/proxy/task_insert.gosettling the primary key in PreExecute
// validate/fill primary key, return the batch IDs; auto-ID allocates here
it.result.IDs, err = checkPrimaryFieldData(ctx, allFields, it.schema, it.insertMsg)
if err != nil {
    mlog.Warn(ctx, "check primary field data and hash primary key failed", mlog.Err(err))
    return err
}

Gate two: hash rows by primary key to vchannels

After validation comes Execute. The Proxy first asks its cache for the collection's virtual-channel list (chMgr.getVChannels(collID)) — recall Lesson 7: a collection is cut into several shards, each backed by a vchannel. The key action now is assignChannelsByPK: it takes each row's primary key, hashes it, and routes the row index to a vchannel by hash value, producing a "channel → row-offset list" map.

This step decides the physical distribution of data. The same primary key always hashes to the same vchannel, so every version of a row (including later updates and deletes) lands in the same shard — crucial for "delete by primary key" and "which segment does this row belong to" later (Lessons 17 and 20). Hash sharding also gives load balance: huge batches spread evenly across vchannels with no hot shard. Grasp this and you understand Milvus's bedrock logic: scale out by sharding, shard by primary-key hash.

Distinguish two easily-confused concepts here: vchannel (virtual channel) versus pchannel (physical channel). What the Proxy hashes to is a vchannel — a logical notion of "a shard of a collection," born when the collection is created and gone when it is dropped. What actually carries the WAL is a pchannel — a physical log partition, fixed in number, pre-opened by the cluster (one per message-queue topic). Many vchannels from many collections multiplex the same pchannel. That mapping (vchannel → pchannel) is detailed next lesson; here just remember: the Proxy cares about the logical shard, the vchannel, and the vchannel name itself encodes which pchannel it belongs to, so the write finds the right WAL.

One subtlety more: if the collection sets a partition key (Lesson 6), the sharding logic takes another branch, repackInsertDataWithPartitionKeyForStreamingService — beyond hash sharding it also routes rows into the correct logical partition by partition key. But the skeleton is unchanged: still "compute membership by some key, then group and pack." With or without a partition key, the Proxy's mission at this layer is the same: deterministically cut an unordered batch of rows into "one group per vchannel." Determinism matters — the same input always yields the same sharding, so retries and replays never scramble the data.

a batch of rows
with primary-key IDs
→
assignChannelsByPK
hash each PK
→
vchannel A
rows 0,3,7…
＋
vchannel B
rows 1,2,5…

Gate three: pack into messages, Append to the WAL

With rows sharded, the Proxy repacks the rows on each vchannel into individual insert messages (see repackInsertDataForStreamingService), each carrying its vchannel, collection ID, partition info, and schema version. Note one key change: the segment ID is left empty (set to 0) here — "which segment these rows go to" is no longer decided by the Proxy but deferred to the StreamingNode when it writes the WAL (detailed in Lesson 17). The Proxy only "shards + packs," never "segments."

The streaming.WAL() here returns a StreamingClient (WAL accessor). It is an in-process client wrapping three capabilities — Append / Read / Broadcast — with built-in service discovery and auto-reconnect: the Proxy need not know "which machine the StreamingNode responsible for a given pchannel is on right now"; the StreamingClient finds the route and reconnects on drops. So the Proxy-side code is strikingly simple — one AppendMessages, with all the addressing, networking, and retries swallowed by this client layer. That is what a good interface looks like: keep complexity to yourself, hand simplicity to the caller.

The final, central step: the Proxy calls streaming.WAL().AppendMessages(ctx, msgs...) to append the batch to the WAL. This is the essence of the new write path — Client → Proxy → StreamingClient.Append → StreamingNode → WAL backend — not the old "Proxy publishes to a message queue, DataNode consumes" model. On success the Proxy fills the returned MaxTimeTick back as this write's timestamp, used for session consistency (what you just wrote is readable next moment).

Why take "segmenting" out of the Proxy's hands and defer it to the StreamingNode? Because a segment is stateful: how many rows a growing segment can hold and when to seal it depend on this shard's current write progress, and only the StreamingNode that exclusively owns that pchannel knows it most accurately. The Proxy is stateless and replicable (Lesson 10); if each Proxy decided segment IDs on its own, several Proxies writing the same shard concurrently would clash. Centralizing segmenting in the single StreamingNode guarantees consistent assignment and keeps the Proxy lightweight. This is the "stateless to the stateless, stateful to the stateful" layering principle showing up once more on the write path.

Proxy stepwhat it doeskey symbol
validate PKvalidate/fill the key, allocate on auto-IDcheckPrimaryFieldData
get shardsfetch the collection's vchannel listchMgr.getVChannels
hash-shardroute rows to vchannels by PKassignChannelsByPK
packone group of InsertMessages per vchannel, segmentID emptyrepackInsertData…
append WALatomic batch append, get back a TimeTickWAL().AppendMessages
internal/proxy/task_insert_streaming.goinsertTask.Execute: shard → pack → Append
// 1) get the collection's vchannel list
channelNames, err := it.chMgr.getVChannels(collID)
// 2) hash-shard by PK, repack into insert messages (segmentID left 0)
msgs, err := repackInsertDataForStreamingService(
    it.TraceCtx(), channelNames, it.insertMsg, it.result, ez, it.schemaVersion)
// 3) append the whole batch to the WAL
resp := streaming.WAL().AppendMessages(ctx, msgs...)
// 4) use the returned max TimeTick as this write's timestamp
it.result.Timestamp = resp.MaxTimeTick()

Wiring the pipeline together

String the Proxy's write-path actions into one line and a clean "processing" chain appears: validate → settle PK → hash-shard → pack → append to WAL. Each step paves the way for the next, turning "a messy batch of rows" into "sharded, timestamped, ordered messages sitting in the WAL waiting to be consumed."

1

receive insert

wrapped as insertTask into dmQueue (Lesson 10).

2

validate + settle PK

checkPrimaryFieldData

auto-ID assigns a globally unique key per row.

3

hash to vchannel

assignChannelsByPK

same PK always lands on the same shard.

4

pack into messages

one group of InsertMessages per vchannel; segmentID left empty.

5

Append to WAL

WAL().AppendMessages

whole batch appended atomically; get back a TimeTick.

6

return to client

success returns at once; persistence happens asynchronously in the background.

Timestamps: how a write is ordered globally

Running through this whole lesson, yet never addressed head-on, is the timestamp. Remember Lessons 3 and 10 stressing "every write needs a timestamp"? On the write path its role is subtler but more crucial than on reads. As each insert message enters the WAL it is stamped with a monotonically increasing timestamp (TimeTick). It is not a wall clock but the WAL's logical sequence number: whoever enters the log first gets the smaller timestamp. This "logical ruler" is exactly what lets writes scattered across shards and arriving from many clients be ordered into one globally ordered timeline.

The payoff only fully materializes on reads. Recall the guarantee timestamp from Lesson 10: a search carries "please consume at least up to this point," and judging "have we reached it?" relies precisely on these write-time TimeTicks. In other words, the write side faithfully orders every record so the read side can precisely control visibility — strong consistency reads the latest timestamp, bounded staleness tolerates a slightly older one. Write and read are quietly stitched together by the same timestamp ruler. That is why the Proxy fills the MaxTimeTick from AppendMessages back to the client: it is this write's "coordinate" on the global timeline; holding it, the client's next query can demand "read at least up to this coordinate" and thus read its own just-written data.

WAL log: ordered by monotonically increasing TimeTick
t₁
insert A
t₂
insert B
t₃
insert C
t₄
this write
t₅
…later
read with guarantee ts = t₄: consuming up to t₄ suffices to answer, guaranteeing A/B/C and this write are seen

Keep this timeline in mind and you hold the skeleton of the whole write path: the Proxy validates, shards, packs, and finally appends messages in timestamp order to the WAL; the backend consumes, segments, and persists in that order; reads then use timestamps to control what is visible. A row's journey is essentially the process of it finding its place on this global timeline.

Atomic batches and what "write succeeded" means

One last commonly-misread point: what does an insert returning success actually mean? It does not mean the data has landed in segment files in object storage — that is done asynchronously in the background (Lesson 17). It means the batch of messages was reliably appended to the WAL, and the WAL is Milvus's single source of truth. Once in the WAL, even if the StreamingNode crashes immediately, it can replay from the WAL on restart and lose nothing. So "write succeeded" = "durably logged," not "flushed to a segment."

That is also the origin of the atomic batch: a batch of inserts either all enter the WAL and become visible together, or the whole batch fails. The Proxy decides this uniformly from AppendMessages's return — UnwrapFirstError() fails the whole batch if any one message failed. This all-or-nothing semantics means the client never sees a half-applied state. Remember this lesson: the Proxy is the write's processing workshop; the WAL is the write's finish line. Next lesson we step into the WAL itself.

The Proxy translates insert into the WAL's language: validate PK → hash to shard → pack the format → append in order, the whole batch entering the WAL atomically. So "success" = reliably appended to the WAL (the single source of truth), not flushed to a segment — flushing happens asynchronously in the background.

Honesty about a boundary, though: when a batch is split across multiple vchannels, AppendMessages appends per vchannel, and strict atomicity is not guaranteed across vchannels — its promise is "messages belonging to the same vchannel are written together as one transaction." Scenarios that truly need atomic effect across multiple physical channels (certain DDL/DCL) take another path, Broadcast (mentioned next lesson). For the vast majority of inserts a batch often lands on one or a few vchannels, and combined with the determinism of PK hashing that is already enough to give the client the sense that "this write succeeded as a whole." Understand this boundary and you won't misread "an insert's atomic batch" as "an arbitrary cross-shard distributed transaction" — what Milvus does here is a pragmatic trade-off, not an expensive two-phase commit.

Finally, pull the camera back to the throughline. What you saw this lesson is how the Proxy translates an insert into a language the WAL understands: validate-and-settle the key, hash-and-settle the shard, pack-and-settle the format, append-and-settle the order. Having done all this, the data rests safely in the log, waiting to be consumed, segmented, and persisted — which is exactly the subject of the next two lessons. Remember the shape of this pipeline; every later step will line up against it precisely.

🌍 Big picture
In one line: insert enters the Proxy → validate and settle the primary key (checkPrimaryFieldData, auto-ID allocates) → hash by PK to vchannels (assignChannelsByPK) → pack into InsertMessages (segmentID left for the StreamingNode) → streaming.WAL().AppendMessages appends the whole batch to the WAL → fill MaxTimeTick back as the timestamp and return. Write success = in the WAL (single source of truth), which is not the same as flushed to a segment. Carve the shape of this pipeline into memory; every later step — consume, segment, persist, compact — will line up against it.
📌 Key points
  • Primary key first: checkPrimaryFieldData validates/fills the key; auto-ID assigns a globally unique ID per row, and the key is the basis for later routing.
  • Hash sharding: assignChannelsByPK routes rows to vchannels by PK; the same key always lands on the same shard.
  • Segment ID deferred: the Proxy leaves segmentID empty when packing; segmenting is the StreamingNode's job (Lesson 17).
  • Write path: streaming.WAL().AppendMessages appends the batch to the WAL — i.e. "Client → Proxy → StreamingClient.Append → StreamingNode → WAL backend."
  • Atomic batch: the whole batch enters the WAL and becomes visible, or fails together; write success = durably logged, not flushed to a segment.

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. Why does the Proxy settle the primary key with checkPrimaryFieldData first, before sharding the insert?
  1. Because the primary key decides which distance metric the vectors use
  2. Because routing 'which row to which vchannel' hashes the primary key (assignChannelsByPK); without a settled key there is no routing, and with auto-ID this step also assigns a globally unique key per row
  3. Because the segment ID must equal the primary key
  4. Because vectors can't be compressed without a key first
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. The key is both identity and routing basis: sharding hashes it, deletes/updates align the same row by it. So it must be settled before the write (auto-ID allocates here); the whole pipeline then trusts keys are complete
2. The Proxy leaves segmentID empty (0) when packing, deferring segmenting to the StreamingNode. What is the main benefit?
  1. A segment is stateful: capacity and seal timing depend on the shard's current progress, known best by the StreamingNode that exclusively owns that pchannel. The Proxy is stateless and replicable, so letting it decide segments would cause concurrent clashes
  2. Because segment IDs are generated by the client SDK
  3. It lets the system skip timestamps
  4. Because the Proxy doesn't know how many shards a collection has
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: A. 'Stateless to the stateless, stateful to the stateful': centralizing segment assignment in the single StreamingNode that owns the channel keeps assignment consistent and the horizontally-scalable Proxy lightweight
3. When an insert returns success, what does it precisely mean?
  1. The data is persisted on all replicas
  2. The data is already in binlog files in object storage
  3. The batch was reliably appended to the WAL (single source of truth); even if the StreamingNode crashes at once, it replays from the WAL on restart with no loss. It does NOT mean flushed into segment files — that is done asynchronously in the background
  4. An index is already built and the data is searchable
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: C. The WAL is the single source of truth: once logged, data is durable and replayable, so 'write success = logged' not 'flushed to a segment'. Persisting and indexing proceed asynchronously off the WAL
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • This lesson notes an insert's 'atomic batch' only guarantees 'messages of the same vchannel are written together as one transaction'; strict atomicity is not guaranteed across vchannels. Think: why does Milvus pick this pragmatic trade-off over a two-phase commit? In what scenario might a user observe 'partial visibility', and why is the impact tiny for the vast majority of inserts?

Streaming & WAL

Last lesson the Proxy validated, sharded, and packed a batch of inserts, finally appending them to the WAL. This lesson we step into the WAL itself — the backbone of Milvus's write path. This is a "light tour": we make the WAL's core concepts (single source of truth, message and TimeTick, the three channels, StreamingClient, StreamingNode/StreamingCoord) intuitive; a line-by-line dissection of the WAL internals waits for Part 7. By the end you'll understand why "in Milvus, the log is not an aid but the data itself."

📒 Analogy
Think of the WAL as a bank's running ledger: every deposit or withdrawal is recorded in the ledger first (append), before any balance changes. The ledger is append-only, never edited, and each entry has a strictly increasing sequence number (TimeTick). Even if the ledger system crashes for a moment, on restart you just read the ledger from the top to recompute balances, reports, and statements — because the ledger is the truth and everything else is derived from it. Milvus's WAL is exactly that ledger: all writes go to the WAL first, and segments, indexes, and delete markers are all "read out" of it. This lesson, let's get to know a few core rules of this "ledger."

The WAL is the single source of truth

Nail this down first: the WAL (Write-Ahead Log) is the single source of truth for all mutations in Milvus. Any insert, delete, or DDL must be written to the WAL first to count. Segment files, indexes, in-memory data structures — they are all derivatives of the WAL, different shapes obtained by replaying the log. This is Lesson 7's "log as data" realized at the system level: the log is not a crash-recovery side-product; it is the authoritative copy of the data.

To feel how counter-intuitive this positioning is, contrast it with a traditional database. There, the table is the protagonist and the write-ahead log is merely insurance for "recover if we ever crash" — nobody reads it directly in normal operation. Milvus flips this entirely: the log is promoted to lead actor, and the table (segment) becomes a materialized view of the log. Why dare to design this way? Because a vector DB's workload has a distinctive shape: writes are a continuous incoming stream, reads are retrieval over a snapshot, and the two cadences differ wildly. Collapsing writes into a single append-only log makes the write path as short as possible (just append), then lets the various "read shapes" grow out of the log in the background, asynchronously and each taking what it needs. It is a "record first, reconcile later" architectural philosophy — the simpler the write, the easier the whole system is to make correct and fast.

This positioning yields an extremely clean mental model: as long as the WAL exists, the data exists. A crashed node, a corrupted segment file, a lost index — none is fatal; replay the WAL to rebuild. Conversely, only when data has successfully entered the WAL is the write "successful" (exactly last lesson's conclusion). So the whole system's reliability boundary collapses to one clear point: guard the WAL. That is also why WAL backends use highly-reliable, durable messaging systems like Kafka / Pulsar / Woodpecker / RMQ — handing the most critical job, "don't lose the log," to the component best at it.

A note on these backends' trade-offs: Kafka and Pulsar are mature industry distributed messaging systems, suited to large production clusters; RMQ (RocksMQ) is Milvus's embedded lightweight implementation, running with zero external dependencies in single-node/Standalone form (recall Lesson 8); Woodpecker is Milvus's own object-storage-oriented WAL solution. Whichever you pick, they all unify upward into the abstraction "one PChannel = one topic" — exactly the point of the StreamingClient wrapping above: making "which system the log lives in" a replaceable deployment choice rather than an assumption hard-wired into the code. You learn with RMQ on a small machine and switch to Pulsar to scale a production cluster, and the write-path code does not change at all.

Message and TimeTick: the log's two building blocks

The basic unit flowing in the WAL is a Message: an InsertMessage, a DeleteMessage, a DDL message... each with a header (type, vchannel, collection ID, etc.) and a body (the actual data). What the Proxy packed last lesson are exactly these messages. A message is self-describing: the consumer, looking only at the message itself, knows "what operation this is, which shard it acts on, and how to apply it."

What strings these messages into one ordered line is TimeTick. TimeTick is a monotonically increasing log sequence number on each pchannel — think of it as the log's "clock." It is not physical wall-clock time but a logical ruler: whoever appends first gets the smaller TimeTick. This ruler gives scattered writes a definite order, and lets the read side judge precisely "up to which point have I consumed." The MaxTimeTick the Proxy filled back to the client last lesson is one tick on this ruler. The message is content; TimeTick is order — put the two blocks together and you get an "ordered, replayable" log.

One common confusion to clear up: if TimeTick increases monotonically, must the system precisely number every message with no gaps? It is not that rigid. TimeTick is more like a continually advancing "watermark" on the log: the StreamingNode periodically inserts special TimeTick messages into the WAL, declaring "as of now, all writes on this channel below this timestamp are ready." Seeing this watermark, a consumer knows "I can safely make data up to here visible." This mechanism solves a thorny problem: writes are intermittent, so how does a consumer tell "there really is no new data" from "data is in flight and hasn't arrived"? The answer is the watermark — TimeTick writes the very fact that "time is passing" into the log itself, so even a channel with no business writes for a while keeps its "clock" advancing, and consumer visibility never stalls on a quiet channel. Grasp this and you grasp the heartbeat underlying Milvus's consistent reads.

WAL on one pchannel: messages laid out by strictly increasing TimeTick
t₁insert
t₂insert
t₃delete
t₄insert
t₅ddl
t₆…

Three channels: PChannel, VChannel, CChannel

"Channel" is the WAL's partitioning concept, of three kinds, each with its own role, and easily confused — let's settle them once:

  • PChannel (physical channel): the physical log partition that actually carries the WAL; one PChannel maps to one topic on the WAL backend. Fixed in number, pre-opened by the cluster, the log's "physical track." TimeTick is defined on each PChannel.
  • VChannel (virtual channel): a logical notion, "a shard of a collection." Born and dies with the collection. Many VChannels multiplex one PChannel — what the Proxy hashed to last lesson is a VChannel.
  • CChannel (control channel): a cluster-wide, singleton control channel for control information needing global ordering across PChannels. It guarantees certain control operations have a consistent order cluster-wide.

The simplest way to remember their relation: VChannels are logical shards (many), PChannels are physical logs (few), many logical shards multiplex one physical log; the CChannel is the single "control broadcast line" overhead. Why not give each VChannel its own physical topic? Because collections can number in the thousands and shards even more; topics are a scarce resource and a one-to-one mapping would explode. Multiplexing PChannels carries elastic logical shards on fixed physical resources — the classic "decouple logical from physical" move in distributed systems.

A subtle ordering granularity point hides here, worth spelling out. TimeTick's monotonic increase is defined on the PChannel physical log, meaning: all messages on one PChannel have a global order, but there is no natural total order across PChannels. That is exactly enough — because messages of the same VChannel (same shard) always land on the same PChannel, "order within a shard" is naturally guaranteed, while shards are inherently independent and need no cross-comparison. So how about the few operations that "need cross-PChannel global consistency" (typically DDL, e.g. creating/dropping a collection must reach all shards at once)? That is the reason CChannel and Broadcast exist: they specialize in stringing the small set of "must-be-globally-consistent" control information into a total order over one singleton channel. Carrying "strong order within a shard" and "total order across shards" separately is Milvus's key trade-off for both high throughput and correctness — the vast majority of data takes the cheap in-shard order, the rare control takes the expensive global order.

channellayerscopepurpose
PChannelphysicalone MQ topicactually carries the WAL; TimeTick defined here
VChannellogicalone shard of a collectionwrite-routing unit; multiplexes a PChannel
CChannelcontrolwhole cluster (singleton)global control ordering across PChannels

StreamingClient: Append / Broadcast / Read

That streaming.WAL() from last lesson returns the StreamingClient (the WALAccesser interface in code). It is an in-process WAL access layer that wraps all the complexity of facing the WAL backend — addressing, connecting, retrying — and exposes only three clean capabilities upward:

  • Append (write): write messages to the WAL. Normal writes go through RawAppend / AppendMessages, appending messages (transactionally) per VChannel to the corresponding PChannel. The spine of the write path.
  • Broadcast: atomically write one message across multiple VChannels, for DDL/DCL that "must take effect on several shards at once." It works with the CChannel for globally consistent ordering.
  • Read (scan): read messages from the WAL by position, for consumers (the StreamingNode's flusher, the QueryNode's delegator, etc.) to replay the log and catch up to the latest state.

Why does this wrapping layer matter? Because it completely hides "is the WAL backend Kafka, Pulsar, or something else" from the layers above. The Proxy and the consumers inside StreamingNode all talk only to the WALAccesser interface, never needing to know which messaging system is underneath, what its client API looks like, or how to reconnect on a drop. To swap backends you just replace the implementation under this layer, and the upper layers change not a single line — exactly the "isolate replaceable backends behind an interface" recurring in Lessons 8 and 14. One more easily-missed point: the StreamingClient is in-process, meaning a call to Append is not a raw network RPC but goes through this local client first, which handles batching, addressing, and retries. That keeps the write-side code both simple and robust: the business only expresses "I want to write this batch of messages," while "which machine to write to, how many times to retry on failure" is quietly absorbed by this layer.

Proxy write
RawAppend / AppendMessages
by VChannel
WAL (PChannel)
transactionally appended to the log (single source of truth)
Read consume
StreamingNode flusher
tail the log → growing segment → flush
internal/distributed/streaming/streaming.gothe WALAccesser interface (excerpt)
type WALAccesser interface {
    // write: append one/many messages to the WAL
    RawAppend(ctx context.Context, msg message.MutableMessage, opts ...AppendOption) (*types.AppendResult, error)
    AppendMessages(ctx context.Context, msgs ...message.MutableMessage) AppendResponses

    // broadcast: atomic cross-vchannel write, paired with the control channel
    Broadcast() Broadcast
    // control channel name
    ControlChannel() string

    // read: scan the WAL by position for consumers to replay
    Read(ctx context.Context, opts ReadOption) Scanner
}

StreamingNode and StreamingCoord

The WAL system is held up by two roles. One is the StreamingNode: each StreamingNode exclusively manages a subset of PChannels, responsible for writes and consumption on them. It carries several key duties: TimeTick & transactions (ordering messages, ensuring a same-VChannel batch is transactional), locking, shard management (segment assignment) — remember "segment ID left empty" last lesson? It is the StreamingNode that assigns the segment ID as it writes the WAL — and RecoveryStorage: maintaining checkpoints, metadata, and recovering by replaying the WAL after a crash.

The other role is the StreamingCoord, which is a singleton and — an easily-missed key point — runs inside the RootCoord process. It manages two things: channel management (deciding which StreamingNode each PChannel is assigned to, and rebalancing as nodes come and go) and the Broadcaster (implementing atomic cross-PChannel DDL/DCL). Think of the StreamingCoord as "the brain of the WAL subsystem": it does not haul logs itself, but decides who owns which physical channel and how those must-be-global-atomic control operations are coordinated.

Why is the StreamingCoord placed inside the RootCoord process rather than as a standalone component? Because its job is naturally close to RootCoord's: RootCoord already owns DDL and the global meta (Lesson 11), and channel assignment plus cross-channel broadcast are precisely "cluster-level metadata decisions." Co-locating them avoids one more cross-process hop and one more thing to deploy and fail over. This is the same "fold singleton coordination duties together" instinct as the MixCoord idea from Lesson 9 — not everything that is conceptually a "coordinator" needs its own process. The division to remember is clean: StreamingCoord decides (assignment, coordination), StreamingNode executes (carries the log, assigns segments, recovers) — yet another instance of "control plane decides, data plane does work."

write sideProxyvalidate/shard/pack, calls StreamingClient.Append
clientStreamingClient (WALAccesser)in-process Append/Broadcast/Read + discovery/reconnect
nodeStreamingNodeowns a subset of PChannels: TimeTick/txn, segment assignment, RecoveryStorage
backendWAL backend (Kafka/Pulsar/Woodpecker/RMQ)one PChannel = one topic, durable log

Log as data: other shapes catch up by replaying

Put it together and you get the most central sentence of Milvus's write architecture: the WAL is the truth; everything else is its "projection". A segment is the columnar file produced by accumulating insert messages from the WAL and persisting them; an index is an acceleration structure built atop a segment; the searchable data in a QueryNode's memory is what it got by consuming the WAL and replaying messages. Different shapes, but all doing one thing: following the WAL forward, "translating" the log into the form each needs.

WAL (the truth)
ordered append-only insert/delete log
replay
growing/sealed segments
accumulate messages, flush to columnar binlog
indexes
acceleration structures built atop segments
QueryNode memory
searchable data from consuming the WAL

This "log as data" design pays off in enormous decoupling and elasticity. Add a QueryNode? Let it start consuming from some WAL position and gradually catch up — no downtime, no data hauling. A consumer fell behind? It accelerates its own replay along the WAL to catch up, affecting no one else. The write side just reliably appends messages in order; the read, persist, and index sides each consume independently — not directly coupled to each other, only collaborating indirectly through this one ordered log. That is why Milvus can optimize "write fast" and "read steady" separately: a single log cleanly separates them. Next lesson we follow one of those consumers — the one that accumulates WAL inserts into a segment and flushes it into a binlog — to see the first concrete step of "log becoming data."

A parting line worth savoring: in Milvus, "writing" and "persisting" are two deliberately separated things. Writing answers only to the WAL — fast and light; persisting is what background consumers do slowly along the WAL — steady and frugal. It is this WAL that cleanly cuts apart "what must be done at once" from "what can be done at leisure" — understand it and you hold the master outline of the whole write path.

One WAL, two tempos: a write only reliably appends to the WAL — fast, light, must finish now; while flushing to segments, building indexes, loading into memory are done by background consumers replaying the WAL — steady, cheap, at leisure. Segments / indexes / memory are all projections of the WAL — that is "log as data".
🌍 Big picture
In one line: the WAL is the single source of truth; it is made of messages (content) + TimeTick (a per-PChannel monotonically increasing order); channels split into PChannel (physical topic) / VChannel (logical shard, multiplexing a PChannel) / CChannel (cluster-wide control); StreamingClient offers Append/Broadcast/Read; the StreamingNode exclusively manages PChannels (TimeTick, segment assignment, RecoveryStorage) and the StreamingCoord is a singleton running inside RootCoord doing channel assignment and broadcast. Segments, indexes, and in-memory data are all shapes derived by replaying the WAL — log as data.
📌 Key points
  • Single source of truth: every mutation enters the WAL first; segments/indexes/in-memory data are WAL derivatives, rebuilt by replay after a crash.
  • Message + TimeTick: a message is self-describing content; TimeTick is a per-PChannel monotonically increasing logical sequence number ordering messages.
  • Three channels: PChannel (physical topic) ← VChannel (logical shard, multiplexes a PChannel) + CChannel (cluster-wide control ordering).
  • StreamingClient: the WALAccesser interface, in-process Append / Broadcast / Read, wrapping addressing and reconnection.
  • Two roles: the StreamingNode exclusively manages PChannels (TimeTick/txn, segment assignment, RecoveryStorage); the StreamingCoord is a singleton running inside RootCoord doing channel assignment and cross-channel broadcast.
  • Log as data: consumers replay along the WAL to catch up; write/read/persist are decoupled — the root of Milvus's elasticity.

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. What does 'the WAL is the single source of truth' most precisely mean?
  1. Every mutation must enter the WAL first to count; segments, indexes, and in-memory searchable data are derivatives obtained by replaying the WAL, and can be rebuilt by replay after a crash
  2. The WAL is equivalent to the metadata in etcd
  3. The WAL stores a cache of query results
  4. The WAL is just a crash-recovery backup, idle during normal reads/writes
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: A. Milvus inverts the traditional 'table is primary, log is insurance' relation: the log is promoted to the authoritative copy, and segments/indexes are its materialized views. Guard the WAL and you guard the data
2. Which statement correctly describes the relation among PChannel, VChannel, and CChannel?
  1. VChannel is a logical shard (many), PChannel is a physical log (few, one = one topic), many VChannels multiplex one PChannel; CChannel is a cluster-wide singleton control channel for global ordering across PChannels
  2. PChannel is logical and VChannel is physical
  3. CChannel carries ordinary insert data
  4. Each VChannel owns its own physical topic, one-to-one
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: A. Topics are scarce and collections/shards can number in the thousands, so one-to-one would explode. Multiplexing carries elastic logical shards on fixed physical resources; TimeTick is defined per PChannel, so in-shard order is natural and cross-shard total order is delegated to the CChannel
3. Which describes the StreamingNode vs StreamingCoord division correctly?
  1. The StreamingCoord is a standalone process and the StreamingNode is read-only
  2. The StreamingNode assigns channels and the StreamingCoord hauls logs
  3. Both run inside the Proxy
  4. The StreamingNode exclusively manages a subset of PChannels (execution duties: TimeTick/txn, segment assignment, RecoveryStorage); the StreamingCoord is a singleton running inside the RootCoord process, doing decisions like channel assignment and cross-channel broadcast
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: D. Once more 'control plane decides, data plane does work': the Coord decides who owns which channel and coordinates global-atomic ops; the Node actually carries and consumes the log, assigns segments, recovers. Co-locating the Coord with RootCoord saves a cross-process hop
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • The biggest payoff of 'log as data' is decoupling and elasticity: adding a consumer (e.g. a QueryNode) just needs it to start consuming from some WAL position and gradually catch up, with no downtime or data hauling. Using TimeTick's 'watermark' mechanism, explain how a lagging consumer decides 'I have caught up', and why consumer visibility does not stall even when a channel has no business writes for a while.

DataNode & flush

Last lesson we saw the WAL is the single source of truth, and planted a hook: segments, indexes — the "searchable data" — are all shapes derived by consuming the WAL and replaying the log. This lesson covers the first step of "log becoming data" — how insert messages in the WAL first accumulate in memory into a growing segment, then at the right moment flush (seal and persist) into binlog files in object storage. Along the way we settle a commonly-confused question: who actually does this — the StreamingNode or the DataNode?
