The one case where the reverse can win is a very weak filter (it keeps almost everything): then the filter barely shrinks the candidate set, and paying for it up front buys little. Knowing which regime you're in is part of understanding why Milvus chose the default it did.

✅ Filter-then-search (Milvus)

compute a bitset via expr/exec first; the vector search only sees qualifying rows. Result count is guaranteed, no wasted compute. The default for hybrid queries.

⚠️ Search-then-filter

take topK then apply the condition — may fall short of K, forcing repeated retries with a larger topK; slow and unstable. Only occasionally worth it when the filter is very weak.

Scalar index: making the "filter" step itself faster

Everything above describes the path without a scalar index — the exec engine scans the whole column chunk by chunk to compute the bitset. But remember the scalar indexes from Lesson 24? When the filtered field has an inverted / bitmap / sort index built on it, the engine needn't scan column by column at all: it can query the index directly to get "the set of row IDs satisfying the condition", then turn that into a bitset. So an equality filter like category == "shoes", or a range filter like price within [a, b], can be looked up rather than scanned. Use the index when there is one, fall back to a full exec scan when there isn't — this is exactly where expr/exec cooperates with scalar indexes: the expression layer decides "what condition to compute", the execution layer decides "look it up via an index or scan-and-compute", and both converge into the same bitset fed to the vector search.

There is also a subtler regime worth naming. When the filter is very strong (few rows qualify) and you're searching a graph index like HNSW, "filter first" can make the graph too sparse to traverse — most neighbors are filtered out, so the walk can't hop to the next node. The engine then falls back to strategies (widen the search range, or brute-force a tiny candidate set) to protect recall. Either way the main line — the filter constrains the search — never changes: the bitset is always the "who may be returned" pass; only "how to find all the nearest neighbors under that constraint" differs. The practical takeaway: the strength of your filter genuinely affects search strategy and recall, so building the right scalar index and writing good filter conditions matters just as much as picking the right vector index.

Division of labor: expr / exec / bitset

RoleWhatResponsible forWhere
exprlogical expression tree (ITypeExpr)describes "what the filter computes"core/src/expr
execvectorized engine + physical ops"how to compute it fast" over chunkscore/src/exec
bitsetone-bit-per-row qualifying maskthe "pass" linking filter to searchthe evaluation output

This three-way split — expr / exec / bitset — is worth internalizing because it recurs throughout database engineering. expr is the "what": a declarative, type-checked description of the predicate, easy to validate and rewrite but deliberately ignorant of how it runs. exec is the "how": the machinery that turns that description into fast, vectorized work over real data. And the bitset is the clean interface between filtering and searching — a tiny, uniform artifact ("these rows qualify") that lets two very different subsystems cooperate without knowing each other's internals. Separating "what" from "how", and connecting stages through a minimal shared artifact, is exactly how complex engines stay maintainable and fast at once. You'll see the very same expr/exec design again, in far more depth, when we open the C++ core in Part 8 — proof that this isn't a query-path quirk but a core architectural choice.

📌 Key points
  • Expression tree (expr): the filter string compiles to an ITypeExpr logical tree — leaves are cols/consts, internal nodes are comparison/logic/arithmetic.
  • Vectorized execution (exec): Task/Driver/QueryContext turn the logical tree into physical operators, evaluated batched + SIMD-friendly over columnar chunks, producing a bitset.
  • Filter-then-search: the bitset prunes the search; filtering first guarantees a full topK and is faster/steadier than "search-then-filter".
  • Layering: expr says "what to compute", exec says "how to compute it fast" — the classic database execution-engine split.
  • Bridging the steps: filtering (this lesson) shrinks the candidates, search (Lesson 27) finds nearest neighbors, reduce (Lesson 29) merges the result — the three together are one complete query; and with a scalar index, filtering can "look up" rather than "scan", faster still.

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. In a Milvus hybrid query, after the execution engine (exec) evaluates the scalar filter, what does it hand to the vector search?
  1. A freshly built index tree
  2. A re-sorted copy of the raw vectors
  3. A SQL result set
  4. A bitset: one bit per row marking pass/fail, used as the pruning mask for vector search
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: D. exec evaluates the filter in a vectorized way over columnar chunks, producing a bitset (qualifying-row mask). The vector search only looks at the 1-bits — that is bitset pruning.
2. Why does Milvus default to 'filter-then-search' rather than 'search-then-filter'?
  1. Because filtering is slower than vector search, so do it first
  2. Because searching first corrupts the index
  3. They are equivalent; it's just convention
  4. Filtering first reliably yields a full topK within the qualifying set; searching first then filtering may drop most of topK, leaving fewer than K
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: D. Take topK first then filter and you may end up with fewer than K matches, forcing repeated retries with a larger topK — slow and unstable. Filtering first constrains the search to the qualifying set, guaranteeing the count and saving the most expensive vector compute.
3. Why compile the filter string into an expression tree and evaluate it 'by chunk' with a vectorized engine, instead of interpreting it row by row?
  1. The tree is just cosmetic, no performance impact
  2. To save disk space
  3. To sort results alphabetically
  4. Parse once (reusable + optimizable); batched per-column/chunk evaluation uses SIMD, is cache-friendly with almost no branch mispredictions — far faster than row-by-row
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: D. A tree lets the same filter be reused across segments/chunks and enables constant-folding/short-circuit/range-merge optimizations; columnar chunks + vectorization turn scattered row-wise work into batched column-wise work that exploits SIMD and cache — the basis of a fast modern analytical engine.
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • Imagine a very selective query (only 0.1% of rows qualify) that also needs similarity search on a graph index like HNSW. 'Filter first' can make the navigable graph too sparse to hop through, hurting recall. Consider: what strategies can the engine use to balance the filter constraint with recall? (Hints: widen the search scope, brute-force a tiny candidate set…) What does this reveal about how filter selectivity feeds back into search strategy?

Reduce & result assembly

In the last few lessons, a search is scattered far and wide: the Proxy fans it out to multiple shards, each shard's delegator sends it to the QueryNodes holding different segments, and each segment searches its own little slice in segcore. So the question is: how do all these "local topKs" merge into one final answer that is correctly ordered, deduplicated and complete? That is what Reduce does. It is not one step but three levels of bottom-up merging — within a segment, within a node, across shards — and each level only passes its own topK upward, so the data shrinks as it climbs. Understand Reduce and you understand "distributed topK", which looks simple but has real subtlety.

🔌 Analogy
Like a tiered selection: first each class picks its top 3 (segment topK), then the classes' top-3s compete at the grade level for the grade's top 3 (node merge), and finally the grades' top-3s meet in the school final for the school's top 3 (cross-shard merge). No need to make all thousands compete at once — each level only sends up its own top few: fair and cheap. Reduce is that tiered selection, and the final "school top 3" is your topK.
🌍 Big picture
In one line: segment topK (segcore) → node merge (delegator) → cross-shard merge (Proxy), merged level by level. Each level only sends up a local topK, so the data crossing the network stays small; merging also dedups by primary key, sorts by distance, then paginates by offset/limit. This scatter-gather shape is the universal skeleton of almost every distributed retrieval/query engine.

Three levels: from segment to node to Proxy

Walk the scatter backwards and you get the three merge levels. Level one, in the segment: after segcore computes all candidate distances in a segment it does NOT return them all — too many — but does a local reduce (internal/core/src/segcore/reduce/Reduce.cpp), keeping only that segment's topK. Level two, in the node: a QueryNode (delegator/shard leader) holds many segments of this shard (sealed + growing); it merges their topKs into "this shard's topK". Level three, at the Proxy: the Proxy receives every shard's topK and does the final cross-shard merge (see docs/developer_guides/proxy-reduce.md), producing the global topK, and returns it — with the requested scalar fields — to the client.

segment topK
segcore Reduce.cpp (keep K per segment)
merge
shard topK
delegator merges this node's segments (sealed+growing)
merge
global topK
Proxy's final cross-shard merge → return to client
1

Segment reduce (segcore)

each segment computes candidate distances and locally selects its topK, sending up only that slice. Reduce.cpp

2

Node merge (delegator)

a shard's many segments (sealed+growing) topKs are merged into "this shard's topK".

3

Cross-shard merge (Proxy)

shards' topKs gather at the Proxy for a final merge into the global topK.

4

Fill & return

fetch output_fields by PK, assemble the result, return to the client.

internal/core/src/segcore/reduce/Reduce.cppsegment-level reduce: converge candidates into the segment's topK (illustrative)
// in a segment: for each query vector (nq of them), take the best topK by distance
// key actions: sort by distance + dedup by primary key + truncate to topK
ReduceHelper::Reduce();          // converge each segment's search results
ReduceHelper::Marshal();         // pack into a cross-process transmittable result

Why levels? Can't we merge all at once?

Picture the naive approach: every segment and node ships all candidates to the Proxy, which sorts once and takes the topK. That might be fine at small scale, but at billions it collapses — the network hauls enormous data and the Proxy sorts an astronomical number of records: slow and memory-hungry. The essence of hierarchical merging is "converge early": since the final answer is only K items, each level only needs to send up its own top K (anything beyond can't make the global top K). So each segment sends K, each shard sends K, and the data crossing the network is pinned to a tiny size. It is a humble but powerful observation: topK merging is divide-and-conquer — the union of local topKs is guaranteed to contain the global topK.

Let that guarantee sink in, because it's the mathematical heart of the whole design. Why is it safe for a segment to send up only its own top K and discard the rest? Because the global #1 result must, by definition, also be #1 in whatever segment it lives in — it can't be the best overall while being only, say, the 50th-best in its own segment. So no globally-relevant candidate is ever hiding below a segment's local top K; throwing away each segment's "also-rans" is provably lossless. This is what licenses the aggressive early convergence: each level can shrink its output to K with zero risk of dropping a true winner. Contrast that with computing an average or a median across shards — those you genuinely cannot compute from per-shard top-Ks, because the answer depends on data each shard discarded. topK is special: it's decomposable, and Milvus's three-level reduce is simply that decomposability cashed in for scale.

✅ Hierarchical merge (Milvus)

each level sends only a local topK; data shrinks upward; the Proxy merges just "K × number-of-shards". Scales to billions, stable latency.

⚠️ One-shot full gather

all candidates to one place for a single sort: network and memory explode with data volume, can't withstand scale. Only holds at toy size.

What does a merge actually do

At every level a merge is the same three things, just over a different scope. (1) Sort by distance/score: merge several sorted results into one still-sorted result (k-way merge). (2) Dedup by primary key: the same entity can appear in multiple segments — e.g. it was just written into a growing segment and also has an old version in a sealed one, or a delete and insert coexist; the merge must identify by primary key and keep only the "should-be-seen" row, so no entity appears twice. (3) Paginate by offset/limit: after the global sort and dedup, skip the first offset and take limit, supporting pagination. Note: pagination happens after the merge — you need a correct global order first, which is also why deep pagination (large offset) is inherently more expensive in distributed retrieval.

ActionWhatWhy
Sortk-way sorted merge, by distance/scorefuse several local topKs into one global order
Dedupidentify by PK, keep the "should-see" versionsame entity may appear across segments; avoid dup/stale
Paginateskip offset, take limitsupport paging; deep paging is inherently pricier

A concrete walk-through

Let topK=3, with 2 shards, each holding 3 segments. Bottom level: the 6 segments each search their own top 3, giving 6 "segment topKs". Second level: shard A's 3 segments (9 candidates total) merge+dedup down to shard A's top 3, and shard B likewise to its top 3. Third level: the Proxy takes A's and B's top 3 (6 total) and finally merges, dedups, sorts, and takes the global top 3. Throughout, the most that ever crosses the network is "3 per segment", "3 per shard", "6 at the end" — always on the order of K, independent of the total underlying data. That is exactly why distributed topK can be both correct and scalable. Back to the full chain: Lesson 28's filter narrowed the candidates, Lesson 27 found the topK inside a segment, and this lesson merges the scattered topKs into the final answer; the next lesson (30) supplies the last piece — exactly which moment's data these segments and nodes searched, i.e. consistency and timestamps.

The three-level reduce, walked once: 6 segments each yield top3 → shard A merges seg1-3 to [0.05,0.12,0.31], shard B to [0.08,0.20,0.40] → the Proxy merges, dedups, and sorts those 6 into the global top3 [0.05, 0.08, 0.12]. Every level passes only K rows up — the network always carries O(K), no matter how many billions sit underneath.

Step back and notice the shape of this pattern, because you'll meet it far beyond Milvus. "Compute locally, send up a small summary, merge upward" is the same skeleton as MapReduce, as a SQL GROUP BY executed across shards, as aggregating metrics from a fleet of servers. The three-level reduce here — segment → node → Proxy — is just that skeleton fitted to topK: each tier does as much work as it can where the data already is (avoiding moving raw data), and passes upward only a result that is tiny and already half-merged. The payoff is twofold: the network never carries more than O(K) per source, and the final merger (the Proxy) never faces more than "K × number of children", no matter how many billions of vectors sit underneath. Whenever you design a distributed query of your own, ask the same question Milvus answered here: can I push work down to the data and only float a small summary up? If yes, you've found your reduce.

📌 Key points
  • Three merge levels: segment (segcore Reduce.cpp) → node (delegator) → cross-shard (Proxy, see proxy-reduce.md).
  • Divide-and-conquer: each level sends only a local topK; the union of local topKs contains the global topK; only K-scale data crosses the network, so it scales to billions.
  • A merge does three things: sort by distance (k-way), dedup by PK (an entity may appear across segments), paginate by offset/limit (deep paging is pricier).
  • Scatter-gather: fan out to shards/segments, search local topKs, gather and merge upward — the universal skeleton of distributed retrieval/query engines.
  • Approximate in the segment, exact in the merge: recall is set by the in-segment ANN precision (nprobe/ef); the merge only faithfully combines what it is given. To get more accurate results, tune the in-segment search, not the merge. Deep paging and an oversized K are the real reasons merging gets expensive.

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. At which levels does Milvus merge (Reduce) the results of one distributed search?
  1. Only inside each segment, no further merging
  2. In the client SDK
  3. Only once, entirely at the Proxy
  4. Three levels: in-segment (segcore Reduce.cpp) → in-node (delegator) → cross-shard (Proxy), bottom-up
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: D. Merging follows the data's physical layout in three levels: each segment reduces to its topK, the node (delegator) merges a shard's segments, and the Proxy finally merges across shards into the global topK. Each level only sends up a local topK.
2. Why does hierarchical merging scale to billions while 'send all candidates to one place and sort' does not?
  1. Because levels use the GPU
  2. Because levels compress the data
  3. Because levels skip sorting
  4. Because the union of local topKs contains the global topK, so each level sends only K items — only K-scale data ever crosses the network
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: D. Only K items are needed; anything outside a level's top K can't be in the global top K, so each level sends only its top K. The Proxy then faces just S×K (independent of total data), whereas one-shot gather must move/sort candidates that explode with data volume.
3. Why must a merge 'dedup by primary key'?
  1. Because the PK sorts results alphabetically
  2. Because not deduping corrupts the index
  3. Because dedup reduces network traffic
  4. Because the same entity may appear across segments (growing new value / sealed old value / delete tombstone); without dedup you'd return duplicates or stale versions
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: D. In a 'log-as-data, immutable-segment' model, one row may exist in several segments (old value, new upsert, tombstone). The merge must identify by PK and, with timestamp/delete bitmap, keep only the 'should-be-seen' version, or results would be duplicated or stale.
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • This lesson says 'the merge is exact; approximation happens in the in-segment ANN search'. Reason from that: if a search's recall is poor, which knobs should you tune (hint: nprobe/ef, in-segment search precision) rather than the merge? Then consider: why does deep pagination (large offset) make the merge expensive, and what is a better approach than a large offset for an 'infinite scroll' scenario?

Consistency & timestamps

From Lesson 25 to 29 we walked a search end to end: the Proxy dispatches, the delegator fans out, segcore searches a segment, exec filters, reduce merges. But one question we kept quietly deferring until now: which moment's data did those segments and nodes actually search? Can a search see data you just inserted? That is what consistency and timestamps answer. The core is just three words: TSO (a global clock), the guarantee timestamp, and MVCC (visibility by timestamp). Together they let Milvus both "query while you write" and let you choose "how fresh" you need.

🔌 Analogy
Like reading a versioned online document. Every edit is stamped with a timestamp; opening the doc really means "show me the version as of moment T". For "absolutely latest", the system must wait for all in-flight edits to land (a bit slower but complete); for "just give it to me fast", it hands you a slightly earlier snapshot (maybe missing the last few edits, but instant). Milvus's consistency levels let you pick that "how fresh" dial — and the timestamp is the unified ruler that makes it all computable.
🌍 Big picture
In one line: RootCoord's TSO issues a global monotonic timestamp; every write and read is stamped; a read carries a "guarantee timestamp Tg" (set by its consistency level); a QueryNode waits until the data it has consumed (tsafe) catches up to Tg, then by MVCC returns only the version with "ts ≤ Tg and not deleted". The freshness-vs-latency trade-off compresses into one knob: "how new is Tg, and do we wait."

TSO: one global, ever-increasing clock

One of the hardest things in a distributed system is "who came first". Multiple Proxies take writes at once, each node has its own local clock — how do you order two operations? Milvus's answer is the TSO (Timestamp Oracle): RootCoord (today, that part inside MixCoord) maintains one global, monotonically increasing timestamp source. Whichever Proxy asks, the ts it gets never repeats and never goes backward, and strictly reflects "who asked first gets the smaller ts". So the whole cluster shares a single time ruler: every write message is stamped as it enters the WAL, and every read gets a ts too. With this global clock, "ordering" turns from fuzzy physical time into comparable integers. Consistency and MVCC are all built on it.

TSORootCoord
a global, monotonic timestamp source — the cluster's single clock
writeinsert/delete ts
each write is stamped as it enters the WAL (order = precedence)
readguarantee ts (Tg)
each read gets a Tg: see all writes up to Tg
visibilityMVCC
a row is visible iff its ts ≤ Tg and no tombstone with ts ≤ Tg deletes it

The guarantee timestamp and five consistency levels

The Tg (guarantee timestamp) a read carries means "please let me see at least all writes up to Tg". How large Tg is depends on the consistency level you pick — exactly what parseGuaranteeTsFromConsistency in internal/proxy/util.go does. Milvus offers five levels (commonpb.ConsistencyLevel_*), essentially different settings of "how new is Tg":

LevelTg valueMeaning / trade-off
Stronglatest tMaxsee all committed writes so far; freshest, may wait
BoundedtMax − gracefulTimetolerate a little staleness (e.g. seconds) for lower latency; a common default
Sessionthis client's last-write tsread-your-own-writes
Eventually1 (tiny)no waiting, returns immediately, may be stale; fastest
Customizeda user-supplied tsprecisely control "as of which moment" — time-travel queries
internal/proxy/util.goderive guarantee ts Tg from the consistency level (illustrative, simplified)
// parseGuaranteeTsFromConsistency: consistency level → Tg
switch level {
case Strong:      Tg = tMax                 // latest
case Bounded:     Tg = tMax - gracefulTime  // tolerate some staleness
case Eventually:  Tg = 1                    // no waiting
// Session: this session's last-write ts; Customized: user-given ts
}

MVCC: deciding "who should be seen" by timestamp

With Tg, "visibility" has a precise definition. Milvus uses MVCC (multi-version concurrency control): every row carries its write ts, and a row is visible to this read iff its insert ts ≤ Tg AND no delete tombstone with ts ≤ Tg voids it (recall Lesson 20 — a delete stamps a tombstone, an upsert is delete+insert). In other words, a read is not "the current final state" but "the snapshot as of Tg". Two benefits: first, reads and writes don't block each other — writes keep appending at higher ts, reads only care about their own Tg; second, results are inherently self-consistent — within one query, every segment and node filters by the same Tg, seeing the same time-slice, never "new on one side, old on the other". MVCC turns the hard problem of concurrency into "everyone reads their own time snapshot".

Multiple versions of one PK k: reading at Tg=100, which version should be seen? (green ✓ = visible)
v1insert k @ts=80→overwritten later
v2delete k @ts=90→tombstone ts=90 ≤ 100
v3insert k @ts=95→ts=95 ≤ 100, no later tombstone ✓
v4insert k @ts=120→ts=120 > 100, invisible

The wait mechanism: answer only once tsafe ≥ Tg

Here is the crucial piece: how do we guarantee that the writes up to Tg have actually reached the QueryNode? After all, writes flow asynchronously through the WAL; a node may not have consumed the latest yet. Milvus's trick is to give each QueryNode a tsafe (served watermark) — how far it has consumed and applied the WAL, expressed as a TimeTick. TimeTick is the WAL's monotonically advancing clock (Lesson 16); each chunk of log a node consumes pushes tsafe forward. When a read arrives with Tg, the node compares: if tsafe ≥ Tg, it has everything up to Tg, so search now; if tsafe < Tg, it suspends and waits until tsafe catches up to Tg (or times out). This "wait until the data is fresh enough, then answer" mechanism is the heart of docs/developer_guides/how-guarantee-ts-works.md — and the very reason the word "guarantee" is in "guarantee timestamp".

Waiting can't be infinite, of course. If a node falls too far behind (it lagged badly, or an upstream write surge hit), the read returns an error or degrades after a timeout rather than hanging forever — handing the correctness-vs-availability boundary back to the caller. This also explains why Strong's latency rises under heavy write pressure: Tg is pinned to the latest tMax, and the node must drain its backlog of WAL before tsafe can catch up. Conversely, Bounded nudges Tg back a little, so a read very often satisfies tsafe ≥ Tg on arrival and barely waits — which is why it is the default. Note too that tsafe advances per vchannel (shard): a query waits for every shard it touches to reach tsafe ≥ Tg, so one stuck shard's consumption slows the whole strong-consistency read. Internalize this and you can map a production "occasional slow query" directly onto "write backlog / a node lagging in consumption" — they're usually two sides of the same coin.

So how do you pick a level in practice? A simple decision guide: need to read your own write immediately (you just uploaded an image and want to search it)? Use Session or Strong. Extremely consistency-sensitive, willing to be slower for the very latest (some finance/audit cases)? Use Strong. The vast majority of high-concurrency search / recommendation / RAG, where missing a few latest rows is harmless but low latency and high throughput matter? Use Bounded (the default). Only need "roughly fresh" and chase raw speed (offline analytics, massive recall)? Use Eventually. Doing a time-travel query against a historical snapshot? Use Customized with an explicit ts. In one line: first decide how stale this query can tolerate, then work backward to the level — far wiser than reflexively reaching for Strong "to be safe", because unnecessary strong consistency usually buys you unnecessary latency.

t1writes keep entering the WAL, TimeTick advances; the QueryNode consumes and applies, tsafe rises
t2a read arrives with Tg=100; right now the node's tsafe=98 < 100
t3the node suspends: keep consuming the WAL until tsafe ≥ 100
t4tsafe=100, all writes up to Tg are present; filter by MVCC and return

The trade-off: the fresher you want it, the longer you may wait

Now the design intent of the five levels is clear: they are a knob between "freshness" and "latency". Strong sets Tg to the latest tMax — sees the most, but if the node hasn't caught up it must wait, so latency may be higher. Bounded nudges Tg back a touch (tMax − gracefulTime), trading "tolerate a few seconds of staleness" for "almost never wait", which is why it is a common default. Eventually sets Tg tiny, never waits, returns instantly, at the cost of maybe missing the last few writes. Session guarantees you at least read your own writes, friendly to "write then immediately query" interactions. Customized hands "as of when" entirely to you. Recall Lesson 1's "query while you write": that works precisely because growing segments + the tsafe mechanism make "freshly written data immediately visible to some Tg" — whether you see it, and whether you wait, is set by the level you choose. There is no one-size-fits-all answer, only a trade-off matched to the scenario: want strong consistency, accept a possible wait; want low latency, accept possible staleness.

Consistency level = the freshness ↔ latency knob: a read carries a guarantee ts (Tg); the node compares its tsafe — tsafe ≥ Tg answers now, otherwise it waits. Strong(Tg=tMax) sees the newest but may wait; Bounded(default, rolled back by gracefulTime) barely waits; Eventually(Tg≈1) never waits, may be slightly stale; Session guarantees you read your own writes; Customized time-travels to a moment you pick.

Toward freshness (Strong)

see all committed writes so far; for correctness-critical scenarios. Cost: must wait when the node hasn't caught up; latency may rise.

Toward low latency (Bounded / Eventually)

tolerate a little staleness, barely wait, respond fast; for high-concurrency cases (recommendation, RAG) where a few missing rows are harmless.

A closing of the whole guide so far

This lesson also closes the query path (Part 6). Stringing the six parts together: data you write first becomes vectors (Part 2), is written along the log via the Proxy into segments and binlogs (Part 4), and is built into indexes (Part 5); at query time the Proxy sets Tg and dispatches, the delegator fans out to segments, segcore filters + searches, reduce merges, and finally tsafe + MVCC guarantee you see a self-consistent snapshot with a freshness you can choose. With that, the main thread — "how one similarity search is completed correctly, efficiently and controllably in a distributed system" — closes the loop. The remaining parts (the C++ core, the streaming system, operations and contributing) drill further down and reach further out, but they all hang on this thread.

Looking back, why is this the finale lesson? The timestamp is the thread that stitches every earlier mechanism together. Write ordering depends on it (Lesson 16's TimeTick), delete/upsert versioning is told apart by it (Lesson 20's tombstones), segment visibility is judged by it (Lesson 7's growing/sealed), and the merge picks the right version with it (Lesson 29). This lesson unifies them into one sentence: a read is "take a Tg, wait for the data to catch up, then view the snapshot as of Tg". Next time you write consistency_level="Bounded" in the SDK, may you picture this whole chain — TSO stamping, the Proxy fixing Tg, the QueryNode waiting on tsafe, segcore filtering by MVCC — a whole apparatus that makes "distributed, query-while-you-write, and still correct" hold at once. If the previous five lessons were about how results are searched out and merged, this one is about why those results are correct and as of which instant — the former is performance, the latter correctness; together they truly finish the story of "one query".

📌 Key points
  • TSO: a global, monotonic timestamp source in RootCoord; both writes and reads are stamped, giving the cluster one clock.
  • Guarantee ts Tg + five levels: Strong=latest tMax, Bounded=tMax−gracefulTime, Session=this session's last write, Eventually=1, Customized=user-given (parseGuaranteeTsFromConsistency).
  • MVCC: a row is visible iff insert ts ≤ Tg and no tombstone with ts ≤ Tg; a read sees "the snapshot as of Tg" — reads/writes don't block, results are self-consistent.
  • Wait mechanism: a QueryNode answers only once its tsafe (consumed WAL TimeTick) ≥ Tg — the source of the word "guarantee".
  • Trade-off: freshness ↔ latency on one knob; strong consistency may wait, low latency may be slightly stale — pick a level by scenario.

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. What does the 'guarantee timestamp Tg' on a read mean, and what determines it?
  1. The data's physical address on disk
  2. 'Let me see at least all writes up to Tg'; set by the read's consistency level (parseGuaranteeTsFromConsistency)
  3. How many results the query returns (topK)
  4. The vector dimension
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. Tg expresses 'up to which moment this read must see writes'. The consistency level maps it: Strong=latest tMax, Bounded=tMax−gracefulTime, Eventually=1, Session=this session's last write, Customized=user-given.
2. By what mechanism does a QueryNode 'guarantee' it sees all writes up to Tg?
  1. It restarts itself to load the latest data
  2. It simply trusts its local clock
  3. It locks the whole collection, blocking writes
  4. It tracks tsafe (the consumed WAL TimeTick); it answers only when tsafe ≥ Tg, otherwise it waits (or times out)
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: D. A node's tsafe is how far it has consumed/applied the WAL (a TimeTick). On a read, if tsafe ≥ Tg all writes up to Tg are present, so search now; otherwise it waits until tsafe catches up. That is the source of the word 'guarantee'.
3. Which statement about Milvus consistency levels is correct?
  1. Strong is always faster than Bounded
  2. It only decides 'how fresh a read sees writes', not whether writes persist; different queries on one collection can use different levels
  3. Choosing Eventually causes data loss
  4. It is a global switch for the whole DB, changing it affects all reads and writes
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. A level is a 'per-read freshness requirement'; it doesn't change write persistence or eventual visibility. So a reconciliation job can use Strong, user search Bounded, offline backfill Eventually — independently. Strong is freshest but may wait, not necessarily faster.
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • Pick a consistency level for one of your own scenarios and justify it: e.g. 'a user uploads an image then immediately does image-to-image search', 'similar-product recommendation on a busy storefront (high concurrency)', 'offline batch dedup', 'audit replay of data as of a historical moment'. Which of Strong / Bounded / Session / Eventually / Customized fits each? Use the two clues: 'how stale can it tolerate' and 'latency requirement'.

WAL architecture

Lesson 16 said it: Milvus records every write into a WAL (Write-Ahead Log) first, and that log is the system's single source of truth. That lesson only sketched it; this part (Part 7) goes into the log's "engine room" — who runs it, how it is written, and how it recovers after a crash. The skeleton: StreamingCoord decides "which log goes to which machine", StreamingNode "actually writes that log", and underneath sits a pluggable WAL Backend (Kafka / Pulsar / Woodpecker / RocksMQ). Understand this and you understand the very bottom of how Milvus "writes".

🔌 Analogy
Think of the WAL as a bank's ledger: for any operation, record first, then act — once it's in the ledger, even if the teller goes home or the system reboots, replaying the entries one by one fully restores the state. StreamingCoord is like head-office dispatch: deciding which branch (PChannel) each bookkeeper (StreamingNode) handles. StreamingNode is the bookkeeper: writing entry by entry, stamping timestamps, managing transactions, locking when needed. The kind of paper the ledger is on (Kafka/Pulsar/…) can change, but the iron rule "record before you act" does not.
🌍 Big picture
In one line: StreamingCoord (a singleton inside the RootCoord process) assigns PChannels to StreamingNodes; a StreamingNode lands each write into the WAL Backend through an "interceptor chain" (TimeTick/Txn/Shard/Lock), and RecoveryStorage handles checkpoints and crash recovery. The WAL Backend is pluggable (Kafka/Pulsar/Woodpecker/RocksMQ), with one topic per PChannel. The whole write path's reliability and ordering rest on this architecture.

Who runs the WAL: StreamingCoord and StreamingNode

There isn't one WAL but many — recall Lesson 7: a collection is sharded into VChannels mapped to physical PChannels, and each PChannel is an independent log stream. With so many logs, someone must decide "which log is written by which machine", and that's StreamingCoord's job. It is a singleton, doesn't run as a separate process, but runs inside the RootCoord part (today, inside MixCoord). It does channel management: assigning PChannels to StreamingNodes, monitoring node health, rebalancing on join/leave (the balancer), and allocating VChannel/CChannel. In short, StreamingCoord is the "scheduling brain of the log", but it writes no log itself.

The one that actually writes is StreamingNode. Each StreamingNode owns a batch of assigned PChannels and runs a full WAL mechanism per PChannel (code in internal/streamingnode/server/wal/). It has plenty to do: stamp each write with a TimeTick (the monotonic clock), manage transactions, do segment assignment (Shard Management), lock for exclusive writes when needed, provide a scanner for consumers to read the log, and — most importantly — checkpoint via RecoveryStorage and rebuild state from the log after a crash. In a line: StreamingCoord owns "who writes", StreamingNode owns "how to write it well, stably, and recoverably". This is Milvus's recurring "coordinator schedules, node executes" split, showing up once more at the very bottom of the write path.

scheduleStreamingCoord
singleton inside RootCoord; assigns PChannels to StreamingNodes, health monitoring, rebalancing (writes no log)
executeStreamingNode
runs the WAL per PChannel: interceptor chain (TimeTick/Txn/Shard/Lock) + scanner + RecoveryStorage
storageWAL Backend
pluggable: Kafka / Pulsar / Woodpecker / RocksMQ, one topic per PChannel

The interceptor chain: every write "runs the gauntlet"

A StreamingNode doesn't just "append straight to the end". Every Append first passes through an interceptor chain — an ordered set of handlers, each owning one cross-cutting concern. The beauty: separate orthogonal concerns — stamping timestamps, managing transactions, assigning segments, locking — so each is independent, pluggable and testable, rather than tangled together. The main gates are:

The interceptor chain = an assembly line: every Append threads ① Lock (outermost — acquired first and held across the whole chain) ② TimeTick (stamp a PChannel-level monotonic ts — what the read path's tsafe waits on) ③ Txn (transaction atomicity, handled inside the TimeTick interceptor) ④ Shard (segment assignment — the segment ID is fixed here), then ⑤ truly lands in the WAL Backend. Concerns stay orthogonal and pluggable — a new write semantic is just one more link, no change to the core append.
1

Lock interceptor (outermost)

acquires the lock first and holds it across the whole chain — exclusive/shared access control at VChannel or PChannel scope, ensuring correct ordering of concurrent writes. It sits at the outer layer.

2

TimeTick interceptor

stamps the message with a PChannel-level monotonically increasing TimeTick — the "log clock" that the read path's tsafe waits on (Lesson 30).

3

Txn (handled inside the TimeTick interceptor)

manages the transaction lifecycle: framing a batch that should be "all-or-nothing visible" into one atomic log transaction.

4

Shard interceptor

does segment assignment: which growing segment these rows land in (the segment ID is decided here — recall Lesson 15, "segment ID is assigned at the StreamingNode").

5

Land in the Backend

after all interceptors, the message is actually appended to the WAL Backend's topic, becoming one immutable log entry.

internal/streamingnode/server/wal/the WAL and its interceptor chain (illustrative)
// each PChannel has a WAL; Append passes the interceptor chain first
type WAL interface {
    Append(ctx, msg) (MessageID, error)   // via interceptors -> land in backend
    Read(opt) (Scanner, error)            // consumers read the log in order
}
// interceptors: timetick / txn / shard / lock, mounted in order

WAL Backend: the underlying "log paper" is swappable

At the end of the interceptor chain, the message is actually written to the WAL Backend. Milvus abstracts "what stores the log underneath" into a pluggable interface (walimpls), so you can pick different implementations per deployment shape while the upper write logic doesn't change a line. The contract is simple: one PChannel maps to one topic in the backend; the log is appended in order within a topic and can be replayed by multiple consumers. The common backends each have trade-offs:

Why insist on one topic per PChannel? Because a PChannel is Milvus's smallest unit of ordering: within a single PChannel every write carries a strictly increasing TimeTick and is consumed in exactly that order, so "written first, seen first" always holds inside a shard. Across PChannels there is no global-ordering requirement (that would sacrifice parallelism); cross-shard global coordination is handled separately by the CChannel (the control channel). Getting both seemingly contradictory properties at once — strong ordering within a shard, high parallelism across shards — comes precisely from the clean "one shard, one log, one topic" correspondence. Grasp this and you see why scaling out means "add shards / add PChannels": each extra independent log adds another stream of parallel throughput, while the ordering inside each log is untouched.

BackendFormGood for
WoodpeckerMilvus's built-in WALrecommended for new instances: good perf, simple ops, low cost
Pulsarexternal message queuethe traditional cluster default, mature ecosystem
Kafkaexternal message queuewhen you already have Kafka infrastructure
RocksMQsingle-node local queueStandalone default (not supported in cluster)

Separating the log's semantics (append, ordered, replayable) from its implementation (Kafka or Woodpecker) is one of the smartest parts of this architecture: the semantics are a contract Milvus holds firmly, while the implementation is delegated to the most suitable storage. This echoes Lesson 8's deployment shapes — the same code uses RocksMQ in Standalone and Pulsar/Woodpecker in a cluster, entirely transparent to the upper layers.

RecoveryStorage: after a crash, redo it from the log

The WAL is called a "write-ahead" log precisely because its value pays off on a crash. Picture a StreamingNode suddenly dying: its in-memory growing segments, freshly assigned segment info, and consume progress are all gone. What now? The answer is RecoveryStorage (internal/streamingnode/server/wal/recovery): during normal operation it periodically checkpoints, recording "how far I've safely processed the log and the corresponding metadata"; after a restart it starts from the latest checkpoint and replays the WAL after it, rebuilding segments, progress and other state exactly. Because the WAL is the single source of truth and every entry carries a monotonic TimeTick, "replay" is deterministic — replaying the same log necessarily yields the same state. Checkpoint + replay is another landing of the distributed-systems philosophy "failure is the default, backed by the log" (recall Lesson 14's "failure as default").

If you've used a traditional database, "WAL + checkpoint + crash replay" will feel familiar — single-node databases (MySQL's redo log, PostgreSQL's WAL) rely on exactly this to not lose committed transactions after a crash. Milvus borrows the same plain-but-powerful idea, but scales it out to a distributed system and makes it the trunk rather than a side-record: in a single-node database the WAL is "a by-product that exists for crash recovery", with tables and indexes as the stars; in Milvus, the WAL itself is the star and the only source of truth, while tables (segments) and indexes become derived data — "born from the log, rebuildable from the log at any time". This inversion of primary and secondary is the essence of "log as data", and it's why Milvus dares to keep segments in memory and let multiple data forms trail the log asynchronously: as long as the WAL survives, anything lost can be replayed from scratch.

One side we've left unspoken: the consume side. A log gets written, but someone has to read it back and use it. A StreamingNode provides a scanner so consumers can read a PChannel's log in order — recall Lesson 17, where the StreamingNode's flusher follows the WAL to turn messages into growing segments and then flush them into binlogs, and Lesson 26, where the QueryNode's delegator keeps growing data fresh by consuming the tail of the WAL. In other words, the same WAL is replayed by many consumers, each at its own pace, each tracking its own "how far I've read" (its checkpoint/seek position). This "one log, many parties replaying it on demand" pattern decouples writing, flushing, querying and replication: nobody calls anybody directly — each just watches the same append-only log and advances at its own rhythm. Reducing "collaboration" to "sharing one ever-growing log" is the root reason this architecture can be both complex and orderly.

Checkpoint
record processed log position + metadata
→
Crash
in-memory state lost
→
Replay
replay WAL after the checkpoint
→
State restored
segments/progress rebuilt exactly

All of it, for a "single source of truth"

Step back: this whole machinery — scheduling, interceptors, a pluggable backend, checkpoint-replay — ultimately serves one sentence: make the WAL a reliable, ordered, replayable single source of truth. Once that holds, everything above falls into place — the read path waits on TimeTick/tsafe for the data it should see (Lesson 30), DataNode/StreamingNode rebuild segments by replaying the WAL, replicas and CDC sync by copying the WAL (the next two lessons). "Writing" collapses into "append to one reliable log", and "recovery/sync/consistency" all reduce to "replay that log". That is the full power of "log as data". Next lesson, we look at a special kind of write — DDL/DCL — and why it can't just go into one log like an ordinary write, but needs a mechanism called the Broadcaster to atomically broadcast across many logs.

📌 Key points
  • Split: StreamingCoord (singleton, inside RootCoord) schedules — assigns PChannels to StreamingNodes; StreamingNode executes — actually writes each PChannel's WAL.
  • Interceptor chain: every Append passes Lock / TimeTick / Txn / Shard in order (Lock is outermost, acquired and held first), separating locking, timestamp-stamping, transactions and segment assignment.
  • Pluggable WAL Backend: Kafka / Pulsar / Woodpecker / RocksMQ, one topic per PChannel; the semantics (append/ordered/replayable) are fixed, the implementation swappable.
  • RecoveryStorage: periodic checkpoints + replay the WAL from a checkpoint after a crash, deterministically rebuilding state — "failure is the default, backed by the log".
  • The destination: all the complexity serves "make the WAL the single source of truth"; recovery/sync/consistency all reduce to "replay the log".
  • Primary/secondary inversion: in a traditional database the WAL is a by-product for crash recovery and the tables are the star; in Milvus the WAL is the star and single source of truth, while segments/indexes are derived data rebuildable from the log.

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. In Milvus's streaming system, how do StreamingCoord and StreamingNode divide the work?
  1. StreamingCoord (a singleton inside RootCoord) schedules — assigns PChannels to StreamingNodes; StreamingNode executes — actually writes each PChannel's WAL
  2. StreamingNode schedules and StreamingCoord writes the log
  3. Both write the log directly, with no division
  4. They only read, never write
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: A. Another 'coordinator schedules, node executes': StreamingCoord does channel management (assign PChannels, health monitoring, rebalancing) but writes no log; StreamingNode runs the full WAL (interceptor chain + scanner + RecoveryStorage) for each assigned PChannel.
2. Every Append passes an 'interceptor chain'. Which set are its main stages?
  1. compress / encrypt / dedup / sort
  2. build index / query index / drop index / rebuild
  3. TimeTick (stamp a monotonic ts) / Txn (transaction) / Shard (segment assignment) / Lock (exclusive/shared)
  4. parse SQL / optimize / execute / return
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: C. The chain separates cross-cutting concerns in order: TimeTick stamps the clock the read path waits on, Txn handles transaction atomicity, Shard decides the segment ID (segments are assigned here), Lock controls concurrent exclusivity. A new write semantic is just a new interceptor.
3. Which statement about the WAL Backend and crash recovery is correct?
  1. The backend is pluggable (Kafka/Pulsar/Woodpecker/RocksMQ, one topic per PChannel); RecoveryStorage rebuilds state deterministically via checkpoint + WAL replay
  2. After a crash data can't recover, you must reload
  3. Recovery relies on aligning nodes' local clocks
  4. The backend is hard-coded to Kafka and cannot change
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: A. Milvus abstracts log storage into a pluggable interface (walimpls), one PChannel per topic; after a crash RecoveryStorage replays the WAL from the latest checkpoint. Because the WAL is the single source of truth with monotonic TimeTicks, replay is deterministic and state rebuilds exactly.
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • This lesson says Milvus makes 'the WAL the protagonist, with segments/indexes as log-derivable derived data' — the opposite priority of a traditional single-node DB (where the WAL is just a crash-recovery byproduct and tables are the protagonist). Consider: what capabilities does this 'inversion' buy Milvus (segments in memory, multiple data forms tailing the log asynchronously, replicas/CDC syncing by copying the log)? And what cost or precondition does it impose (a strong dependence on the WAL's reliability, ordering and replayability)?

DDL/DCL through the log

Last lesson's writes had an implicit premise: one insert/delete lands in one PChannel. But one kind of write is inherently different — DDL/DCL (create/drop collection, create/drop partition, create database, alter alias, RBAC grants…). They change not one shard's data but the metadata of the whole collection (even the whole cluster), so they must apply atomically and at once to many PChannels: you can't have the east shard already "know the collection exists" while the west shard does not. An ordinary write into one log suffices; DDL/DCL must "broadcast one statement to all relevant logs, all-or-nothing". A mechanism called the Broadcaster handles exactly this.

🔌 Analogy
An ordinary write is like a memo to one department (into that one line). DDL is like changing the company-wide rulebook at once: you can't have Marketing on the new rules while Finance is still on the old — chaos. The right way: first lock the relevant departments (so nobody secretly edits the same rulebook meanwhile), deliver the new rules to every department simultaneously, and only once every department acknowledges "received and in effect" does the change count. The Broadcaster does exactly this "lock → deliver to all → collect acks", ensuring everyone agrees on "which rulebook is current".
🌍 Big picture
In one line: DML uses Append (into one PChannel); DDL/DCL uses Broadcast — via StreamingCoord's Broadcaster, atomically broadcast to all relevant PChannels, with resource locking and ACK confirmation. Every DDL/DCL event is a typed Message (create-collection / create-partition / RBAC… each its own type). So "changing structure" and "changing data" share one log and one set of timestamps — naturally ordered, replayable and recoverable.

DML vs DDL: one log vs a flock of logs

First, sort out the two kinds of writes. DML (data manipulation) — insert, delete, upsert — affects specific rows; those rows hash by primary key into some VChannel/PChannel, so one DML only needs one log (Lessons 15, 31). DDL (data definition) — create/drop collection, create/drop partition, create database, alter alias — and DCL (data control) — RBAC grant/revoke — change metadata (schema, partitions, permissions), which holds for all shards of the collection. Imagine "create collection C" reaching only half the PChannels: half the shards think C exists and accept writes, the other half don't recognize C — data splits. So DDL/DCL must meet two hard requirements: (1) atomicity (all relevant PChannels take effect, or none) and (2) a clear order relative to DML ("create the collection, then write into it" causality must not scramble). An ordinary Append can't give these two, hence the Broadcaster.

Why is "half applied" so deadly? Because many Milvus components independently consume their own PChannels to perceive the world (Lesson 31's "one log, many parties replaying it"). If a create-collection message reaches only half the PChannels, the StreamingNodes, DataNodes and QueryNodes owning the other half land in a split state — holding different beliefs about whether the same collection even exists: some start allocating segments, building indexes and accepting queries for it, while others reject incoming writes as an "unknown collection". This metadata-level inconsistency is far scarier than losing a few rows — it robs the system of a single answer to "which collections exist right now, with what schema, and who has permission", triggering cascading errors. So DDL/DCL atomicity isn't a nice-to-have, it is the floor of correctness: either everyone enters the new belief at once, or everyone stays on the old — never an in-between. Once you see this floor, you understand why the Broadcaster's seemingly fussy "lock + all-acks" is indispensable: it exists to abolish the in-between state entirely.

One log vs a group of logs: DML (insert/delete) hashes by primary key into a single PChannel — one log entry, small scope, high parallelism, the fastest path. DDL/DCL (create/drop collection, RBAC) change whole-collection metadata, so they go through the Broadcaster — «lock + collect all acks» to atomically broadcast to every PChannel — all or nothing, never the split state where half the shards know collection C and half don't.

DML: single-PChannel Append

insert/delete land in one PChannel by primary key, into one log. Small scope, high parallelism.

DDL/DCL: multi-PChannel Broadcast

create/drop collection, RBAC, etc. change metadata and must atomically broadcast to all relevant PChannels — all or nothing.

The Broadcaster: lock, broadcast, collect acks

Take the most intuitive causal example: you first create_collection("docs") and immediately insert a batch into docs. If "create collection" and "insert data" travel two unrelated paths, the insert message could well arrive before the create-collection at some shard — and that shard is baffled: "docs? I don't know it." So the write fails or is undefined. The only reliable way to rule this out is to line "create collection" and "insert" up on the same ordered timeline, with "create collection" taking effect atomically and first across all relevant shards. The Broadcaster serves the first half (atomic broadcast), while the "same timeline" comes from encoding DDL into the WAL too, sharing the TimeTick. Now let's see how the Broadcaster actually achieves "atomic".

The Broadcaster (code in internal/streamingcoord/server/broadcaster) is the part of StreamingCoord dedicated to "atomic cross-PChannel broadcast". A DDL/DCL's journey: the client's request goes through the Proxy and calls StreamingClient.Broadcast (not the ordinary Append); the message is handed to StreamingCoord's Broadcaster; the Broadcaster first does resource locking — locking the resource this change touches (say a collection) so two concurrent DDLs can't conflict on the same object; then it distributes the DDL message to all relevant PChannels (each writes it as a log entry); finally it tracks the ACK from each PChannel — only when all relevant PChannels confirm "I've written this DDL into my log" does the broadcast succeed and the lock release. Lock + all-acks together guarantee "atomic, consistent" effect.

A few details here are worth savoring. First, the granularity of the resource lock: the Broadcaster locks "the resource this change touches" (say one specific collection), not the whole cluster — so unrelated DDLs (altering collection A vs B) can run in parallel and only truly conflicting ones are mutually exclusive, keeping correctness without sacrificing concurrency. Second, what if it fails: if some PChannel is slow to ACK during the broadcast (e.g. its owning node died), the broadcast isn't counted as successful; combined with the last lesson's "node lost → PChannel reassigned → replay from checkpoint", the node that takes over recovers this DDL along with everything else, still converging to consistency. Third, the meaning of the ACK: it confirms not "the message reached the network" but "this DDL has been written into that PChannel's WAL" — so once the broadcast succeeds, the change is recorded durably and replayably in every relevant log and survives any crash. Stringing these together, the Broadcaster essentially builds an "all-or-nothing" atomic-commit protocol on top of many independent logs — the classic distributed-systems hard problem, which Milvus solves quite cleanly with "log + lock + ACK".

DDL request
StreamingClient.Broadcast
①lock
Broadcaster
lock the involved resource (e.g. a collection)
②broadcast
many PChannels
each writes the DDL into its own WAL
③collect acks
atomic effect
all acked → success → release lock
1

Broadcast initiated

the Proxy calls StreamingClient.Broadcast; the DDL message goes to StreamingCoord's Broadcaster.

2

Resource lock

lock the resource this change touches, preventing concurrent-DDL conflicts.

3

Broadcast to PChannels

distribute the message to all relevant PChannels, each landing it in its own WAL.

4

All-acks to succeed

track each PChannel's confirmation; after all ACK, release the lock — broadcast succeeds.

internal/streamingcoord/server/broadcasteratomic cross-PChannel broadcast (illustrative)
// DDL/DCL go via Broadcast, not Append:
//   StreamingClient.Broadcast(msg)
//     -> Broadcaster: lock resource -> distribute to all relevant PChannels
//     -> collect each PChannel's ACK -> release lock, success

Message semantics: every event is a "typed sentence"

Whether DML or DDL/DCL, what's written into the WAL is a Message, and a semantic, typed one — not a blob of bytes but "a sentence the system understands". The streaming system defines distinct message semantics for different events (categorized in the docs message/message-semantic-*): create-collection is one type, create-partition another, and database, alias, RBAC each their own, with transaction begin/commit having their own messages too. Because messages are typed, each consumer knows precisely "what this log entry asks me to do": build a new segment, flush a batch, update a schema, or adjust a permission. Modeling "everything that happens in the system" uniformly as "a stream of semantic messages" is what lets this streaming architecture gracefully carry DML, DDL, DCL, transactions, replication and more — they differ in form, but in the log they are all "messages", all obeying the same ordered, replayable rules.

Message classTypical eventsScope
Collectioncreate/drop collection, alter schemaall PChannels of the collection
Partitioncreate/drop partitionall PChannels of the collection
Databasecreate/drop databasecluster level
Aliascreate/alter/drop aliascluster level
RBAC (DCL)grant/revoke role permissionscluster level
Txntransaction begin / commitframe a batch as one atomic unit

A pattern emerges from this table: the larger the scope, the more it needs "broadcast + atomic". Cluster-level Database/Alias/RBAC and collection-level Collection/Partition all need every relevant PChannel in lockstep, so they go through the Broadcaster; transactions (Txn) are another kind — not "broadcast to many shards" but framing a run of writes "begin…commit" into one atomic unit within one log, via the Txn interceptor from the last lesson. Seeing "atomicity" in two dimensions clarifies it: cross-PChannel atomicity via the Broadcaster (lock + all-acks), within-one-PChannel multi-write atomicity via transactions. With these two mechanisms, Milvus covers atomic needs of different scales, from "change a collection's structure" to "treat a batch as one".

Typed messages also bring a big engineering convenience: evolvability. To support a new DDL, you just add a new message type and a handling branch in the Broadcaster and the consumers — no need to redesign writing or ordering, because "broadcast + atomic + ordered + replayable" are shared by all messages. That is the compounding return of modeling "system events" as a "message stream": build the infrastructure once, and each new event is just "another kind of car on existing rails".

Why DDL also "goes into the log"

You might ask: just write metadata into etcd (Lesson 14) — why also stuff it into the WAL? The key is ordering and consistency. If DDL went via etcd and DML via the WAL, the two paths would have separate timelines, making "create collection, then write" causality hard to guarantee — a shard might receive a write while it "hasn't seen the create-collection yet", and be at a loss. By encoding DDL/DCL into the same WAL, ordered by the same TimeTick, "changing structure" and "changing data" land on one timeline: order is unambiguous and consistent for all consumers. And once DDL is in the log, it gains all the WAL's benefits: replayable (recover "the collection was created" after a crash), multi-consumer (each component perceives structural changes as needed). Metadata still ends up in etcd and the like, but "when the change happened and how it orders against data" is adjudicated uniformly by the WAL. This again shows the unifying beauty of "log as data": treat structural changes as just another message in the data stream, and the whole system only needs to understand one thing — "one ordered log". Next lesson, we see how this log can also be "copied out" — cross-cluster replication and CDC.

📌 Key points
  • DML vs DDL/DCL: DML affects some rows, into one PChannel (Append); DDL/DCL change metadata and must take effect atomically across all relevant PChannels.
  • Broadcaster (streamingcoord/server/broadcaster): StreamingClient.Broadcast → lock resource → broadcast to PChannels → collect ACKs → release lock, ensuring atomic consistency.
  • Message semantics: every event is a typed Message (create-collection/partition/database/alias/RBAC/txn each a type), so consumers know precisely "what to do".
  • DDL also in the log: sharing one WAL and one TimeTick with DML, "changing structure" and "changing data" land on one timeline — naturally ordered, replayable, consistent for all.
  • Two kinds of atomicity: cross-PChannel atomicity via the Broadcaster (lock + all-acks), within-one-PChannel multi-write atomicity via transactions (the Txn interceptor); an ACK confirms "written into the peer's WAL", so the change is durable and never lost.

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. Why can't DDL/DCL (create/drop collection, RBAC, etc.) just write into one PChannel like an ordinary insert?
  1. Because PChannels can't store metadata
  2. Because DDL is too large to fit in one log
  3. Because DDL must be slower than DML
  4. Because they change whole-collection/cluster metadata and must take effect atomically across all relevant PChannels, or shards would split on 'does the collection exist / schema / permissions'
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: D. DML affects some rows (one PChannel); DDL/DCL change metadata (schema/partitions/permissions) true for all of a collection's shards. If broadcast to only half the PChannels, shards disagree on 'does the collection exist', causing metadata-level inconsistency — a correctness floor, hence atomicity.
2. How does the Broadcaster ensure a DDL/DCL's 'atomic broadcast'?
  1. Have the client notify each shard one by one
  2. StreamingClient.Broadcast → lock the resource → distribute to all relevant PChannels → collect each PChannel's ACK → release the lock
  3. Write etcd directly, bypassing all PChannels
  4. Just write to one random PChannel
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. DDL goes via Broadcast, not Append: the Broadcaster locks the affected resource (preventing concurrent conflict), distributes the message to all relevant PChannels (each writes its WAL), tracks each ACK, and succeeds only after all confirm, then releases the lock. Lock + all-acks give 'all-or-nothing'.
3. Why does Milvus also encode DDL into the WAL, rather than only writing it into etcd?
  1. Because etcd lacks capacity
  2. To make DDL slower for observability
  3. So 'changing structure' and 'changing data' share one log and one TimeTick — naturally ordered (e.g. 'create then write' causality), replayable, consistent for all consumers
  4. The WAL is cheaper than etcd
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: C. If DDL went via etcd and DML via the WAL, two timelines make 'create-then-write' causality hard. Encoding DDL into the same WAL with the same TimeTick puts structural and data changes on one timeline, consistent for all; DDL also gains replayability and multi-consumer benefits. Metadata still lands in etcd, but 'when/how-ordered' is adjudicated by the WAL.
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • This lesson split 'atomicity' into two dimensions: cross-PChannel atomicity (Broadcaster: lock + all-acks) and within-PChannel multi-write atomicity (transactions: the Txn interceptor). Think of a concrete example needing each (e.g. create-collection must be acknowledged by all shards at once; a batch upsert must be all-or-nothing visible). Then consider: if one PChannel never ACKs during a broadcast (its node crashed), how — combined with Lesson 31's 'node lost → PChannel reassigned → replay from checkpoint' — does the system still converge to consistency?

Replication & CDC

By now in this part, "log as data" has recurred again and again. This lesson is its ultimate payoff: to sync one cluster's data to another cluster (cross-region disaster recovery, read replicas, smooth migration…), the simplest and most powerful way is not to copy state table by table and segment by segment, but to — copy that WAL over and let the other side replay it. Because the WAL is the single source of truth and replay is deterministic, "copying data" elegantly reduces to "copy an ordered log + replay it". That is Milvus's replication and CDC (Change Data Capture).

🔌 Analogy
Like a head office and a remote branch sharing one ledger: every entry head office (primary) records, it copies verbatim to the remote branch (secondary), which replays the copied entries — since both read the same ledger in the same order, the final balances match to the cent. The branch needn't understand any business detail, it just faithfully replays each copied entry. Should head office's city lose connectivity, the remote branch holds a complete copy of the ledger and can take over and keep operating — that is the basis of disaster recovery.
🌍 Big picture
In one line: Milvus does cross-cluster WAL replication via CDC (star topology). The primary's WAL → CDC ChannelReplicator → the secondary's Proxy → the secondary's WAL (via a Replicate interceptor) → the secondary replays. Because what's replicated is the log itself, each entry carrying a monotonic TimeTick, the secondary just consumes/replays its own WAL like any normal cluster and rebuilds data consistent with the primary — replication needs almost no "special logic".

The essence: replicate the "log", not the "state"

Syncing two databases has two fundamentally different paths. One is replicating state: periodically copy the "final results" — tables, segments, indexes — wholesale. Intuitive, but heavy and hard: data keeps changing, so a half-done copy is inconsistent, and you must handle increments, conflicts, versions. The other is Milvus's path, replicating the log: copy only the ordered stream of "what changed", and let the other side replay in the same order, naturally arriving at the same state. This is the classic database idea of CDC (Change Data Capture) — ship not "what it is now" but "what happened". For Milvus this path is almost free: it already has a WAL recording all changes, globally ordered and replayable; replication is merely one more consumer (the remote cluster) of that existing log. "Turning sync into copying one ordered log" is another big engineering saving from "log as data".

⚠️ Replicate state (the clumsy way)

copy tables/segments/indexes wholesale. With data changing, consistency is hard; you must handle increments, conflicts, versions — heavy and brittle.

✅ Replicate the log (Milvus / CDC)

copy only the ordered stream of "what changed"; the other side replays in order to the same state. Reuses the existing WAL, with almost no extra mechanism.

Data flow: from primary to secondary

Unpack the replication chain. In the primary, CDC's ChannelReplicator (code in internal/cdc/replication) watches the primary's WAL and reads out and forwards each change message to the secondary; the secondary's Proxy receives them, but it does NOT treat them as "new writes" to re-stamp — instead, via a special Replicate interceptor, it preserves the messages' existing TimeTick and other metadata and writes them into the secondary's own WAL. After that, the secondary consumes that WAL exactly like a normal cluster: StreamingNode lands segments and flushes binlogs, QueryNode loads segments and builds indexes… business as usual. The whole chain is:

The cleverest stroke here is the Replicate interceptor's "don't re-stamp" design. Recall Lesson 31: when an ordinary write enters the WAL, the TimeTick interceptor stamps it with a fresh local timestamp. But a replicated message already carries the TimeTick the primary stamped — if the secondary stamped its own on top, the two sides' ordering would diverge and their data would no longer agree. So the Replicate interceptor's job is exactly the opposite: recognize "this is a replicated message", keep its TimeTick verbatim, and leave it alone. That one small step of preserving the original order is what keeps the replay order on both sides strictly identical, and hence the state strictly identical. Notice how the only "special" part of the whole replication mechanism shrinks to this single interceptor doing one thing — "don't mis-stamp" — while everything else reuses the existing WAL write/consume/land-segment/build-index flow. The more you reuse and the fewer special cases you add, the simpler and less error-prone the system — the mark of good architecture.

Primary WAL
primary's change log
→
ChannelReplicator
read & forward (CDC)
→
Secondary Proxy
via Replicate interceptor
→
Secondary WAL
replay → rebuild data
internal/cdc/replicationcross-cluster WAL replication (illustrative)
// ChannelReplicator: read the primary WAL -> forward to the secondary
//   primary WAL --(read change messages)--> Replicator
//     --> secondary Proxy (Replicate interceptor: keep original TimeTick)
//       --> secondary WAL --> secondary consumes/replays as usual

Roles and topology: a star, one primary, many secondaries

Milvus's cross-cluster replication uses a star topology: one primary cluster at the center replicates one-way to several secondary clusters. Who is primary, who is secondary, and which channels replicate where, is coordinated and role-managed by the CDC controller (internal/cdc/controller). A star, not an arbitrary mesh, is a deliberate simplification: it keeps "change flows out from a single source" clear, avoiding the loops and conflicts of multi-primary mutual replication — once more the design bias of "converge complexity to one authoritative source" (recall the TSO does the same). When needed, primary/secondary roles can switch (e.g. the primary fails, promote a secondary to be the new primary), which is the basis of disaster failover.

Why one-directional rather than two sides syncing each other? Because "who is the single truth" must be unambiguous. If both clusters could write and replicate to each other, you'd hit the classic multi-primary conflict: the same row changed to different values on both sides at nearly the same instant — whose wins? Resolving multi-primary conflicts requires complex merge/arbitration logic, both hard and error-prone. Milvus's choice is clean: only one primary writes at a time, and changes flow one-way to the secondaries, which are read-only (serving reads only). With "truth from a single source", there's simply no room for conflict. When you need "active-active" or a switchover, the controller explicitly changes roles rather than letting both sides write freely. This "prefer simple over ambiguous" trade-off is the same engineering aesthetic as the TSO handing out numbers from one source and the Broadcaster broadcasting from one source: converge "who decides" onto one clear authority and most of the complexity collapses.

Star topology · replicate the log, not the state: one primary at the center replicates its already-existing WAL one-way to several secondaries (the CDC controller coordinates the roles). The neat trick: a secondary's Replicate interceptor «keeps the TimeTick the primary already stamped, instead of re-stamping» — that one "preserve the order" step keeps the primary and secondary replays in strictly the same order, hence identical state. A secondary is therefore just an ordinary consumer that happens to read the upstream log from far away.
primaryprimary cluster
the single source of change; its WAL is replicated out
controllerCDC controller
coordinates roles and the replication relationships (who replicates to whom)
secondarysecondary clusters (many)
one-way receive and replay the primary's WAL; can be promoted to primary

Where it's used: DR, cross-region, migration

Put "copy one log to a remote site and replay" to work and it solves several very real needs. (1) Disaster recovery (DR): keep a hot-standby cluster in another region tracking the primary; if the primary's data center fails entirely, promote the standby to primary and keep serving, with almost no data loss (depending on replication lag). (2) Cross-region read replicas: let a secondary closer to users serve local reads, cutting cross-region latency — writes still go to the primary, reads are offloaded nearby. (3) Smooth migration: to move data to a new cluster/version, first let the new cluster, as a secondary, catch up on the primary's full history and increments; after catching up, switch once, with almost no downtime. These three look different but are the same underneath: let another cluster have the same data by replaying the same log. That is the power of "log as data" at cross-cluster scale.

There's an unavoidable real-world factor here: replication lag. Stamping on the primary, forwarding, then replaying on the secondary all takes time, so the secondary's data is always slightly behind the primary (by an amount that depends on network and load). This lag determines "how much data you can lose" on failover: if the primary collapses entirely at some instant, the latest changes "already written on the primary but not yet replicated to the secondary" are at risk of loss — this is exactly RPO (Recovery Point Objective), the measure of DR capability. Happily, because what's replicated is an ordered log, the secondary knows exactly "which TimeTick it has replayed to", so it can resume from where it left off and catch up with confidence — never a "half-replicated, state scrambled" situation. To push RPO very small, minimize replication lag; to lose absolutely nothing, you need stronger synchronous-replication semantics — which returns to Lesson 30's eternal theme: consistency/freshness always trades off against performance/cost, and cross-cluster replication is no exception. Understand this trade-off and you'll know what each knob does when designing a DR plan.

① Disaster recovery (DR)

keep a hot standby in another region tracking the primary; on a full primary-DC failure, promote the standby to keep serving, with near-zero data loss.

② Cross-region reads

a standby near users serves local reads, cutting cross-region latency; writes still go to the primary, reads shared locally.

③ Smooth migration

moving to a new cluster/version, let the new one stand by and catch up all history + increment, then switch once — near-zero downtime.

Why it's so clean

Step back: cross-cluster data sync is notoriously hard in many systems — change capture, ordering, conflicts, resumable transfer, consistency… Why does Milvus's look so "light"? The answer is again three letters: WAL. Because the WAL is the single source of truth, all changes already have a global order (TimeTick), and replay is already deterministic — so "replication" degenerates into "ship one already-recorded, already-ordered, replayable log to a remote site too". The secondary needs no special "sync logic"; it is simply a normal cluster that took on one more upstream log; and all the Replicate interceptor does is "don't re-stamp replicated messages, keep their original TimeTick", so both sides' order stays strictly identical. A good foundational abstraction makes the hardest problems heal on their own — that is what this lesson, and all of Part 7, most wants to leave you with.

With that, Part 7 (the streaming system) is complete: Lesson 31 saw how the WAL is managed, written and recovered, Lesson 32 saw how DDL/DCL enter the log atomically by broadcast, and this lesson saw how the log is replicated to another cluster. Together they answer one question — when you fully model "writing" as "append to one reliable, ordered, replayable log", how do reliability, consistency, recovery, and even cross-cluster replication all become "operations on that one log". That is the design philosophy of Milvus's streaming system, and one of the roots of what sets it apart from many vector stores.

📌 Key points
  • Replicate the log, not state: cross-cluster sync = replicate the WAL + replay; ship "what happened" (CDC), not "what it is", reusing the existing WAL with almost no extra mechanism.
  • Data flow: primary WAL → CDC ChannelReplicator (internal/cdc/replication) → secondary Proxy (Replicate interceptor, keep original TimeTick) → secondary WAL → replay as usual.
  • Star topology + roles: one primary, many secondaries, one-way; the CDC controller coordinates roles; primary/secondary can switch — the basis of DR failover.
  • Uses: cross-region DR (promote standby), cross-region read replicas, smooth migration — all "let another cluster have the same data by replaying the same log".
  • Replication lag & RPO: the secondary always trails the primary slightly, which sets "how much can be lost" on failover; because what's replicated is an ordered log, the secondary knows exactly which TimeTick it has replayed to, so it can resume from where it left off and catch up.
  • Why clean: the WAL is the single source of truth + globally ordered + deterministic replay, so "replication" degenerates to "ship one more copy of the log"; a good abstraction heals the hardest problems.

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. What is the core idea behind Milvus's cross-cluster data sync?
  1. Let both clusters write and overwrite each other
