the proxy/impl.go Proxy validates, enqueues, fans out to the cluster, and merges results back.

📌 Key points
  • One contract: all APIs are defined by MilvusService in milvus-proto (generates milvuspb: SearchRequest/SearchResults…); server and SDKs all generate from the same proto, so behavior is inherently consistent.
  • Transport/logic split: the gRPC Server in internal/distributed/proxy only receives and forwards in one line; the real logic is the Proxy in internal/proxy/impl.go.
  • Many SDKs: pymilvus/Go/Java/Node are just "method↔proto message" translators; choose by team preference — core capability is unified by the proto.
  • Two doors: gRPC (efficient binary, production choice) and REST (gin, /v2/vectordb, zero-friction) lead to the same Proxy — identical semantics, only different wrapping.

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. Why does Milvus define all external APIs centrally in one milvus-proto (MilvusService) rather than each SDK agreeing on its own?
  1. Purely to generate more code
  2. Because each language's features should differ anyway
  3. The proto as single source of truth: server and all SDKs generate/bind from the same proto, sharing an identical understanding of requests/responses — consistent cross-language behavior and controlled evolution
  4. Because gRPC forbids multiple clients
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: C. milvus-proto uses Protocol Buffers to describe MilvusService and each endpoint's request/response messages (SearchRequest/SearchResults…), compiled into per-language code (milvuspb in Go). Server and all SDKs follow the same proto, so they agree on 'what a search looks like, what fields return' — no mismatch; adding a field means editing the proto then regenerating, backward-compatibly. Pulling the contract into its own repo lets a multi-language ecosystem orbit one center.
2. What's the relationship between the gRPC Server in internal/distributed/proxy and the Proxy in internal/proxy/impl.go?
  1. They are two names for the same class
  2. The former is the 'transport layer' (receive, auth, rate-limit; methods often just one line forwarding), the latter the 'logic layer' (real validation, enqueue, fan-out, merge); transport decoupled from logic
  3. Proxy(impl.go) does networking, Server(distributed) does business logic
  4. Server handles the C++ core, Proxy handles the GPU
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. service.go's Server RegisterMilvusServiceServer's itself as the MilvusService impl, but its Search is essentially one line: return s.proxy.Search(ctx, request). The real logic is impl.go's Proxy.Search (validate, enqueue dqQueue, fan out to QueryNodes, merge). The split exists because 'transport' (gRPC/TLS/auth/rate-limit/REST) and 'logic' (search semantics) change independently; decoupling lets each evolve and be tested, and lets one logic layer be reused by both the gRPC and REST doors.
3. About the gRPC and REST entrances, which statement is correct?
  1. Only gRPC works; REST is just for documentation
  2. REST is always faster and more bandwidth-efficient than gRPC
  3. REST and gRPC run two fully separate business logics and may return different results
  4. Both doors lead to the same Proxy with identical semantics, differing only in 'wrapping': gRPC binary-efficient (production), REST (gin, /v2/vectordb) zero-friction; the REST handler essentially turns JSON into the same proto calls on the same Proxy
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: D. The Proxy starts both gRPC and a gin HTTP server in service.go (registerHTTPServer; handlers in httpserver/handler_v2.go, path /v2/vectordb). Both doors converge on the same Proxy logic — the REST handler turns JSON into the matching proto request and calls the same Proxy methods, so semantics are identical. The difference is only wrapping: gRPC (HTTP2 binary, long connections, strongly typed) for production; REST (JSON, curl-ready) for getting started and gRPC-less/cross-language environments.
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • This lesson says 'one contract + one facade + two wrappings'. Tie to Lesson 3 (a request's journey) and Lesson 10 (Proxy): (1) From calling client.search(...) in pymilvus to getting results, which layers are crossed (SDK→proto→which door→transport Server→logic Proxy→cluster)? (2) Why does splitting 'transport (distributed/proxy)' from 'logic (proxy/impl.go)' help with adding new protocol entrances, doing auth/rate-limiting, and writing unit tests? (3) If you suspect a search result is wrong, how can you use 'gRPC and REST land on the same Proxy' to quickly tell whether it's a server-side or SDK-side problem?

Observability: logs/metrics/traces

Last lesson, a request came in through the API. But once it runs through Milvus's distributed system — across the Proxy, fanned to several QueryNodes, stirring DataCoord — how do we "see" it? When something breaks, where do we look? That's what observability solves. It has three pillars: logs (what happened), metrics (how's the whole doing), and traces (which nodes one request crossed). This lesson shows how Milvus implements all three — especially how they jointly trace a request spanning many nodes.

🔌 Analogy
Think of operating a distributed system as a hospital monitoring a patient. Logs are the detailed chart notes: at what time what happened, which medicine, what the doctor wrote — line by line, timestamped, traceable. Metrics are the bedside vital-signs monitor: heart rate, blood pressure, oxygen — numbers over time, telling at a glance "is the overall condition fine, any abnormal swings". Traces are following this one patient end to end: registration → exam → lab → pharmacy, how long each took, where it stalled. In a hospital of "many cooperating departments" (many nodes), one department's notes aren't enough — you must stitch the same patient's footprints across departments into one line to see where it's slow or wrong. All three are indispensable.
🌍 Big picture
In one line: Milvus's observability has three pillars — logs via pkg/mlog (ctx mandatory, structured fields, propagatable across nodes over gRPC); metrics via Prometheus-style pkg/metrics (each component instrumented); traces via OpenTelemetry (pkg/tracer + otelgrpc interceptors, spans flowing across RPCs). Together they answer "what a request actually experienced across the distributed system".

The three pillars of observability

"Observability" sounds abstract; unpacked it's three concrete things, each answering a different question. Logs answer "what exactly happened": timestamped event records, the first-hand evidence for going through the case file after a problem. Metrics answer "how's the whole doing": masses of events aggregated into numbers — requests per second, average latency, memory, queue length — plotted over time, ideal for watching trends and setting alerts. Traces answer "which stages one request crossed, and how long each took": the footprints of one request across nodes and phases, stitched into a hierarchical timeline.

Why is each indispensable? Each has blind spots. With only metrics, you see "latency spiked" but not which request, stuck where; with only logs, amid masses scattered across nodes, it's hard to pick out and connect the entries "belonging to the same request"; with only traces, you know "it stalled in the QueryNode segment" but may need that moment's detailed logs for the root cause. So mature systems go three-pronged: metrics to detect anomalies (alert), traces to locate which request and stage, logs to reconstruct that stage's detail. The table puts the three side by side, to remember "what each answers, implemented by what".

It's worth spelling out aggregation, because it's the most fundamental divide between logs and metrics. Logs are un-aggregated: every time something happens, one line is faithfully recorded, so they're rich in detail but frighteningly large in volume — a busy cluster can emit hundreds of millions of log lines a day, and there's no way to grasp the whole by reading them one by one. Metrics are the opposite: from the very start they fold same-kind events into numbers — not "request #10001 took 8 ms" but a running "average latency over the last minute" or "total request count", a cumulative quantity or distribution. That aggregation makes metrics extremely lightweight (numeric curves, not mountains of text), ideal for long retention, fast charting, and real-time alerting — at the cost of dropping individual detail: they can tell you "latency rose" but not "which request". So the two are natural complements — one fine and heavy, one coarse and light; one for investigating after the fact, one for watching the dashboard live. Grasp this "aggregate or not" tradeoff and you see why you can't keep only one: logs alone drown you and can't alert in real time, while metrics alone leave you seeing the smoke but unable to find the fire when something breaks.

PillarQuestion answeredFormMilvus impl
Logswhat exactly happened (event by event)timestamped text/structured recordspkg/mlog (zap)
Metricshow's the whole doing (trends/alerts)numeric curves over timepkg/metrics (Prometheus)
Traceswhich stages a request crossed, how long eachhierarchical cross-node timelinepkg/tracer (OpenTelemetry)

Logs: mlog — ctx mandatory, structured, propagatable across nodes

First the pillar you deal with most — logs. Milvus has an iron rule: logging may only use pkg/mlog — not the standard log, not bare fmt.Println, not raw zap. Why so strict? Because mlog shoulders the hardest parts of distributed logging for you, and its design is thoughtful throughout.

First touch: every log must carry ctx. You write mlog.Info(ctx, "message", mlog.String("field", value)) — the first argument is always the context. Why mandatory? Because ctx can carry "identity" accumulated along the way. Second touch: structured fields. Not splicing variables into a sentence ("user " + id + " failed") but typed key-value pairs like mlog.String, mlog.Int64. Structured logs are machine-queryable: a log system can filter precisely by "field = value" instead of grepping text. Third and most elegant: fields can propagate across nodes. Attach fields to ctx with mlog.WithFields(ctx, ...) and child contexts inherit them; further, mark a field with mlog.OptPropagated() (e.g. collection name, collection ID) and, via gRPC interceptors (mlog.UnaryServerInterceptor / UnaryClientInterceptor), those fields "fly" with the RPC to downstream nodes. So a marker stamped on a request at the Proxy reappears verbatim in the QueryNode and DataNode logs it triggers — one ID nets every related log across the whole cluster. That's the most precious ability when debugging distributed systems. The two styles compared:

Proxy
stamp req_id=abc + OptPropagated fields
→
QueryNode
interceptor inherits → logs carry req_id=abc
→
DataNode
inherits too → logs carry req_id=abc

A word more on why this "every log must carry ctx" rule deserves strict enforcement. In a single-machine program, whether a log carries context barely matters — it's all one process, and a little scrolling lets you line things up. But Milvus is distributed: a single request's "story" is written scattered across several processes and several machines. If every node just heads-down records its own segment with no shared "clue ID", those logs are like loose manuscript pages with no page numbers — all the information is there, yet it can never be reassembled into one whole story. The job of ctx is to stamp the same number on every page. Because that number rides along with ctx and can propagate across nodes through the interceptors, you can later take it and gather, in one stroke, every log belonging to the same request from a dozen machines. So this rule is not code-style fastidiousness; it's the life-or-death line for whether a distributed system can be debugged at all — drop one ctx and some critical log may go "off the grid", never rejoining the story it belongs to.

❌ String-spliced logs

log.Printf("user %d search failed", id). Info buried in text; each node logs its own, unmatchable; filtering by user means regex guessing; cross a node and the trail breaks.

✅ mlog: structured + propagatable

mlog.Info(ctx, "search failed", mlog.Int64("user", id)). Typed fields, precisely filterable; via ctx + OptPropagated + interceptors, the marker flows with the request across nodes, one ID stitching the whole cluster.

Metrics & traces: Prometheus + OpenTelemetry

The other two pillars both build on industry open standards, so they plug straight into mature ecosystem tools. Metrics are Prometheus-style: under pkg/metrics, each component has its own instrumentation file (datacoord_metrics.go, datanode_metrics.go, querynode, proxy, even cgo_metrics.go watching the Go↔C++ bridge). These are exposed at an HTTP endpoint, periodically scraped by Prometheus into time series, then drawn into dashboards by Grafana — latency, QPS, queue levels, memory at a glance, with thresholds for automatic alerts.

Traces use OpenTelemetry (today's prevailing tracing standard): the core is in pkg/tracer, depending on go.opentelemetry.io/otel. Its key is again "stitching the trail across nodes" — via otelgrpc interceptors (GetInterceptorOpts): when a request goes from Proxy over gRPC to a QueryNode, the interceptor tucks the "trace context" (containing this trace's unique ID and current span) into the RPC metadata; the downstream node, on receipt, attaches its own processing as a child span on the same trace. All nodes' spans, gathered into a backend like Jaeger, assemble a complete call tree: which segment is slowest, which hop errored, drawn as a timeline you see through at a glance. Notice logs (OptPropagated fields) and traces (otel trace context) use the same move — tuck "identity" into ctx, then propagate with the RPC. No coincidence — it's the core method of distributed observability.

A word on why Milvus chooses to stand on open standards like Prometheus and OpenTelemetry rather than rolling its own. Observability is only as valuable as your ability to plug it into handy tools and actually look — collecting metrics and spans isn't enough; someone has to chart them, alert on them, and search them by trace ID. Tools like Prometheus (metric storage and alerting), Grafana (dashboards), and Jaeger (trace visualization) are already the de facto standards of the cloud-native world, and most ops engineers already know them. Milvus only has to make its metrics and traces speak the common tongue these tools understand, and users can drop it straight into their existing monitoring stack instead of learning a proprietary dashboard for one more database. It's pragmatic ecosystem thinking: do the part Milvus genuinely owns — collection (instrument, propagate, expose endpoints) — properly, and on the part the mature ecosystem already does well — display and alerting — don't reinvent the wheel, just open up for integration. It's the same restraint as Lesson 37's "hand GPU algorithms to Knowhere/RAFT and focus on integration" and Lesson 8's "lean on mature components like etcd and object storage": leave the specialist work to specialist tools, and just do the connecting glue well.

Three as one: tracing a request across the distributed system

Wire the three pillars together for a real "search got slow" investigation. Step 1, metrics detect the anomaly: on Grafana the P99 latency curve suddenly rises, an alert fires — you know "something's wrong", but not who or where. Step 2, traces locate the stage: open a slow request's trace and the call tree is clear — the Proxy was fast, but among the three fanned-out QueryNodes, one child span is unusually long; the problem narrows to "that hop". Step 3, logs reconstruct the detail: take this request's propagated ID, filter the logs of that QueryNode, and the related entries surface neatly — turns out a segment was faulting in via mmap, cold data slow on first load (Lesson 35). Three steps, root cause in hand.

Three pillars as one · tracing a request across the cluster: give the request an identity that propagates across nodes (a traceID tucked into ctx, carried by gRPC interceptors the whole way). So the Proxy's span fans out to three QueryNode child spans, forming a call tree — QueryNode B's is unusually long, slow on this hop. The pillars relay: ① metrics detect "something's wrong", ② traces locate "which hop", ③ logs filter by the same ID and reconstruct the detail (an mmap page-fault, Lesson 35). Detect, locate, reconstruct — none is dispensable.
1

Metrics detect

Grafana's P99 latency rises, an alert fires — you know "something's wrong" but not who or where. (Prometheus metrics)

2

Traces locate

open the slow request's trace tree: one QueryNode's child span is unusually long; the problem narrows to "that hop". (OpenTelemetry)

3

Logs reconstruct

filter that node's logs by the request's propagated ID; the detail surfaces: an mmap fault, a cold segment slow on first load. (mlog)

The soul of this teamwork is that recurring throughline: give the request an "identity" that propagates across nodes. Whether mlog's propagatable fields or otel's trace context, the essence is tucking an identifier into ctx and letting gRPC interceptors carry it the whole way. That's why fragments scattered across a dozen nodes and thousands of logs/metrics/spans can re-gather, around "the same request", into one complete story. Recall Lesson 38's iron rule "every log must carry ctx" — now you see its depth: ctx isn't just a parameter, it's the thread stitching the whole distributed observability together. Next lesson turns to another ops fundamental: how this system's hundreds of configuration items are managed (paramtable and milvus.yaml).

📌 Key points
  • Three pillars: logs (what happened), metrics (overall trend/alert), traces (which stages a request crossed) — each has blind spots, so three-pronged: metrics detect, traces locate, logs reconstruct.
  • Logs = mlog: ctx mandatory, structured fields (mlog.String/Int64), propagatable across nodes via WithFields+OptPropagated+gRPC interceptors — one ID stitches cluster-wide logs.
  • Metrics = Prometheus (pkg/metrics per-component → scrape → Grafana); traces = OpenTelemetry (pkg/tracer + otelgrpc, trace context propagated over RPC, child spans forming a call tree).
  • Core method: tuck the request's "identity" into ctx and propagate with gRPC — logs and traces use the same move, which is the deeper point of the "every log carries ctx" rule.

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. What does each of the 'three pillars' of observability answer, and how do they work together?
  1. All three are names for the same data; keep just one
  2. Logs = what happened (detail), metrics = overall trend (alert), traces = which stages a request crossed; together: metrics detect → traces locate → logs reconstruct
  3. All three only matter on a single machine, useless in distributed mode
  4. Logs alert, metrics store details, traces draw dashboards
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. Each has blind spots: metrics show 'latency rose' but not which request/step; logs are massive and scattered, hard to connect one request's entries; traces show 'stuck in a segment' but lack detail. So three-pronged: metrics detect (alert), traces locate (which request/hop), logs reconstruct (that segment's detail). Milvus uses pkg/mlog, pkg/metrics (Prometheus), pkg/tracer (OpenTelemetry) respectively.
2. Milvus mandates logging via pkg/mlog with ctx on every call. What's the key benefit?
  1. mlog writes logs straight into the vector index
  2. ctx automatically translates logs into Chinese
  3. ctx can carry propagatable structured fields: via WithFields+OptPropagated+gRPC interceptors, the request's 'identity' flows with RPCs across nodes, so one ID gathers related logs cluster-wide
  4. Carrying ctx is purely to make signatures longer
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: C. Milvus is distributed: one request's 'story' is written across many processes/machines. mlog mandates ctx so logs carry structured fields (mlog.String/Int64); attaching them via WithFields with OptPropagated plus gRPC interceptors (Unary Server/Client) propagates them downstream. So a marker stamped at the Proxy reappears in the QueryNode/DataNode logs it triggers, and one ID later nets all related logs cluster-wide — a lifeline for distributed debugging.
3. This lesson says logs (mlog propagatable fields) and tracing (OpenTelemetry) 'use the same move'. What is it?
  1. Tuck the request's 'identity/context' into ctx and let gRPC interceptors carry it with the RPC to downstream nodes, re-gathering fragments scattered across nodes around 'the same request'
  2. Compress everything into one file
  3. Send both logs and spans to the same GPU
  4. Have each node number things by its local clock
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: A. The otelgrpc interceptor tucks the 'trace context' (the trace's unique ID and current span) into RPC metadata for downstream, which attaches its work as a child span on the same trace; mlog's OptPropagated fields propagate over RPC the same way. Both are 'tuck an identifier into ctx, propagate with gRPC' — the core method of distributed observability and the deeper point of 'every log carries ctx': ctx is the thread stitching it all together.
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • This lesson uses 'a hospital monitoring a patient' for the three pillars and stresses they cooperate by 'giving a request an identity that propagates across nodes'. Please: (1) in your own words, describe how you'd use metrics, then traces, then logs in turn to locate the root cause when 'search P99 suddenly slows' in production; (2) explain why, in a distributed system, logs alone or metrics alone are insufficient; (3) tying to Lesson 38's 'every log carries ctx' rule, discuss why 'tuck identity into ctx, propagate over gRPC' underpins both log aggregation and tracing.

Configuration: paramtable & milvus.yaml

A distributed system like Milvus hides hundreds of knobs: timeouts, pool sizes, quota ceilings, ports, storage paths, thresholds… How are they set, overridden per environment, read type-safely by code, and which can be turned while running (no restart)? This lesson covers Milvus's config system: on top is paramtable (a type-safe config registry); underneath are layered sources (the milvus.yaml file / environment variables / etcd), merged by priority, with some supporting hot reload.

🔌 Analogy
Think of config as a building's control panel. Each switch is labeled and typed (this is temperature, range 16–30, default 22) — you can't mis-set "temperature" to a sentence; that's paramtable's "type safety". A switch's value has several sources with a priority: printed on the wall is the factory default (milvus.yaml), a sticky note by the door can override the default (env vars), and a facilities control room can change some switches remotely, in real time (etcd) — the higher, more "personal" source wins. Better still: some switches can be turned while the building runs (hot reload — log level, rate limits), while others are locked, changeable only when shut down (ports). A well-designed panel makes you both able to change easily and unable to break things by accident.
🌍 Big picture
In one line: each config item is a ParamItem (with Key, default, doc, typed getters like GetAsInt/Bool), grouped by component (grpc/quota/http…); values come from layered sources — milvus.yaml, env vars, etcd — merged by config.Manager by priority (lesser value = higher priority), with mutable items hot-reloadable via callbacks. Code only reads typed values; it never parses strings itself.

A distributed system has hundreds of knobs

First, the scale of the problem. Milvus has a dozen kinds of components, each with its own tunables: the Proxy's rate limits, each RPC's timeout and retry, pool sizes, queue lengths, quotas and tenant limits, object-storage address and bucket, etcd paths, log level, whether to mmap a field… hundreds in all. Managing this many configs raises four unavoidable problems: how to set defaults for out-of-the-box use; how to override per environment (dev/test/prod differ); how code reads them (type-correctly — ports are ints, switches are bools — without hand-written string parsing everywhere); and which can be changed at runtime without restarting the whole cluster to tweak one threshold.

Without a unified scheme, these become a disaster: defaults scattered everywhere, changing one config means touching ten files, components disagree on what "timeout" means, reading config means strconv.Atoi all over the place that might panic… Milvus's answer is two layers: the upper paramtable provides a "type-safe, documented, grouped" read surface; the lower config provides a "multi-source, priority-merged, hot-reloadable" value surface. Split the two and "how to use config" and "where config comes from" each stay clean — a familiar "separation of concerns" again.

The payoff of this separation is most obvious when you're reading the source or chasing down "why isn't this config taking effect?". When you want to know "what is some timeout actually set to, and why that value", your path is crisp: first go to paramtable, find the matching ParamItem, and read its Key and default; then drop to the config layer and check whether that Key has been overridden in milvus.yaml, an env var, or etcd, and which source outranks which. The "use" question lives upstairs and the "fetch" question downstairs, cleanly divided, so you never spin in a tangle. The opposite — every piece of business code reading files and env vars directly and each deciding "who overrides whom" — would let the same config resolve to different values in different components, with override rules that each disagree, a nearly un-diagnosable bug. So funneling all value-fetching into config and all reading into paramtable isn't mere tidiness; it's what makes the whole system's config behavior predictable and explainable — a knob, however many places read it, has exactly one current value reached by one highest-priority chain, and that determinism is the bedrock requirement of large-system config management.

paramtable: a type-safe config registry

The upper layer first. Each config item, in code, is a ParamItem (defined in pkg/util/paramtable/param_item.go). It's far more than "a value" — it registers the knob's full metadata: Key (a hierarchical key like "A.B.C"), DefaultValue, Doc (what the knob is for), FallbackKeys (compatibility with old key names), plus behavior flags like PanicIfEmpty, Immutable, Formatter.

The key part is its typed getters: GetValue() for string, GetAsBool() for bool, GetAsInt() / GetAsInt64() for ints… So consumer code looks like Params.ProxyCfg.SomeTimeout.GetAsInt() — getting a correctly-typed value directly, never parsing strings itself. This seems small but matters: it funnels "string → type" conversion into one place (inside ParamItem), avoiding scattered strconv and duplicated bugs, and making "forgot to convert, converted to the wrong type" errors impossible to occur. These ParamItems are also organized by component/topic across files: grpc_param.go, quota_param.go, http_param.go, knowhere_param.go… each file gathering one class of knobs, neatly. The layered diagram shows the structure from "value base" to "grouped knobs" to "a single ParamItem".

Beyond type safety, the metadata a ParamItem registers each earns its keep, and it's worth naming them one by one. DefaultValue makes the system work out of the box — configure nothing and it still runs on a set of sensible defaults. Doc is more than a comment: tools can extract it to auto-generate the config documentation, so the one-line explanation beside each knob in milvus.yaml and the definition in code share a single source and never drift apart. FallbackKeys handles historical baggage: when a config item is renamed, its old key name is still recognized as a fallback, so upgrading Milvus doesn't make an existing config file go invalid overnight. And PanicIfEmpty is a startup-time guardrail: for the critical configs that "can't function at all if absent", it deliberately fails loudly at startup over the missing value, rather than carrying an empty value and crashing mysteriously halfway through. Making a config item a fully-registered object instead of a bare string reflects a whole set of deliberate care for maintainability, upgradeability, and diagnosability — exactly what separates mature engineering from a toy project.

value baseconfig.Manager (merges sources, gives the current value by priority)
backed by file/env/etcd sources; supports hot-update dispatch
groupscomponent params (grpc_param / quota_param / http_param …)
categorize hundreds of knobs by component/topic for findability & upkeep
itemParamItem (Key/Default/Doc + GetAsInt/Bool…)
registers metadata, offers typed reads; code reads typed values, no string parsing

Where config comes from: layered sources and priority

Now the lower layer — where values actually come from. The config package (pkg/config) defines several sources, each with a priority. The most basic is FileSource: reading configs/milvus.yaml, the human-readable default config file and the "catalog of every knob" — to learn which configs exist and their defaults, look here. Above it is EnvSource: environment variables, handy for temporary overrides in containers/CI without editing files. Highest is EtcdSource: config in etcd, for cluster-wide, runtime distribution and modification.

Multiple sources require deciding who wins. config.Manager (pkg/config/manager.go) merges the sources by priority: the source defines HighPriority=1, NormalPriority=11, LowPriority=21, noting "lesser value = higher priority". Intuitively, "the more personal and dynamic source wins": etcd (runtime) beats env vars, env vars beat file defaults. This "layered override" pattern is surely familiar — the same play as most mature config systems: give sensible defaults, then allow precise layered overrides, both out-of-the-box and tunable per environment. The three sources side by side:

Where config comes from = layered sources + priority merge: one knob (e.g. quota.timeout) may be set in several sources; config.Manager merges by priority — lesser value = higher priority, and the more personal/dynamic source wins: etcd (runtime) > env vars > milvus.yaml file > built-in default. Here etcd pushes 60s (highest priority, and it has a value), so it overrides yaml's 30s and the default 10s — effective value = 60s. Code reads only the typed value, never caring which layer it came from; mutable keys even hot-reload, no restart.
milvus.yaml
file default (low)
＜
env vars
deploy override (mid)
＜
etcd
runtime push (high)
→
config.Manager
merge by priority → ParamItem.GetAsInt()

Why is "the more personal and dynamic the source, the higher its priority" a sensible rule? Because it lines up with people's real intent when they adjust config. The file default is "the reasonable choice in most situations" — the most universal, and the one that most deserves to be overridden by special cases. An env var is "this particular deployment's need", more fitted to the moment than a generic default. And a runtime value in etcd is "a decision ops is pushing deliberately, right now", often to deal with a situation that's actively unfolding (temporarily tightening rate limits to ride out a traffic spike, say) — it's the most personal, and the one that most deserves to win. Designing priority as "dynamic beats static, specific beats general" is essentially making the system honor the later, more specific, more targeted decision. It also hands you a key for troubleshooting config: when something "was changed in the file but won't take effect", nine times out of ten it's being quietly overridden by a higher-priority source (an env var or etcd) — walk the priority chain from high to low and you'll usually catch the culprit.

SourceWhere fromTypical usePriority
FileSourceconfigs/milvus.yamldefaults + knob catalog (human-readable)low (fallback)
EnvSourceenvironment variablestemporary override in containers/CI, no file editsmedium
EtcdSourceconfig in etcdcluster-wide, runtime distribution/changeshigh (most personal)

Hot reload: some knobs turn while running

Finally, the move that best embodies "distributed-ops friendliness": hot reload. Often you just want to tweak a rate limit or log level, but not restart the whole cluster for it (a restart means jitter, even brief unavailability). Milvus's config system supports this: write the change into etcd, config.Manager senses it via its Dispatcher, callbacks notify the relevant ParamItem to refresh its lastValue — so the code's next GetAsInt() gets the new value, effective immediately, no restart.

1

Change the value in etcd

write a new value for a mutable config (rate limit, log level) into etcd.

2

Manager senses it

config.Manager observes the change via EtcdSource; the Dispatcher dispatches the event.

3

Callback refreshes ParamItem

the relevant ParamItem's callback fires, updating its lastValue.

4

Code reads the new value

the next GetAsInt() returns it, effective at once — Immutable items refuse this flow.

But not every knob may change this way. Some configs shouldn't vary at runtime (service ports, structures fixed once initialized), so ParamItem uses Immutable / Forbidden flags to lock them, refusing hot reload — a protection: better to forbid the change than let "changing something at runtime that shouldn't move" cause weird failures. Marking "mutable / immutable" explicitly on each knob is the mark of a good control panel: let you easily move what can move, lock outright what can't. Tying the lesson together: paramtable gives you a type-safe, documented, well-grouped read surface; config gives you a multi-source, priority-merged, hot-reloadable value surface; the two cooperate to keep "hundreds of knobs" in good order. Next lesson closes Part 9 — deployment: standalone vs cluster, its etcd/object-storage/messaging dependencies, and how to actually run the whole thing.

📌 Key points
  • Two layers: paramtable (read surface: type-safe/documented/grouped) + config (value surface: multi-source/priority/hot-reload); "how to use config" separated from "where it comes from".
  • ParamItem: one per knob, registering Key/DefaultValue/Doc and offering typed getters (GetAsInt/GetAsBool); code never parses strings — conversion funneled to one place.
  • Layered sources: FileSource (milvus.yaml default/catalog) < EnvSource (env vars) < EtcdSource (runtime distribution), merged by config.Manager by priority (lesser = higher).
  • Hot reload: mutable items refresh via etcd + Dispatcher callbacks, effective without restart; Immutable/Forbidden items are locked, refusing runtime changes for safety.

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. Why is Milvus's config split into two layers, paramtable and config?
  1. Because Go forbids reading files in one package
  2. Separate 'how to use config' (paramtable: a type-safe/documented/grouped read surface) from 'where config comes from' (config: a multi-source/priority/hot-reload value surface), making config behavior predictable and explainable
  3. Purely to add a layer and look complex
  4. paramtable handles CPU config, config handles GPU config
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. The upper paramtable offers a type-safe, documented, grouped 'read surface'; the lower config offers a multi-source, priority-merged, hot-reloadable 'value surface'. Decoupled, debugging 'why is this value / why didn't my change take effect' is clear: find the ParamItem's Key and default in paramtable, then check config for higher-priority overrides in yaml/env/etcd. A knob has one current value from one source — the determinism large-system config management fundamentally needs.
2. Code reads config via Params.XxxCfg.Yyy.GetAsInt() instead of parsing strings itself. The benefit?
  1. It funnels 'string→type' conversion inside ParamItem, avoiding scattered strconv and duplicated bugs and eliminating 'forgot/wrong conversion'; ParamItem also registers metadata like Key/Default/Doc
  2. GetAsInt automatically uploads config to etcd
  3. Values read this way are always 0
  4. It bypasses priority and reads the file directly
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: A. Each config item is a ParamItem (param_item.go) offering typed reads: GetValue/GetAsBool/GetAsInt/GetAsInt64. Consumers get correctly-typed values; conversion lives in one place inside ParamItem — no scattered strconv, no 'forgot/wrong conversion'. ParamItem also registers Key, DefaultValue, Doc, FallbackKeys, PanicIfEmpty: defaults give out-of-the-box use, Doc auto-generates docs, FallbackKeys keep old key names working, PanicIfEmpty fails fast at startup when a critical config is missing.
3. About config's multiple sources, priority, and hot reload, which is correct?
  1. File config always beats etcd because files are more 'official'
  2. Every config can be hot-reloaded freely at runtime, no exceptions
  3. Priority means a larger value wins
  4. Sources file(milvus.yaml) < env < etcd, merged by config.Manager by priority (lesser value wins; more dynamic/personal wins); mutable items hot-reload via etcd+Dispatcher callbacks, Immutable/Forbidden items are locked
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: D. The config package defines FileSource (milvus.yaml, defaults/catalog), EnvSource (env vars, temporary override), EtcdSource (runtime distribution). In source.go HighPriority=1env>file). config.Manager merges sources; mutable items written to etcd refresh a ParamItem's lastValue via Dispatcher callbacks, effective without restart. But items like ports that shouldn't change at runtime are locked via Immutable/Forbidden, refusing hot reload for safety.
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • This lesson likens config to a control panel that's 'labeled, prioritized, partly hot-turnable'. Please: (1) describe a config value's full journey from milvus.yaml/env/etcd to the code's GetAsInt(), and say who wins on conflict and why that design is reasonable; (2) you changed a rate-limit in milvus.yaml in production but it 'doesn't take effect' — using the priority chain, how would you debug? (3) Why doesn't Milvus make every config hot-reloadable, instead locking some via Immutable/Forbidden? What shared engineering value does this share with Lesson 37's 'GPU off by default'?

Deployment & operations

Lesson 8 told you what Milvus depends on (etcd, object storage, message queue) and what deployment shapes exist. As Part 9's finale, this lesson puts on the ops glasses: how you actually run it — from a one-command embedded single node to a full production Kubernetes cluster; and how Part 3's "disaggregated architecture" pays you back at deploy time with the elasticity of "scale exactly the part that's hot". This is the lesson that brings everything down to earth.

🔌 Analogy
Deploying a system is like choosing a place to live. Embedded single-node (standalone_embed.sh) is a fully-furnished studio: utilities all built in, move in instantly, ready in minutes — for one person (dev, trial). docker-compose is a serviced apartment with real separate utilities: etcd, storage, message queue are genuinely separate "lines", yet still one building (one machine). Kubernetes (Helm / Operator) is a whole managed apartment complex: each service lives in its own unit, you can add floors on demand (independent scaling), and a "property manager" (the Operator) handles scaling, fault replacement, and rolling upgrades for you. Which home you pick depends on whether you're "one person trying it out" or "carrying a city's worth of traffic".
🌍 Big picture
In one line: deployment is a ladder — embedded single-node (zero-dep, local) → docker-compose (local full) → k8s Helm Chart / Milvus Operator (production); in cluster mode components scale independently (scale reads via QueryNode, writes via streaming/DataNode); the message queue (rocksmq/Pulsar/Kafka/Woodpecker) is a key deployment decision — it's the WAL backend of Lessons 16/31. Config (Lesson 40), observability (Lesson 39), and version coordination (Lesson 37) all converge here.

From one command to a cluster: the deployment ladder

"How to run Milvus" has no single answer — it's a ladder from simple to elaborate, and which rung you stand on depends on what you're doing. The lowest rung is embedded single-node (scripts/standalone_embed.sh): it embeds the needed dependencies as much as possible — even etcd can be embedded (the script shows ETCD_USE_EMBED=true), messaging uses local rocksmq, data lands on local disk. The upside is nearly zero external dependencies — one command starts a complete Milvus — ideal for learning, local development, running a demo.

One rung up is docker-compose: bring up etcd, object storage (MinIO), and a message queue (e.g. Pulsar) as genuinely separate containers, plus Milvus itself. This rung lets you experience, on one machine, the real shape of "each dependency doing its job" — closer to production than embedded, simpler than k8s — good for local integration testing and small trials. The top rung is Kubernetes, the home turf of production: the project offers a Helm Chart (one-click templated deploy) and the Milvus Operator (an "ops brain" declaratively managing the whole cluster's lifecycle — scaling, self-healing, rolling upgrades). This ladder is considerate by design: zero burden to start, production-grade when serious; you can "climb rung by rung" as needs grow, rather than being scared off by complexity up front. The ladder, drawn:

From one command to a cluster = a deployment ladder: ① embedded single-node (standalone_embed.sh, embedded etcd + local rocksmq, one command, zero deps — learning/dev) → ② docker-compose (etcd/MinIO/Pulsar as separate containers — integration tests) → ③ k8s cluster (Helm one-click, Operator declarative ops — production). Zero burden to start, production-grade when serious. The real cluster payoff isn't "hold more data" but scaling on demand: add QueryNodes for read-heavy, DataNode/streaming for write-heavy, and object storage (stateless) scales on its own — add machines only where it's the bottleneck, spending where it counts.
1

Embedded single-node

standalone_embed.sh: embedded etcd + local rocksmq + local disk, one command, zero external deps. Learning/dev first choice.

2

docker-compose

etcd / MinIO / Pulsar as separate containers + Milvus; experience real division of labor on one machine. Integration tests/small trials.

3

Helm Chart

templated one-click deploy on k8s, tunable params, each component as Pods. Medium/large scale.

4

Milvus Operator

declarative "ops brain": scaling, self-healing, rolling upgrades all automatic. Production first choice.

The cluster payoff: scale independently, on demand

Why bother with cluster mode? Because it cashes in the core dividend of Part 3's "compute/storage separation, decoupled components" architecture: whichever part becomes the bottleneck, add machines to just that part, without scaling the whole. This is the ultimate purpose of why Milvus pulls "coordinators/worker nodes" and "read path/write path" so far apart.

A few concrete scenarios make it click. Workload is read-heavy, write-light (a typical search service)? Then add more QueryNodes so more nodes carry search traffic in parallel, while the write side stays untouched. Conversely, bulk-loading a huge dataset (offline import)? Then temporarily beef up write/streaming and index-building compute, leaving reads unaffected. Out of storage? Because data lives in object storage (stateless, independently scalable), adding capacity doesn't disturb compute nodes. This "on-demand, local, independent" scaling is what single-node can't give — there all roles crowd one process, and to scale you can only replicate the whole, wasteful and inflexible. So the real reason to choose cluster mode is often not "hold more data" but "spend money where it counts": scale precisely the one strained link. The two modes' trade-offs:

standalone

all roles in one process, deps embeddable. Simplest deploy, stable latency, but scaling means replicating the whole. For dev, small scale, low ops burden.

cluster

components as separate Pods, deps external (Pulsar/etcd/S3). Scale independently on demand (reads via QueryNode, writes via streaming/DataNode), stronger fault tolerance. Costlier to operate. Production choice.

Which message queue: a key deployment decision

When deploying Milvus, one decision deserves its own spotlight: which message queue (MQ). Recall Lessons 16 and 31 — Milvus writes by "logging first (WAL)", and this log's backend is exactly the MQ here. In configs/milvus.yaml, mq.type supports four: rocksmq (RocksDB-based, local embedded), Pulsar, Kafka, Woodpecker.

How to choose? The project gives clear defaults and advice. Standalone defaults to rocksmq — local, lightweight, zero external deployment, most carefree; cluster defaults to Pulsar (note: rocksmq isn't supported in cluster mode, being local and unshareable across nodes). Kafka is another mature cluster option. For new deployments, the project explicitly recommends Woodpecker — Milvus's own next-gen log storage, aiming at "better performance, simpler ops, lower cost". This choice is "key" because it directly determines what your write path stands on: pick local rocksmq and you're single-node only; for a cluster with high-throughput writes you need a distributed log like Pulsar/Kafka/Woodpecker. Memorize this table and you won't trip on the MQ at deploy time.

MQ typeWhat it isForDefault/advice
rocksmqRocksDB-based local embedded logstandalone onlystandalone default
Pulsarmature distributed messaging systemclustercluster default
Kafkamature distributed messaging systemclustercluster option
WoodpeckerMilvus's own next-gen log storagestandalone/clusterrecommended for new

Ops trio as one: config, observability, smooth upgrades

Tie Part 9 together and you'll see these lessons are different facets of one thing — how to make Milvus, in production, run stable, be seen, be tunable, upgrade smoothly. Tunable, via Lesson 40's config system: push via etcd, hot-reload rate limits and log levels, adapting to traffic without restart. Seen, via Lesson 39's observability: logs, metrics, traces three-pronged, tracing that one request out of the whole cluster when something breaks. Smooth upgrades, via Lesson 37's index-engine version coordination (take the minimum version across nodes) plus the Operator's rolling upgrade: replace node by node, old and new coexisting without interrupting service.

computeMilvus roles (scale independently in cluster mode)
Proxy / coordinators / QueryNode (reads) / streaming·DataNode (writes)
dependenciesthree external pillars
etcd (consistency/metadata) · object storage (durability) · MQ (ordered log = WAL backend)
deploy hostfrom one process to a cluster
embedded single-node / docker-compose / k8s Pods (Helm · Operator)

This is the full picture of truly operating a distributed vector database: deployment decides what shape it grows into, dependencies shoulder the hardest distributed problems for it (consistency to etcd, durability to object storage, ordered logging to the MQ), independent scaling lets you pour compute precisely at the bottleneck, and config + observability let you see and adjust it without downtime. Looking back over all of Part 9 — from the API contract, to observability, to config, to deployment — we've actually walked "the entire outer ring a user/operator really touches". By now you not only understand how Milvus works inside, but how to use and run it. Next (Part 10), the final leg: practice and contributing — how to build and run, how to test, what code conventions exist, and how to submit your first PR to Milvus.

📌 Key points
  • Deployment ladder: embedded single-node (standalone_embed.sh, zero-dep) → docker-compose (local full) → Helm Chart → Milvus Operator (declarative production ops); climb on demand.
  • Cluster payoff: cashes the compute/storage-separation dividend — scale independently on demand (reads via QueryNode, writes via streaming/DataNode, storage independently in object store), pouring compute precisely at the bottleneck.
  • MQ is a key deployment decision: it's the WAL backend — rocksmq (standalone-only/default), Pulsar (cluster default), Kafka (cluster option), Woodpecker (recommended for new); chosen via mq.type.
  • Ops convergence: config (40, hot reload), observability (39, trace one request), version coordination + rolling upgrade (37/Operator) together make Milvus run stable, be seen, be tunable, upgrade smoothly.

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. Milvus's deployment options form a ladder from embedded single-node to the k8s Operator. What's the point of this design?
  1. Embedded single-node is for production, the Operator for development
  2. The options have totally different, incompatible features
  3. Zero burden to start (standalone_embed: one command, zero deps) and production-grade when serious (Helm/Operator); climb rung by rung as needs grow, not scared off by complexity
  4. Because Milvus only runs on k8s
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: C. The ladder runs simple to elaborate: embedded single-node (standalone_embed.sh — embedded etcd + local rocksmq + local disk, near-zero external deps) for learning/dev; docker-compose brings etcd/MinIO/Pulsar up as separate containers for real division of labor on one machine, for integration tests; k8s Helm Chart templates the deploy and the Milvus Operator declaratively manages the full lifecycle (scaling/self-healing/rolling upgrade) for production. You pick the rung you need rather than shouldering all complexity up front.
2. What's the core payoff of choosing cluster mode (over standalone)?
  1. Cluster mode always has lower latency than standalone
  2. It cashes the compute/storage-separation dividend — scale independently on demand: add QueryNodes for reads, streaming/DataNodes for writes, storage scales independently in the object store — pouring compute precisely at the bottleneck
  3. Cluster mode needs no external dependencies
  4. Cluster mode crams all components into one process
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. Cluster mode cashes Part 3's 'compute/storage separation, decoupled components' dividend: add machines to whichever link is the bottleneck. Read-heavy → more QueryNodes; bulk loading → temporarily beef up write/streaming and index building; out of storage → scale the object store (stateless, doesn't disturb compute). Standalone crowds all roles in one process, so scaling means replicating the whole — wasteful and inflexible. The real reason for cluster is usually not 'hold more data' but 'spend where it counts'. The cost is operating external deps and k8s — choose by real need.
3. About Milvus's message queue (MQ) choice, which is correct?
  1. The MQ is the WAL backend: rocksmq standalone-only (default), Pulsar cluster default, Kafka cluster option, Woodpecker recommended for new; chosen via mq.type — the backend's throughput/reliability bounds write capacity
  2. All four MQs are equivalent; pick any, no difference
  3. rocksmq is the default and recommended in cluster mode
  4. The MQ is just an optional cache; removing it doesn't affect writes
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: A. Recall Lessons 16/31: Milvus writes by logging to the WAL first, and that log's backend is the MQ. mq.type supports rocksmq (RocksDB-based, local embedded — standalone only, standalone default), Pulsar (cluster default; rocksmq isn't supported in cluster, being local and unshareable), Kafka (cluster option), Woodpecker (Milvus's own, recommended for new, for better performance/simpler ops/lower cost). Making the log backend pluggable reflects 'program to an interface, swap the implementation' — choosing the MQ is choosing the foundation for the whole write path.
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • This lesson likens deployment to 'choosing a home' and says cluster mode cashes the compute/storage-separation dividend. Please: (1) recommend a deployment method for each of three scenarios (local learning / a small team's steady-traffic production / a large-scale search service with surging read traffic) and justify each; (2) explain why 'going cluster' isn't always better — it brings elasticity, but at what cost? (3) Tying to Lessons 16/31, explain why the MQ is a 'key deployment decision': making the log backend pluggable (rocksmq/Pulsar/Woodpecker) reflects which recurring design philosophy of this guide (relate to Lesson 22 Knowhere, Lesson 39 Prometheus/OTel)?

Build & run

Across the first nine parts you've seen Milvus inside out. The final part (Part 10) is hands-on: how to build and run it from source, how to test, what conventions to follow, and how to submit your first PR. First, the most practical gate — build and run. A guide-wide thread matters here: because Milvus is Go + C++ glued by cgo (Lessons 2, 34), its build is inherently two-stage — and that fact decides almost every "why" of compiling it.

🔌 Analogy
Building a Go+C++ project is like assembling a device with both a metal chassis and an electronics board. The C++ core is the metal chassis: first into the "machine shop" (the cmake + conan C++ toolchain) to be cast and machined. The Go part is the electronics board: made with its own toolchain, then bolted onto the chassis (that bolting is cgo). The order can't be reversed: without a chassis, the board has nowhere to mount. So you can't just go build — that's "making the board with no chassis", bolting into thin air. You must forge the C++ chassis first, then Go can cgo-link onto it. make milvus is the master assembler who runs the two stages in the right order.
🌍 Big picture
In one line: make milvus = first build-cpp (cmake/conan compiles the C++ core, including generated-proto) then build-go (a CGO_ENABLED=1 go build links the C++ libs, compiling cmd/main.go); the product is one milvus binary that can play any role; deps install via scripts/install_deps.sh, run via scripts/start_standalone.sh / start_cluster.sh. Two stages, born of the Go+C++ nature.

Why you can't just go build: the two-stage build

Someone trying to build Milvus for the first time often reflexively types go build — and hits a wall. The reason is exactly what Lessons 2 and 34 kept stressing: Milvus's compute core is C++, and the Go part must cgo-link to those C++ libs to run. So a lone go build can't find the not-yet-compiled C++ libs and must fail. The right move is make milvus, defined in the Makefile as an ordered pipeline: milvus: build-cpp print-build-info build-go — C++ first, Go second, the order hard-wired.

Unpack the two stages. Stage one, build-cpp: uses cmake + conan (C++ build and package tools) to compile the whole C++ core under internal/core (segment engine, index, exec, mmap… all of Part 8), also depending on generated-proto (generate the proto first). This stage yields a set of C++ static/dynamic libs. Stage two, build-go: runs go build with CGO_ENABLED=1, linking in the previous stage's C++ libs via CGO_LDFLAGS / CGO_CFLAGS, finally compiling the entrypoint cmd/main.go. The two run back-to-back, meshed at the seam by cgo, neither dispensable. This is why building Milvus needs two toolchains: Go's, plus C++'s cmake/conan — whereas a pure-Go project needs only the former. The two stages compared:

Stage 1: build-cpp (C++ chassis)

cmake + conan compile internal/core (segment engine/index/exec/mmap), depending on generated-proto. Yields C++ libs. A pure-Go project has no such step.

Stage 2: build-go (Go board)

CGO_ENABLED=1 go build, linking the stage-1 C++ libs via CGO_LDFLAGS/CFLAGS, compiling cmd/main.go. cgo meshes the two stages here.

Many first-timers stall on stage one — the C++ part — because it must pull and compile a heap of C++ dependencies (FAISS/Knowhere, assorted math and storage libs), which is slow and fussy about the environment. This is exactly where conan earns its keep: it's the C++ world's package manager, pulling those third-party libs at the right versions, building them, and managing them so you needn't hand-run make install on each. cmake is the C++ build orchestrator: it organizes hundreds or thousands of source files by their dependencies and drives the compiler to produce the libs. Knowing this split gives you direction when a build error strikes: is it a dependency not pulled correctly (conan's job), or a compile/link misconfiguration (cmake's job)?

One command, one binary: make milvus

With deps installed and the two stages chained, what you finally get is one executable named milvus — note, one, not a dozen. This is intriguing: Milvus clearly has a dozen roles (Proxy, coordinators, various nodes), yet compiles to a single binary. The secret is the entrypoint cmd/main.go: the same program decides "which role to play this time" by launch arguments. To start a Proxy, launch it in the "proxy" role; for a QueryNode, the "querynode" role.

milvus binary
the one product of a single build
--role→
play one role
proxy / coordinator / querynode / datanode / streamingnode

This "one binary, many roles" design has real benefits. Simple builds: compile once, deploy everywhere, no per-role packaging. Version consistency: all roles are inherently same-source, same-version, ruling out "Proxy new, QueryNode old" mismatches. Flexible deployment: in standalone mode one process can bring up all roles together (a key reason Lesson 41's "embedded single-node" can be so light); in cluster mode the same binary is launched in different roles on different machines. The full build flow, from installing deps to producing the binary, as one line:

1

Install deps

scripts/install_deps.sh: cmake, conan (C++), the Go toolchain, all at once.

2

Generate proto

make generated-proto: generate per-language/per-module code from milvus-proto.

3

Compile the C++ core

build-cpp: cmake/conan compile internal/core, yielding C++ libs.

4

Compile Go & cgo-link

build-go: CGO links the C++ libs, compiling the single milvus binary (cmd/main.go).

A first full Milvus build often takes a good while (that pile of C++ deps plus the core can run to tens of minutes on the first pass) — that's normal, don't assume it's stuck. The good news is incremental builds are far faster: change Go and you only rerun build-go; only a C++ change forces recompiling the relevant C++ part. A practical tip: when your day-to-day edits are Go-only, don't make milvus from scratch every time — if the C++ libs are unchanged, a single Go relink suffices and saves a lot of waiting. Because the first build is heavy, the community ships prebuilt docker images (what Lesson 41's compose/Helm pull): most people who only want to use Milvus never compile it — just pull and run. You truly need this from-source path only when you intend to change its code and contribute. That marks Part 10's quiet shift in identity — from here on you move from user to contributor, and building from source is the first threshold to changing code, running tests, and opening a PR.

Run it: standalone and cluster scripts

Binary built, how to run it? Milvus ships a set of launch scripts under scripts/, matching Lesson 41's deployment shapes. The easiest is scripts/standalone_embed.sh: embedded single-node, even etcd embedded, messaging via local rocksmq, near-zero external deps — one command starts a complete Milvus, ideal for local learning and development. For something closer to the real shape, use scripts/start_standalone.sh (standalone, but deps started separately) or scripts/start_cluster.sh (cluster, components launched separately). To stop, use scripts/stop_graceful.sh — note "graceful", letting in-flight writes/flushes finish rather than brutally killing the process.

CommandWhat it does
scripts/install_deps.shinstall build deps at once (cmake/conan/Go, etc.)
make generated-protoregenerate code from milvus-proto
make milvustwo-stage build of the CPU milvus binary
make milvus-gpubuild the GPU variant (Lesson 37, links CUDA/RAFT)
standalone_embed.shstart a zero-dep embedded single-node (learning/dev)
start_standalone.sh / start_cluster.shstart standalone (separate deps) / start a cluster
stop_graceful.shgraceful stop, letting writes/flushes finish

A word on why "graceful stop" deserves its own script rather than a plain kill. While Milvus runs, memory often holds data not yet flushed (growing segments, unpersisted WAL consume progress, Lesson 17). Brutally killing the process may leave this in-flight state without a clean finish, so a restart must recover via log replay — slower and riskier. A graceful stop first tells components to "prepare to close": flush what should be flushed, record checkpoints, settle in-flight requests, then exit. This is the same production care as Lesson 41's Operator rolling upgrade — how a system shuts down matters as much as how it starts; careless shutdowns are a frequent source of weird data problems. Building the habit of "stop_graceful.sh, not kill" is a small step from toy mindset to production mindset.

To wrap up: Milvus's build is "not just go build" at root because of its Go + C++ bilingual nature — you must forge the C++ core chassis first, then bolt Go on via cgo; make milvus orchestrates both stages into one binary that plays all roles, which launch scripts then run in the shape you want. Grasp this chain and you not only can build Milvus but truly understand the cost and reward of the "Go+C++ bilingual architecture" in engineering terms — it buys peak performance and demands this extra build step. Next lesson: how to write tests for this bilingual system (especially the special rule of "must carry -tags dynamic,test").

📌 Key points
  • Two-stage build: because Go+C++ are glued by cgo, make milvus = build-cpp (cmake/conan compile the C++ core + generated-proto) → build-go (CGO_ENABLED=1 links the C++ libs); you can't just go build.
  • Two toolchains: besides Go you need C++'s cmake/conan, installed at once via scripts/install_deps.sh.
  • One binary, many roles: the product is a single milvus (cmd/main.go) that plays Proxy/coordinator/nodes by launch args; simple builds, version consistency, flexible deployment.
  • Run: launch via standalone_embed.sh (zero-dep learning) / start_standalone.sh / start_cluster.sh; stop gracefully via stop_graceful.sh.

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. Why can't you build Milvus with just go build — why use make milvus?
  1. Because go build is unavailable on Linux
  2. make milvus is just an alias of go build, no difference
  3. Because Milvus is pure C++ with no Go
  4. Milvus is Go+C++ glued by cgo: you must build-cpp first (cmake/conan compile the C++ core into libs), then build-go (CGO_ENABLED=1) links them; a lone go build can't find the not-yet-built C++ libs and fails
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: D. Milvus's compute core is C++, and the Go part must cgo-link those C++ libs to run. The Makefile defines milvus: build-cpp print-build-info build-go — order hard-wired, C++ first, Go second. build-cpp uses cmake/conan to compile internal/core (also needing generated-proto) into C++ libs; build-go runs with CGO_ENABLED=1, linking them via CGO_LDFLAGS/CFLAGS to compile cmd/main.go. A lone go build fails because those libs don't exist yet. Hence two toolchains (Go + C++'s cmake/conan).
2. What form does make milvus produce?
  1. One milvus binary (cmd/main.go) that plays any role — Proxy/coordinator/nodes — by launch arguments; simple builds, version consistency, flexible deployment
  2. A binary that can only act as a Proxy
  3. A dozen binaries, one per role, each needing version sync
  4. A set of Python scripts
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: A. Though Milvus has a dozen roles (Proxy, coordinators, nodes), it compiles to one milvus binary. The secret is cmd/main.go: the same program picks its role this run by launch args (proxy/querynode…). Benefits: compile once deploy everywhere (simple builds), all roles same-source/same-version (no mismatch), one process brings up all roles in standalone while the same binary launches in different roles across machines in cluster (flexible). That's 'one binary + role dispatch'.
3. Which statement about Milvus build/run commands is correct?
  1. GPU and CPU builds use the same make target
  2. You should kill the process directly — safer than a graceful stop
  3. install_deps.sh installs deps; make generated-proto generates proto; make milvus(-gpu) builds the binary; standalone_embed.sh/start_*.sh launch; stop_graceful.sh stops gracefully, letting writes/flushes finish
  4. make generated-proto must run after make milvus
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: C. Full chain: install_deps.sh installs cmake/conan/Go at once; make generated-proto generates code from milvus-proto (must precede compilation since C++/Go both include/import these types); make milvus (CPU)/make milvus-gpu (GPU, links CUDA/RAFT) two-stage build the binary; launch via standalone_embed.sh (zero-dep)/start_standalone.sh/start_cluster.sh; stop_graceful.sh stops gracefully — flush, checkpoint, settle in-flight requests, then exit. A careless kill may lose unflushed state, forcing replay recovery on restart.
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • This lesson says Milvus's build is 'not just go build', rooted in its Go+C++ bilingual nature. Please: (1) in your own words explain what make milvus's two stages (build-cpp / build-go) each do, where cgo meshes, and why the order can't reverse; (2) why does building Milvus need the cmake/conan C++ toolchain while a pure-Go project doesn't? (3) what does 'one binary, many roles (cmd/main.go)' buy for builds, version management, and standalone/cluster deployment? Tying to Lessons 2 and 34, discuss how the 'Go+C++ bilingual architecture' trades off performance against build complexity.

Testing

You can build Milvus; next is to write and run its tests. But you'll soon hit something odd: a plain go test ./... won't run. The reason is that same thread — Milvus is Go + C++, and it uses a "patch a function at runtime" mock tool, so its test command must carry a string of seemingly cryptic flags: -tags dynamic,test and -gcflags="all=-N -l". This lesson unpacks that "incantation" word by word, so you see which pitfall each flag guards against.

🔌 Analogy
Running tests in Milvus is like entering a lab with special protocols. You can't just stroll in like a park (go test won't run); you must don specific gear (those flags): a "dynamic,test pass" (telling the compiler: link C++ dynamically, include test-only code), plus an iron rule — "turn off auto-optimization" (-N -l) so the "inspectors" (the mockey tool) can swap a part for a fake mid-experiment. Miss one piece of gear and the experiment won't even start, throwing baffling errors. Many new contributors stall on their first test run precisely because they forgot this gear. Remember it and you've cleared the first hurdle of Milvus testing.
🌍 Big picture
In one line: Milvus's Go tests must carry -tags dynamic,test (dynamic-link C++ + test code) and -gcflags="all=-N -l" (disable optimization/inlining so bytedance/mockey can patch at runtime); two kinds of mocks — mockery (generated interface fakes; don't hand-edit, regenerate via make generate-mockery-*) and mockey (runtime function patching); run tests via make test-go or module shortcuts like make test-proxy, which first build the C++ with unit tests.

Why you can't just go test: that string of cryptic flags

First, the "incantation". To test one package, Milvus's real command looks like this (from scripts/run_go_unittest.sh): go test -gcflags="all=-N -l" -race -cover -tags dynamic,test ".../proxy/..." -failfast -count=1 -ldflags="-r ${RPATH}". A mouthful, but every piece is purposeful, not piled on at random. Let's unpack each.

-tags dynamic,test is two build tags: dynamic makes Go dynamically link the C++ core libs (cgo's need, continuous with Lesson 42's build); test enables code paths compiled in only for tests (helpers, stubs). -race turns on the race detector — Milvus is full of concurrency, and this catches data races during tests. -cover collects coverage. -failfast stops at once on the first failing case; -count=1 forces no test caching, really running each time (Go caches passing results by default). -ldflags="-r ${RPATH}" bakes in the runtime library search path so the test program can find those C++ dynamic libs at runtime. The most crucial and most overlooked is -gcflags="all=-N -l" — it deserves its own section. The table shows the whole string at once.

FlagWhat it does
-tags dynamic,testdynamic=dynamically link the C++ core (cgo); test=enable test-only code
-gcflags="all=-N -l"disable optimization (-N) and inlining (-l) so mockey can patch functions at runtime
-racerace detector: catch concurrent data races
-covercollect test coverage
-failfast -count=1stop at first failure; no test caching, run for real each time
-ldflags="-r ${RPATH}"runtime library search path so tests find the C++ dynamic libs

The -gcflags trick: so mockey can patch

In this string, -gcflags="all=-N -l" most puzzles newcomers: disable the compiler's optimization and inlining — what for? The answer lies in a special tool Milvus tests use — mockey (ByteDance's open-source bytedance/mockey). It does a "black magic": at runtime, swap a function's implementation for a fake you specify (the industry calls it monkey-patching). This is hugely useful in tests — to test "how this function handles a downstream RPC failing", you needn't actually set up a failing downstream; just use mockey to temporarily replace that RPC function with "return an error".

But this "swap implementation" is surgery at the machine-code level: mockey must find the function's entry and redirect it to the fake. The catch — if the compiler inlined it (splicing a small function's code into the call site so its entry "vanishes") or aggressively optimized, mockey can't find the entry and the patch won't land, so tests either fail to compile or panic at runtime. -N (no optimization) and -l (no inlining) exist exactly for this: they keep every function honestly holding a replaceable entry for mockey to grab. So this flag isn't optional tuning but a prerequisite for mockey to work — the root reason Milvus's testing conventions keep stressing "must carry -gcflags="all=-N -l"". Forget it and your mockey tests fail in baffling ways, often with errors that don't point at the real cause.

Two kinds of mocks: mockery vs mockey

The mockey above is just one of two mocking approaches in Milvus tests. Their names look alike but they're completely different things, easily confused early on, so let's settle it. mockery (vektra/mockery) is compile-time: it reads a Go interface and auto-generates a "fake object" implementing it (you set "return this when this method is called"). These generated files live in internal/mocks or various mock_*.go. Key discipline: these are generated — don't hand-edit — change the interface and regenerate via make generate-mockery-{module} (Part 8's "don't hand-edit generated files").

mockey (bytedance/mockey) is runtime: not interface-based, it directly replaces a concrete function's implementation at runtime (the monkey-patch needing -N -l from the last section). Each shines in its place: when the code under test is interface-oriented (depends on, say, a Coordinator interface), injecting a mockery fake is most natural; when you must replace a concrete function/method (especially package-level functions or hard-to-inject deps), mockey's runtime patch is more flexible. Grasp "interfaces use mockery, functions use mockey" and these two names won't tangle you in Milvus tests. Side by side:

mockery (compile-time / interface)

vektra/mockery reads interfaces and generates fakes (internal/mocks, mock_*.go). Don't hand-edit; after changing the interface, regenerate via make generate-mockery-*. For interface-oriented dependency injection.

mockey (runtime / function)

bytedance/mockey patches concrete functions at runtime (monkey-patch), swapping implementations. Needs -gcflags="all=-N -l". For replacing package-level functions and hard-to-inject deps.

How to run: test-go and module shortcuts

Knowing the flags and mocks, actually running tests is simple — don't type that long string; use the Makefile-wrapped targets. Run all Go unit tests: make test-go (it first runs build-cpp-with-unittest to build the C++ with unit tests, then calls scripts/run_go_unittest.sh with the right flags). Just one module? There's a pile of shortcuts: make test-proxy, make test-querycoord, make test-datacoord, make test-rootcoord, make test-querynode, make test-datanode… The C++ unit tests run via make test-cpp.

1

Build C++ with unit tests first

make test-go first runs build-cpp-with-unittest — Go tests must cgo-link C++.

2

Run go test with the full flags

run_go_unittest.sh runs per package with -tags dynamic,test -gcflags="all=-N -l" -race ....

3

Mocks in place

interfaces injected via mockery-generated fakes; concrete functions patched at runtime by mockey (hence -N -l).

4

Collect results

-race reports races, -cover gives coverage, -failfast stops at first error; CI uses this to gate the PR.

During development, to run just one test function, you can use go test directly, but not one of those flags may be missing: go test -tags dynamic,test -gcflags="all=-N -l" ./internal/proxy/... -run TestXxx. Committing this to muscle memory is daily life writing Milvus code. To wrap up: the "specialness" of testing Milvus all stems from its Go+C++ + runtime-mock nature — dynamic,test for cgo and test code, -N -l for mockey's patching; two mock kinds cover interfaces and functions; the Makefile wraps this complexity into one-command make test-*. Next lesson: the conventions to follow when writing code — error handling (merr), generated files, import order — the invisible bar your code must clear to be merged.

❌ go test ./...

missing the flag string: can't find the not-yet-built C++ libs (dynamic), mockey can't patch (no -N -l) → compile failure or baffling panic.

✅ go test -tags dynamic,test -gcflags="all=-N -l"

dynamic-link C++ + enable test code + keep replaceable entries → mockery/mockey in place, tests run normally. make test-* carries them all.

📌 Key points
  • Test incantation: Go tests must carry -tags dynamic,test (dynamic-link C++ + test code) and -gcflags="all=-N -l" (disable optimization/inlining); a plain go test won't run.
  • Why -gcflags is required: -N -l keep functions with a replaceable entry so mockey's runtime patching works; forget it and mockey tests fail bafflingly.
  • Two mocks: mockery (compile-time generated interface fakes, don't hand-edit, regenerate via make generate-mockery-*) vs mockey (runtime patching of concrete functions).
  • How to run: make test-go (all, first builds C++ with unit tests) or make test-proxy/test-querycoord/… module shortcuts; C++ via make test-cpp.

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. Why must Milvus's Go unit tests carry -tags dynamic,test and -gcflags="all=-N -l"?
  1. Because Go requires these on Linux
  2. To make tests run faster
  3. dynamic,test makes Go dynamically link the C++ core and enable test code (needed by this cgo project); -N -l disables optimization/inlining so functions keep a replaceable entry, letting bytedance/mockey patch at runtime
  4. Pure legacy with no real effect
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: C. Milvus is Go+C++: dynamic dynamically links the C++ core (cgo), test enables test-only code — miss them and it won't compile. -gcflags="all=-N -l" disables optimization (-N) and inlining (-l): mockey is a machine-code 'monkey-patch' redirecting a function to a fake; if a function is inlined/optimized its entry vanishes, the patch can't land, and tests fail to compile or panic. So -N -l isn't tuning but a prerequisite for mockey — exactly why conventions keep stressing it.
2. What's the difference between mockery and mockey in Milvus tests?
  1. Both require hand-editing the generated mock files
  2. mockery (vektra) compile-time generates interface fakes (internal/mocks; don't hand-edit, regenerate via make generate-mockery-*); mockey (bytedance) patches concrete functions at runtime — interfaces use the former, functions the latter
  3. They're the same tool spelled differently
  4. mockery is runtime, mockey is compile-time
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. Alike in name, opposite in nature. mockery (vektra/mockery) is compile-time: reads an interface and generates a fake implementing it (files in internal/mocks, mock_*.go) — a generated artifact; don't hand-edit, change the interface then regenerate via make generate-mockery-{module} (like proto .pb.go 'don't hand-edit'). mockey (bytedance/mockey) is runtime: not interface-based, it replaces a concrete function's implementation (needs -N -l). Rule: inject interface deps with mockery; replace concrete/package-level functions with mockey.
3. Which statement about running Milvus tests is correct?
  1. Go tests don't need to build C++ first
  2. Use make test-go (first build-cpp-with-unittest then run with the right flags) or module shortcuts like make test-proxy/test-querycoord; C++ via make test-cpp; even a single test function needs the full flag string
  3. make test-go skips the race detector
  4. A plain go test ./... suffices, no special flags
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. make test-go first runs build-cpp-with-unittest (Go tests cgo-link C++), then run_go_unittest.sh runs per package with -tags dynamic,test -gcflags="all=-N -l" -race -cover -failfast -count=1 -ldflags="-r RPATH". Module shortcuts: make test-proxy/test-querycoord/test-datacoord/test-rootcoord/test-querynode/test-datanode; C++ via make test-cpp. A plain go test fails for missing flags (can't find C++ libs, mockey breaks); running one function still needs the full flags plus -run TestXxx.
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • This lesson likens the test flags to 'lab gear' and says testing's 'specialness' distills Milvus's two genes (bilingual and concurrent). Please: (1) map -tags dynamic,test / -gcflags="all=-N -l" / -race each to 'why it's needed', especially the -N -l ↔ mockey causation; (2) for two scenarios choose mockery or mockey and justify: A) the function under test depends on a Coordinator interface; B) it internally calls a package-level helper that can't be injected; (3) why must mock files (internal/mocks) not be hand-edited? Is this the same discipline as Part 8's generated proto files?

Code conventions

You can build and test; one gate remains before "your code can be merged" — conventions. Every mature project has coding rules; Milvus especially cares about a few: handle errors with merr (and tell "whose fault" it is), log only via mlog, order imports a fixed way, don't hand-edit generated files. Most are enforced by linters — violate them and CI rejects your PR. This lesson walks you through these "invisible bars", the most Milvus-flavored being merr's "Input vs System" error philosophy.

🔌 Analogy
A team's code conventions are like a big workshop's shared protocol. The most important rule is, when something breaks, tell "whose fault" it is: did the customer bring the wrong materials (an Input error — e.g. searching a nonexistent collection), or did our own machine malfunction (a System error — e.g. a node not yet ready)?
