The two are handled oppositely: the former you honestly tell the customer "this is on you" and don't blindly retry; the latter you retry automatically and alarm, since it's usually temporary. Beyond "whose fault", the workshop has tidiness rules: which slot tools go in (import order), which official instrument to log with (mlog only), and don't manually repaint machine-stamped parts (don't hand-edit generated files). At the door a foreman (the linter) checks each item — non-compliant parts never enter the shop.
🌍 Big picture
In one line: errors use merr (pkg/util/merr) not fmt.Errorf, split into Input (the request itself is wrong, not retriable) vs System (Milvus's own/transient failure, stays retriable); logging only via mlog (pkg/v3/log, pingcap/log, raw zap are linter-forbidden); imports ordered "stdlib → third-party → github.com/milvus-io" enforced by gci; generated files (mocks/proto) must not be hand-edited. Most conventions are machine-checked by golangci-lint/gci.

The signature convention: handle errors with merr (Input vs System)

Milvus's most distinctive and first-to-master convention is error handling. Rule one: don't build errors with fmt.Errorf; use merr (pkg/util/merr). In merr, every error carries an error code and a retriable attribute, defined in errors.go like newMilvusError("service not ready", 1, true) — note that trailing true, meaning "this error may be retried". Create errors with factory functions like WrapErrServiceNotReady(...), WrapErrCollectionNotFound(...), not hand-spliced strings.

But merr's real soul is forcing you to answer: whose fault is this error? Milvus splits errors into two classes. Input errors: caused by the request content itself — searching a nonexistent collection, passing invalid params. These are the user's fault, and the right move is to return faithfully, don't retry (retry ten thousand times, the collection still doesn't exist). System errors: Milvus's own problem or a transient failure — a node not yet ready, an internal momentary race. These are not the user's fault and often clear up shortly, so they should stay retriable. The key test isn't "does it look like validation" but the blame test: did the request content itself force this branch, or an internal/transient failure? The two, clarified:

Input error (blame the request)

caused by the request itself: searching a missing collection, invalid params. Return to the user, not retriable (retry won't help). At the proxy boundary, often marked via WrapErrAsInputError(When).

System error (blame system/transient)

a Milvus bug or transient failure: node not ready, internal momentary race. Stays retriable (may clear up soon), should alarm. The default classification for many merr errors.

Why this distinction matters: it decides "to retry or not"

You might ask: why split so finely? Because — an error's classification directly decides the system's retry behavior, and getting it slightly wrong causes big problems. Many places inside Milvus auto-retry failed operations (typically calls wrapped in retry.Do). If a transient error that should be retried (System, e.g. "node not ready") is mislabeled Input and becomes non-retriable, then an operation that would have succeeded after a brief wait gets condemned on the spot and fails outright — a false alarm you turned real with your own hands.

The reverse is worse: if a doomed Input error (e.g. "collection doesn't exist") is mislabeled a retriable System error, the system will foolishly retry over and over an operation that can never succeed, burning resources and possibly dragging down the caller. So merr has careful designs: ErrCollectionNotFound defaults to a System error (because internal paths in datacoord etc. need to treat "temporarily not found" as retriable during recovery/retry), and only at the user-facing proxy boundary is it "flipped" via WrapErrAsInputErrorWhen into an Input error for the user. This subtlety — "the same error is retriable internally, classified as user error only at the boundary" — is the essence of merr. Two companion iron rules to remember: to add context to an existing error, use only merr.Wrap/Wrapf (not WrapErrXxxErr(err,…), which masks the inner code); before turning an error Input, grep whether any retry.Do depends on its retriability — mislabeling one classification can quietly break a retry chain.

an error occurs
a branch returns err
blame test
whose fault?
request forces it=Input; internal/transient=System
decides
retry.Do
System→retry (may succeed); Input→return to user

Here's a useful habit when reading merr code: watch the boolean (retriable or not) and the numeric code at the end of each definition in errors.go. The retriable flag tells you which class an error is "born into" — true is usually System (transient, worth retrying), false usually a deterministic failure. The numeric codes are segmented into ranges: errors of similar responsibility sit in nearby code ranges (you'll see comments marking which range belongs to which family). This isn't decoration — when you add a new error you must pick a code from the matching family's range, never an arbitrary number, or you break the implicit "code → category" contract and risk colliding with the C++ segcore code ranges. So the right flow for adding an error is: grep errors.go to read the existing ranges, find the family your error belongs to, take an unused code within that range, then set its retriable flag per Input/System.

Other hard conventions: logging, import order, generated files

Beyond merr, a few unglamorous conventions can also block your PR — luckily most are linter-checked, so you needn't memorize them; violations are flagged on the spot. Log only via mlog: as Lesson 39 covered, Milvus's .golangci.yml uses depguard to explicitly forbid pkg/v3/log, pingcap/log, raw zap — all must become pkg/v3/mlog, every log carrying ctx. Import order: gci enforces three sections — stdlib → third-party → github.com/milvus-io, this project's packages always last; make has a gci fix to sort them automatically. Don't hand-edit generated files: mockery-generated mocks (Lesson 43) and proto-generated .pb.go must be changed at the "source" and regenerated, not at the artifact. The conventions, with "who enforces", as a cheatsheet:

ConventionRuleEnforced by
error handlinguse merr factories/Wrap, split Input/System, no fmt.Errorfreview + merr guard tests
loggingonly mlog (with ctx), no log/zap/fmt.Printlngolangci-lint (depguard)
import orderstdlib → third-party → github.com/milvus-iogci
generated filesmocks/.pb.go: change source and regenerate, don't hand-editreview + no-diff regen
configuse paramtable, no raw file/env reads (Lesson 40)review

Mostly machine-enforced: let CI check for you

Tie these conventions together and you'll find a reassuring fact: most don't rely on your memory but on tools to enforce. gci sorts your imports, golangci-lint catches "used a forbidden logging library" and "obvious bad smells", merr's guard tests defend the error-code contract, and CI runs the full make test-*. So the smart move before opening a PR is to run this self-check locally first — format imports, pass the linter, run the relevant module's tests — killing problems before pushing, rather than ping-ponging after CI turns red.

1

Sort imports

gci fix auto-arranges imports into "stdlib→third-party→milvus-io".

2

Pass the linter

golangci-lint: catch forbidden log libs, bad smells; depguard enforces "mlog only".

3

Run relevant tests

make test-proxy etc.: incl. merr guard tests, race detection — confirm no contract broken.

4

Then push the PR

push only when locally green; CI likely passes first try — no post-push ping-pong.

This practice of "solidifying conventions into tools, letting machines enforce for humans" is itself worth learning: people tire, forget, and disagree, but a linter doesn't — it enforces one standard impartially, identically for everyone. That's how a codebase, in the hands of hundreds of contributors, can keep a consistent style and quality floor over the long run. Looking back over this lesson, merr's Input/System split teaches you to treat errors rigorously, while hard conventions like mlog/gci/generated-files teach you to respect the project's shared rules — together, they make "your code look like a native part of Milvus". Achieve that and your PR is truly fit to be merged. Next lesson, the final step: turn your change into a compliant PR (title format, DCO sign-off, linked issue, etc.).

📌 Key points
  • merr error handling: use merr not fmt.Errorf; split Input (request's own fault, not retriable) vs System (own/transient failure, stays retriable); the blame test asks "did the request content force this branch".
  • Classification decides retry: mislabeling kills a "retriable" op or spins a "doomed" one; add context only via merr.Wrap/Wrapf; grep retry.Do before turning something Input.
  • Hard conventions: log only via mlog (with ctx, no log/zap); import order stdlib→third-party→milvus-io (gci); generated files (mock/.pb.go) change source and regenerate, don't hand-edit.
  • Machine-enforced: golangci-lint/gci/guard tests auto-enforce; self-check locally before a PR (format/lint/test) to make code "look like a native part of Milvus".

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. Milvus's merr splits errors into Input and System. What's the core criterion and consequence?
  1. The two are equivalent; classify either way
  2. Input is minor, System is major — split by severity
  3. The criterion is the 'blame test': forced by the request content itself = Input (not retriable, return to user); Milvus's own/transient failure = System (stays retriable, should alarm). Classification directly decides whether the system retries
  4. Input is for C++, System for Go
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: C. The criterion isn't 'does it look like validation' but the blame test: did the request content force this branch (Input — user's fault, retry won't help) or an internal/transient failure (System — not the user's fault, may clear up). The consequence is concrete: many places use retry.Do; mislabeling a retriable System as Input kills an op that would've succeeded after a wait; mislabeling a doomed Input as System spins endless retries. So classification directly decides retry behavior.
2. About adding context to errors and adding new error codes, which follows merr conventions?
  1. Turning an error Input needn't consider any retry.Do elsewhere
  2. Pick any unused number for a new code, ranges don't matter
  3. Add context only via merr.Wrap/Wrapf (not WrapErrXxxErr(err,…) which masks the inner code); new errors pick a code from the existing family range in errors.go and set the retriable flag per Input/System
  4. Just wrap with fmt.Errorf, simplest
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: C. Add context with merr.Wrap/Wrapf — it preserves the inner code; WrapErrXxxErr(err,…) masks it with a new error, losing the original classification. Codes in errors.go are partitioned: similar-duty errors sit in adjacent ranges, so a new error must pick from its family range (not collide with C++ segcore codes) and set the retriable bool per Input/System. Also, before turning an error Input, grep retry.Do to ensure no retry chain depends on its retriability — mislabeling silently breaks retries.
3. Which statement about Milvus's other hard conventions (logging/imports/generated files) is correct?
  1. Generated mock files should be hand-edited to pass tests
  2. Use any logging library; the linter doesn't care
  3. Import order is free; gci is only a suggestion
  4. Log only via mlog (with ctx; log/zap/fmt.Println forbidden by depguard); imports stdlib→third-party→github.com/milvus-io (gci-enforced); mocks/.pb.go regenerate from source, don't hand-edit — mostly auto-enforced by golangci-lint/gci
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: D. .golangci.yml uses depguard to forbid pkg/v3/log, pingcap/log, raw zap, forcing pkg/v3/mlog with ctx on every call (Lesson 39). gci enforces imports into stdlib→third-party→github.com/milvus-io, this project's packages last. mockery-generated mocks and proto .pb.go are artifacts — change the interface/proto then regenerate via make, don't hand-edit (Lesson 43's discipline). These are mostly auto-enforced by golangci-lint/gci and guard tests — self-check with lint+tests locally before a PR.
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • This lesson says 'an error also carries information about what to do with it'; merr gives errors an identity (code/retriable/Input vs System). Please: (1) with a concrete example (e.g. ErrCollectionNotFound) explain why the same error defaults to System (retriable) internally but is flipped to Input via WrapErrAsInputErrorWhen only at the proxy boundary; (2) explain why add context with merr.Wrap rather than WrapErrXxxErr; (3) these conventions are mostly linter-enforced — discuss what 'solidifying conventions into tools' means for a hundreds-of-contributors open-source project, and what shared engineering aesthetic it shares with Lesson 40's 'config funneling' and Lesson 43's 'don't hand-edit generated files'.

Submitting PRs & contributing

You can build, test, and follow conventions — one step remains: turn your change into a PR Milvus will accept. This step has its own rules: the fork-and-pull workflow, a DCO sign-off on every commit (-s), a careful PR title format, and issue / design-doc links that depend on the change type. Plenty of rules — but once you clear them, you go from this guide's "reader" to a Milvus "contributor". This lesson walks the last mile.

🔌 Analogy
Filing a PR is like submitting a paper to a journal. You don't shove your draft into the main issue (you can't push straight to master); you first make your own copy (fork), revise on it, then go through the formal submission channel (open a PR). The submission must follow the format (title as type: description), cite its origin (link the matching issue / design doc), and be signed (DCO sign-off, declaring this code is yours to submit). Miss any one and the "desk-review bots" (Mergify, the DCO check, CI) bounce you back before a human reviewer ever sees it. This isn't hazing — like a journal's format check, it's so that precious human review energy focuses on "is the code good", not "is the format right".
🌍 Big picture
In one line: follow fork-and-pull (fork→add upstream→branch→change→git commit -s to sign DCO→push→open PR→CI+review→merge to master); the PR title is {type}: {description} (feat/fix/enhance/test/doc/…); link issue and design doc by type (fix needs an issue; feat needs issue+design doc); the body is non-empty. Bots (DCO/Mergify/CI) gate the format first; only then do humans review.

fork-and-pull: the standard contribution flow

Milvus (like most open-source projects) uses fork-and-pull. The core idea: you don't have write access to the official repo, so you work on your own copy and request to "pull" the result back. The flow is a fixed few steps. First, fork: on GitHub, copy milvus-io/milvus into your own account. Second, clone and set upstream: clone your fork locally, then add the official repo as the upstream remote (git remote add upstream git@github.com:milvus-io/milvus.git) so you can always sync the latest official code.

local repo
write code + commit -s
push
origin (your fork)
you have write access
PR
upstream (official master)
fetch-only to sync, never push directly

Third, branch and work: cut a topic branch from the latest upstream/master (git checkout upstream/master -b my-topic-branch), change code and commit on it. Fourth, sync and push: before committing, git fetch upstream, rebase to resolve conflicts if needed, then push to your own fork (origin). Fifth, open a PR: on GitHub, file a Pull Request from your branch to milvus-io/milvus's master. Then comes CI auto-running + maintainer review; once approved, your code is merged to master — congratulations, you're a contributor. This "everyone revises on a copy, converging via PRs" model lets thousands of strangers collaborate on one project without stepping on each other. The main path, drawn:

1

Fork & clone

fork milvus-io/milvus to your account, clone locally, add the upstream remote.

2

Topic branch

cut a branch from upstream/master, change code, git commit -s (with DCO sign-off).

3

Sync & push

git fetch upstream, rebase to resolve conflicts, push to your fork.

4

PR → review → merge

file a PR to master; merged after DCO/CI pass + maintainer approval.

DCO: sign off every commit (-s)

There's a gate nearly everyone hits on their first PR — the DCO (Developer Certificate of Origin). The rule is simple: every commit message must contain a line Signed-off-by: Your Name <your@email>. Miss it and the DCO check turns the PR red, blocking the merge.

You needn't type that line — git commit -s automatically appends it to the commit message (-s = sign-off). What does this line declare? It's a lightweight legal statement: you confirm "this code is mine, or I have the right to submit it under this project's open-source license". Open-source projects use it to clarify code provenance and avoid copyright risk — nobody wants someone sneaking in private code from elsewhere. So DCO isn't bureaucracy but the trust bedrock of open collaboration. A common pitfall: if you used AI assistance (like this guide's Copilot), the actual sign-off must be "you, the developer", on the last line (-s uses your git identity automatically); the AI's attribution can be added too, but must not replace your DCO. Signed vs unsigned, compared:

✅ git commit -s (signed)

the message ends with Signed-off-by: You <email>. The DCO check passes and the PR proceeds to review. Declares: I have the right to submit this code.

❌ forgot -s (unsigned)

the commit has no sign-off. The DCO check turns red and blocks the PR; you must add the sign-off (a git rebase can add -s to past commits) to continue. Every commit needs it.

PR title and links: rules by type

Past DCO, there are the PR's own format rules. Milvus requires PR titles as {type}: {description}, with a fixed set of types: feat: (new feature), fix: (bug fix), enhance: (improvement), test: (tests), doc: (docs), plus auto:, build(deps): and so on. This prefix isn't just cosmetic — it lets maintainers see the nature of the change at a glance and drives downstream automation rules.

The key automation decides, by type, what must be linked. fix: must link an issue (write issue: #123 in the PR body) — a bug fix should say which bug. feat: demands more: besides an issue, it must link a design doc (under docs/design-docs) — a feature needs a design before an implementation; without the design doc, the Mergify bot slaps a do-not-merge/missing-design-doc label to block the merge. enhance: depends on size (only large changes — L/XL/XXL — must link an issue). doc: and test:, which don't touch features, needn't link an issue. Also, the PR body must be non-empty; a PR to a 2.x branch must also link the matching master PR (pr: #123). Memorize these as a table and fill them in at PR time so you won't be bounced.

PR typeMeaningMust link
fix:bug fixissue (issue: #123)
feat:new featureissue + design doc (docs/design-docs)
enhance:improvementissue only if large (L/XL/XXL)
doc: / test:docs / testsno issue required

From reader to contributor: you're ready

Having come this far, look back at what you now hold. You understand Milvus's macro architecture (coordinators and nodes, control vs data plane), have walked the full write and query paths, drilled into the C++ core to see how "fast" is made, and mapped the outer ring of API, observability, config, deployment; now you can also build it, test it, change it by its conventions, and turn changes into a compliant PR. That whole set is exactly what a qualified contributor has — you're ready.

The hardest part of filing a PR was never the process but the courage to take the first step. Don't wait to "understand everything" before acting — no one starts that way. A good start is often a small good first issue, fixing a typo in the docs, or adding a missing test. The process becomes familiar once you've done it once; confidence builds when your first PR is merged. Everything in this guide — fifty-odd lessons of diagrams, analogies, source, and quizzes — was to bring you to this step: so you no longer see Milvus as an inscrutable black box, but read its skeleton, grasp its trade-offs, and have the nerve to change and contribute to it. Reaching here, you've walked the road from "a curious bystander" to "a prepared contributor". The last lesson is a glossary for future reference; after it come Part 11, "Advanced topics (optional)" (bulk import, hybrid search, quotas and rate-limiting), and Part 12, "Design themes" — a synthesis of the six throughlines running through the whole guide, well worth finishing. But the real next step waits for you on GitHub — go file your first PR.

📌 Key points
  • fork-and-pull: fork→clone+add upstream→branch from upstream/master→change+commit→fetch/rebase→push to fork→PR to master→CI+review→merge.
  • DCO sign-off: every commit needs Signed-off-by, auto-added by git commit -s; it declares you have the right to submit the code, and a missing one blocks the PR.
  • PR title: {type}: {description} (feat/fix/enhance/test/doc/…); non-empty body.
  • Link by type: fix:→issue; feat:→issue+design doc (docs/design-docs, else Mergify blocks); enhance:→issue only if large; doc:/test:→none. Start from a good first issue and take the first step.

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. Milvus uses the fork-and-pull workflow. Which description is correct?
  1. You lack write access to the official repo: fork to your account (origin) to work, add the official as upstream (pull-only); branch from upstream/master, push to origin, file a PR to master, merged after CI+review
  2. No branch needed, edit master directly
  3. Push changes straight to milvus-io/milvus's master
  4. Forking grants write access to the official repo
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: A. With thousands of contributors, you can't grant everyone write access to the main repo. The fork mechanism: you have full write access to your fork (origin) to change freely; the official repo as upstream is fetch-only, not push. Typical flow: pull latest from upstream→change locally→push to origin→file a PR from origin to upstream→CI+maintainer review→merge to master. Adding upstream also lets you sync before developing, reducing conflicts. This keeps participation open while protecting the main repo's quality.
2. About the DCO sign-off (Signed-off-by), which is correct?
  1. DCO is optional; CI doesn't check it
  2. Every commit needs a Signed-off-by, auto-appended by git commit -s; it declares you have the right to submit this code under the project's license, and a missing one blocks the PR via the DCO check
  3. An AI tool's attribution can replace your DCO sign-off
  4. You only need to sign the first commit once
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. The DCO requires each commit message to contain a line Signed-off-by: Name ; git commit -s auto-appends it using your git identity. It's a lightweight statement: you confirm the code is yours or you have the right to submit it under the project's license, clarifying provenance and avoiding copyright risk. Without it the DCO check goes red and blocks the PR — you can git rebase to add -s to past commits then force-push. Note: with AI assistance, the final sign-off must be you the developer; the AI's attribution is additional, not a replacement.
3. About the PR title format and per-type linking requirements, which is correct?
  1. feat only needs an issue, no design doc
  2. Title is free-form; bots don't check
  3. No PR needs to link any issue
  4. Title is {type}: {description} (feat/fix/enhance/test/doc…); fix must link an issue, feat must link issue+design doc (docs/design-docs, else Mergify blocks), enhance needs an issue only if large, doc/test don't; body non-empty
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: D. Milvus requires PR titles as {type}: {description}, with type fixed to feat (new feature)/fix (bug)/enhance/test/doc/auto/build(deps), so maintainers can identify and drive automation. Linking by type: fix needs an issue (issue: #123); feat needs issue+design doc (under docs/design-docs; missing it, Mergify adds do-not-merge/missing-design-doc to block); enhance needs an issue only if large (L/XL/XXL); doc/test don't. Also the body can't be empty; a PR to a 2.x branch must link the matching master PR (pr: #123).
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • This lesson says a PR's format gates (DCO/title/links) are bot-checked first so human review focuses on 'is the code good'. Please: (1) narrate a full contribution from fork to merge in your own words, clarifying how data flows among upstream/origin/local; (2) explain why feat: PRs must link a design doc, and what shared idea this shares with Lesson 44's 'enforce conventions via linters'; (3) as a newcomer who just finished this guide, what first change would you start with (e.g. a good first issue / doc fix / adding a test), and why is starting small a good strategy for taking the first step?

Glossary

This is the guide's quick-reference glossary, gathering the key concepts from the first 45 lessons into a few tables by theme, each with a one-line definition and its lesson, for easy future lookup. When a term puzzles you, flip here, then follow the lesson back to the full text.

1. Overview & architecture

TermOne-line definitionLesson
Vector databasea DB centered on "similarity search (find the nearest-neighbor topK for a vector)", vs scalar DBs' exact matchL1
Embeddingmap text/images into high-dimensional vectors so "semantically close" becomes "near in distance"L4
ANN (approx. nearest neighbor)nearest-neighbor search trading a little accuracy for huge speed; the algorithmic bedrockL5
Collection / Partitioncollection = a logical "table"; partition = a data-splitting unit within itL6
Segmentthe physical data unit; growing (writable)→sealed (read-only)→indexedL7
Control / data planecontrol plane = coordination/scheduling (Go); data plane = actually moving and computing dataL9
Coordinatormanages metadata & scheduling; rootcoord/datacoord/querycoord, mergeable into MixCoordL11-13
Proxythe user entrance/facade: receive, auth, enqueue, fan out, mergeL10
QueryNode / DataNode / StreamingNodeworker nodes: search; data processing (compaction etc.); consume WAL & flushL10, L17

2. Write path

TermOne-line definitionLesson
WAL (write-ahead log)write changes as an ordered log first, flush asynchronously; recover by replay on crashL16, L31
TimeTick / TSOa globally ordered timestamp (issued by a single-source TSO) defining "who comes first"L16, L30
Flush / Binlogflush an in-memory segment to columnar binlog files on disk (object storage)L17, L18
Compactionmerge small/fragmented segments, purge deleted data; Mix/Merge/Clustering/L0 etc.L19
Delete / bloom filterdeletes mark by primary key; PrimaryKeyStats' bloom filter speeds "is it in this segment"L20

3. Query path

TermOne-line definitionLesson
topKa search returns the K nearest results by distanceL25
Delegatorthe coordinator on a QueryNode: fan out to segments, then merge their topKL26
Three-level reducethree-tier topK merge: in-segment → node (delegator) → ProxyL29
Consistency levelsStrong/Bounded/Session/Eventually/Customized; a freshness vs performance trade-offL30
Guarantee timestampGuaranteeTs derived from the consistency level, deciding "how fresh data must be seen"L30
Filter-then-searchscalar filtering yields a bitset; vector search runs only over the survivorsL28

4. Indexing

TermOne-line definitionLesson
HNSWhierarchical navigable small-world graph index; greedily walk toward neighbors on a graphL5, L22
IVFinverted lists + clustering: locate candidate clusters, then compute within themL5, L22
DiskANN / PQdisk-friendly graph index / product quantization compression (saves RAM, slight accuracy loss)L5, L22
KnowhereMilvus's unified vector index engine (wrapping FAISS etc.); all CPU/GPU indexes go through itL22
Scalar / full-text indexindex scalar fields (filtering) and text (BM25, via Rust's tantivy)L24
GPU indexes (CAGRA etc.)types in Milvus, algorithms in Knowhere/RAFT; compile-time milvus-gpuL37

5. C++ core

TermOne-line definitionLesson
cgothe C-ABI bridge between Go and C++ (*_c.h); coarse-grained calls, zero-copy dataL34
Chunked columns / ChunkedColumna segment is columnar, each column cut into fixed-size chunks; good for SIMD & per-block memoryL35
mmapmap binlog into virtual memory, fault in on demand; lets data exceed physical RAML35
Two-layer expressions / ITypeExprlogical ITypeExpr (what) lowered to physical Expr::Eval (vectorized execution)L36
Operator pipeline / Task·Driver·Operatorvectorized Volcano model: Task (one run), Driver (push data), Operator (single-purpose)L36
SIMDsingle instruction, multiple data: compute a batch of floats in one instruction; basis of vectorizationL36

6. Streaming system

TermOne-line definitionLesson
StreamingNodedrives consume-WAL→flush; DataNode narrowed to compaction etc.L17, L31
Broadcasterthe multi-log broadcaster for DDL/DCL: lock + ACK so many WALs take effect consistentlyL32
CDC / replicationcross-cluster sync: replicate the WAL and replay remotely ("what happened"); star topologyL33
Message queue (MQ)the WAL backend: rocksmq (standalone)/Pulsar (cluster)/Kafka/Woodpecker (recommended)L41

7. Operations & contributing

TermOne-line definitionLesson
milvuspb / MilvusServicethe gRPC contract defined by milvus-proto; every SDK speaks itL38
Three observability pillarslogs (mlog) / metrics (Prometheus) / traces (OpenTelemetry); stitch one request via ctxL39
paramtablea type-safe config registry (ParamItem + GetAsInt); multi-source by priority, hot-reloadableL40
merr (Input vs System)the error library: Input (blame request, not retriable) vs System (blame system/transient, retriable)L44
mockery / mockeymockery = generate interface fakes; mockey = patch functions at runtime (needs -N -l)L43
Test flagsGo tests must carry -tags dynamic,test and -gcflags=all=-N -lL43
DCO / fork-and-pullevery commit needs git commit -s sign-off; fork→branch→PR→review→mergeL45
PR typesfeat (needs issue+design doc)/fix (needs issue)/enhance/doc/test...L45

The main ten parts close here. The following Part 11, "Advanced topics (optional)" is a bonus course for when you have appetite to spare — bulk import, multi-vector hybrid search & reranking, quotas and rate-limiting, plus RBAC/resource groups/multi-tenancy and other production features; come back when you need them. May these diagrams, analogies, and snippets of source help you see a vast vector database as a landscape you can understand — and join. Go contribute — the world awaits your first PR.

Bulk import: the second write path

The writes in Part 4 were the online, streaming path: row by row through Proxy → WAL → growing → flush. But when you must load hundreds of millions of rows at once (migration, offline base-loading), sending every row through the WAL is slow and wasteful. For this Milvus has a second write path — bulk import: read files directly, bypass the WAL, and have DataCoord orchestrate a multi-phase import job that turns whole batches directly into segments. This lesson covers that "by-the-truckload" channel and how it divides labor with streaming writes.

🔌 Analogy
Think of streaming writes as à-la-carte service: guests order dish by dish, the kitchen makes and plates them one at a time — real-time, flexible, but one serving at a time. Bulk import is like restocking a warehouse by the truckload: a truck pulls up to the back, skipping the front-of-house ordering flow, and the goods are counted, shelved, and logged in directly. You wouldn't restock a whole warehouse "à la carte" (too slow), nor call a truck for one guest's dish. Each path has its scene: everyday real-time writes go streaming (Lessons 15–17); one-shot massive loading goes bulk import. Grasp "one database, two restocking channels" and you see why Milvus builds a separate import.
🌍 Big picture
In one line: bulk import is a second write path that bypasses the WAL — the client calls ImportV2 (proxy/task_import.go), DataCoord orchestrates a multi-phase job (datacoord/import_*): Pending → PreImport (scan/validate/estimate) → Import (DataNode reads files straight into segments) → Sorting → IndexBuilding → commit-visible; file reading via importutilv2 supports json/csv/numpy/parquet. It makes "one-shot massive loading" a dedicated batch pipeline.

Why a second write path

The streaming writes of Lessons 15–17 are tailored for online, continuous ingestion: each write is packed by Proxy, appended to the WAL, buffered into a growing segment by the StreamingNode, then flushed. This guarantees real-time visibility, crash recovery, and a single ordered log shared by reads and writes — perfect for "write and query at the same time". But it assumes one thing: writes trickle in steadily.

Reality has another need: load one huge batch of historical data at once — migrating from another system, base-loading hundreds of millions of pre-computed vectors, or periodic batch backfills. Here, routing every row through the WAL causes trouble: it's slow (each row serialized, appended, ordered, consumed), it crowds out online writes (a massive backfill jams the WAL and StreamingNode, hurting normal real-time ingestion), and it's wasteful (this data is "written once, never changed" — it needs none of the WAL's real-time, replayable insurance). Just as you wouldn't restock a warehouse à la carte — batch scenarios need batch tools. So Milvus offers a second path: bulk import, reading files directly, bypassing the WAL, optimized for "large, offline, write-once". The two paths side by side:

Streaming writes (L15–17)

row by row via Proxy → WAL → growing → flush. Real-time visible, replayable, log shared by reads/writes. For online continuous writes; but massive backfills are slow and crowd out online.

Bulk import (this lesson)

read files → bypass the WAL → generate segments directly → sort/index → commit. High throughput, no crowding out online. For migration/offline base-loading/backfill; not for per-row real-time.

One common misconception to clear up: "bypassing the WAL" doesn't mean "unsafe" or "loses data". The WAL's core value is "log first, replay on crash" insurance for online writes that are real-time and could crash at any moment (Lesson 16). Bulk import's situation is fundamentally different: the source data already sits safely in files (parquet and the like in object storage), so if an import fails midway, the source files are still there — just run it again. In other words, the import's source of truth is those source files themselves, not the WAL, and the final commit is atomic (all-or-nothing), so a crash leaves no half-loaded mess. Bulk import bypasses the WAL not by cutting a safety corner but because this scenario simply doesn't need that layer — a tidy lesson in matching a mechanism to its premise: when the premise changes (offline, re-readable source files), pick the path that fits.

ImportV2 and the import job

Bulk import starts at a dedicated API entry: ImportV2. The client tells the Proxy which files to import (json/csv/numpy/parquet files sitting in object storage); on the Proxy side the importTask in internal/proxy/task_import.go catches it and hands it to DataCoord — note the orchestration brain is DataCoord (it already coordinates segments, flushing, and index building, Lesson 12), so scheduling this "turn files into segments" batch job is natural for it.

data files
json/csv/numpy/parquet in object storage
ImportV2
Proxy
importTask catches & forwards
orchestrate
DataCoord
multi-phase job state machine
dispatch
DataNode
read files → write segments (binlog)

DataCoord treats an import not as "an action" but as a stateful, multi-phase job (code in internal/datacoord/import_job.go, import_checker.go, etc.). The job has a state machine: Pending → PreImporting → Importing → Sorting → IndexBuilding → Uncommitted → Committing → done (failures go to Failed). Why a multi-phase job rather than one shot? Because importing massive data is a long-running, failure-prone process, and splitting it into phases enables resumability, queryable progress, and retry on failure — the same wisdom as Lesson 21's "index per segment": break one big task into schedulable, recoverable steps. The pipeline below is the full journey of an import job.

1

PreImport

a DataNode first scans the files: validate schema match, estimate row count and distribution (especially under autoID). Writes no data — a "count-and-plan before work".

2

Import

DataNodes actually read files and write straight into segments (producing binlogs), bypassing the WAL. The main haul, split across DataNodes in parallel by file.

3

Sorting / IndexBuilding

sort the imported segments (by PK/clustering, aiding later search), then build indexes (reusing the Lesson 21–23 index pipeline).

4

Commit to visible

once everything's ready, an atomic commit makes the new segments visible to queries — all or nothing, so you never query a "half-loaded" batch.

The beauty of two phases: PreImport vs Import

The most savorable part of the pipeline is splitting PreImport from Import. Intuitively you might think "read files, write segments — just do it in one step?" But Milvus deliberately does a dry run first (PreImport): only scan, validate, estimate, writing no data. What is this step doing? It's front-loading risk and planning.

Concretely, PreImport first confirms the files are readable and the schema matches — if your parquet columns don't match the collection schema, this step errors early, instead of failing halfway and leaving a pile of half-built segments to clean up. It also estimates row count and distribution: under autoID (auto-increment primary keys), the system needs to know in advance "roughly how many rows, how to slice segments, how many PK IDs to pre-allocate", and PreImport's estimate lets the real Import phase slice segments evenly and reserve enough resources. This "survey first, then build" two-step is a classic engineering move for large, uncertain inputs: spend one cheap pre-scan to buy certainty and efficiency at execution time. Once PreImport passes, the Import phase can haul in parallel with confidence, because the mines were cleared during the survey. This echoes Lesson 44's merr philosophy — an "input error" catchable early at the boundary shouldn't be left to blow up deep inside.

Look one level deeper at why PreImport estimates row counts, because its tie to autoID is worth spelling out. Recall Lesson 6: a primary key can be auto-allocated (autoID). In streaming writes each row's auto-increment PK is handed out one at a time — naturally. But bulk import loads a whole batch at once, so the system must know how many rows are coming in advance to pre-allocate a contiguous range of PK IDs for them — otherwise multiple DataNodes writing in parallel could collide on PKs or leave gaps. PreImport's row estimate is precisely what underpins this PK pre-allocation. So a seemingly trivial "scan first" actually guards primary-key uniqueness, a database's lifeline — proof that "how to safely mass-produce segments" is its own design problem, not just streaming logic poured in bulk.

How it divides labor with streaming writes

Finally, the division and cooperation of the two write paths. They share the same "segment" abstraction (Lesson 7): whether data was streamed into a growing segment then flushed, or bulk-imported straight into a segment, the end product is the same binlog-format segment in object storage, loaded and searched by QueryNodes alike. So bulk import isn't a separate world — it's a more efficient path producing the same product, which is why it slots seamlessly into the whole system.

How to choose in practice? Online, continuous, real-time-visible writes (an app's everyday insert) go streaming; one-shot, massive, offline, write-once loading (migration, batch backfill, offline base-loading) goes bulk import. Many production deployments use both: one big import to base-load historical data, then streaming writes to carry the incremental flow thereafter. Grasp "one segment abstraction, two production paths" and your view of Milvus's write side is complete — it can catch a real-time trickle like a tap, and swallow a truckload like a loading dock. That's the elasticity a mature database should have.

📌 Key points
  • Second write path: bulk import bypasses the WAL, optimized for "large, offline, write-once"; streaming writes (L15–17) are built for online real-time ingestion — the two divide labor.
  • A DataCoord-orchestrated job: ImportV2 → multi-phase state machine Pending→PreImport→Import→Sorting→IndexBuilding→commit; phased for resumability, queryable progress, retry on failure.
  • PreImport vs Import: a dry-run survey first (validate schema, estimate rows/distribution, esp. autoID), then parallel hauling (DataNodes read json/csv/numpy/parquet straight into segments). Survey first, then build.
  • Shared segment abstraction: imported segments are same-format as streamed-and-flushed ones, loaded alike by QueryNodes; the commit is atomic (all-visible or none). Production often uses both: import to base-load + streaming for the increment.

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. Why does Milvus build a separate bulk-import path in addition to streaming writes?
  1. Because imported segments have a different format and must be separate
  2. Routing a one-shot massive load through the WAL row by row is slow, crowds out online writes, and is wasteful (this data is write-once); bulk import bypasses the WAL and reads files straight into segments, optimized for large offline loads
  3. Purely to add one more API
  4. Because streaming writes can't keep data safe
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. Streaming writes (L15–17) are built for online continuous ingestion: row by row via Proxy→WAL→growing→flush, ensuring real-time visibility and crash replay. But loading hundreds of millions of historical rows at once via the WAL is slow (each row serialized/appended/ordered/consumed), jams the WAL and StreamingNode (hurting online writes), and wastes the WAL's real-time/replay insurance on write-once data. So bulk import bypasses the WAL, reading files straight into segments — batch tools for batch scenes. Note 'bypassing WAL ≠ unsafe': the source of truth is the source files themselves; on failure, just rerun.
2. Bulk import is a DataCoord-orchestrated multi-phase job. What are the phases roughly, and why phase it?
  1. Executed entirely by Proxy, not DataCoord
  2. Just one phase: dump the files in at once
  3. Build indexes first, then read files — reverse order
  4. Pending→PreImport (scan/validate/estimate)→Import (read files straight into segments)→Sorting→IndexBuilding→commit; phased for resumability, queryable progress, retry on failure
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: D. The client calls ImportV2; Proxy's importTask hands it to DataCoord (already the coordinator of segments/flushing/indexing). DataCoord treats import as a stateful job: Pending→PreImporting→Importing→Sorting→IndexBuilding→Uncommitted→Committing (Failed on error). Importing massive data is long-running and failure-prone, so phasing enables resume, queryable progress, and retry — the same 'schedulable, recoverable steps' wisdom as Lesson 21's per-segment indexing. DataNodes do the actual read-files-into-segments; importutilv2 supports json/csv/numpy/parquet.
3. Why split PreImport from Import, and what does PreImport do?
  1. PreImport deletes old data
  2. PreImport does a dry-run survey first: validate schema, estimate row count and distribution (esp. under autoID, to pre-allocate PK IDs), writing no data; it clears mines and plans segment slicing so the real Import can run in parallel with confidence
  3. They're identical, just named differently
  4. PreImport writes the data once and Import writes it again
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. 'Survey first, then build': PreImport only scans/validates/estimates, writing no data. It confirms files are readable and the schema matches (erroring early instead of leaving half-built segments), and estimates row count/distribution — under autoID, the system must know the count to pre-allocate a contiguous PK-ID range for parallel DataNodes, else PKs collide or leave gaps. A cheap pre-scan buys certainty and efficiency at execution time; akin to Lesson 44's merr 'catch input errors early at the boundary'.
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • This lesson says bulk import and streaming writes are 'one segment abstraction, two production paths'. Please: (1) pick a write path for each scenario and justify: A) an online app continuously inserting user data; B) migrating 500M historical vectors from a legacy system; C) a nightly batch backfill of yesterday's data. (2) Explain why 'bypassing the WAL' is safe in the import scenario — what is the import's 'source of truth'? (3) Why does it matter that both paths produce 'same-format segments'? What shared design aesthetic does this share with Lesson 22 (Knowhere's unified interface) and Lesson 36 (operators uniformly consuming/emitting a batch)?

Hybrid search & reranking

The searches so far were mostly "one query vector, find neighbors in one vector field". But real workloads often need to combine multiple signals: both semantic similarity (dense vectors) and keyword hits (sparse vectors / BM25, Lesson 24); or matching both an image and a text embedding. That's hybrid search: a collection can have multiple vector fields, one request runs several sub-searches in parallel, then fuses (reranks) the result lists into one final ranking. This lesson covers how it fans out and how it fuses.

🔌 Analogy
Think of hybrid search as hiring by combining multiple dimensions. For the same candidate pool, HR ranks them several ways: by "skill match", by "past experience", by "culture fit". Each ranking has merit, but you ultimately want one combined ranking. How to fuse several rankings into one? Three ways: look only at each person's rank in each list and reward high ranks (RRF); give each list a weight and combine weighted scores (weighted fusion); or have a senior expert re-interview the shortlist and give an authoritative order (model reranking). Hybrid search does exactly this "score several ways → fuse into one list".
🌍 Big picture
In one line: HybridSearch carries several sub-searches in one request (SubReqs, each over a vector field); the Proxy fans them out, gets a ranked list each, then fuses into the final topK with a ranker — three kinds: RRF (by rank), WeightedRanker (by weighted score), and model reranking (internal/util/function/rerank, calling an external cross-encoder). Parsing is in parseRankParams (proxy/task_search.go) / rerank_meta.go.

Why hybrid search

Single-vector search has an innate limit: one embedding is only good at capturing one kind of signal. Dense vectors (Lesson 4) excel at semantics — "sofa" matches "couch" even with different spelling; but they're insensitive to exact keywords — search a specific model "XJ-200" and a semantic vector may miss. Sparse vectors (sparse / BM25, Lesson 24) are the opposite: great at exact keyword hits, oblivious to semantic synonyms. Each has a blind spot, and they complement each other.

Dense vectors

great at semantics: sofa≈couch, doctor≈physician — matches despite different wording. Blind spot: insensitive to exact keywords/model numbers (XJ-200).

Sparse vectors (sparse / BM25)

great at exact keyword hits: search a word, hit that word. Blind spot: oblivious to synonyms/semantics — reword it and it misses.

So a natural idea arises: can we use both and cover each other's weaknesses? That's the motivation for hybrid search. To support it, Milvus lets a collection define multiple vector fields (Lesson 6) — say a dense_vec for semantic embeddings and a sparse_vec for keyword weights. At search time the same query hits both fields: the dense path finds the semantically nearest, the sparse path the keyword-best, and the results are fused. Beyond "semantic + keyword", hybrid search also covers multimodal (image + text vectors together), multi-view (different embedding models of the same object), and more. In a line: when "similar" can't be measured by a single yardstick, combine several and fuse — a key step from "toy demo" to "real workload".

One HybridSearch: several sub-searches

Mechanically, a hybrid search is not one search but a bundle. The client's HybridSearchRequest carries a set of sub-requests (SubReqs) — each a plain vector search: which vector field, which query vector, what topK, what filter. The Proxy side (internal/proxy/task_search.go), seeing len(SubReqs) > 0, knows this is an "advanced" hybrid search, so it fans each sub-request out as an independent search (reusing the whole Lesson 25–29 query path), runs them in parallel, each merging its own topK.

A key realization: each sub-search is itself a complete vector search you've already learned. It fans through delegators to segments, does filter-then-search, does the three-level reduce (Lesson 29). So hybrid search builds no new search engine — it runs N plain searches together, then adds a fusion step. Again that familiar philosophy: compose existing capabilities into a new feature rather than reinvent. The number of sub-searches is capped (defaultMaxSearchRequest) so one request can't fan out enough to crush the system. Once all sub-searches return, the Proxy holds N separately-ranked candidate lists — and the question becomes: how to combine these N into one?

One easy-to-miss detail that strongly affects quality: each sub-search usually needs to fetch more candidates than the final topK. Intuitively you might think "I only want the top-10, so top-10 per path is enough" — but fusion reshuffles the ranks: a document ranked 15th on the semantic list yet 2nd on the keyword list may well land in the final top-10; if each path fetched only its own top-10, that document was never retrieved on the semantic side and is lost for good at fusion time. So in practice each sub-search fetches a candidate pool larger than the final topK (say final 10, but fetch 50 per path), leaving fusion room to "come from behind". This is one of the most common hybrid-search tuning traps — fusion isn't stitching a few top-Ks together but re-ranking over a larger candidate set — and missing it is exactly how recall silently drops after fusion.

HybridSearch
one request, N SubReqs
fan out
sub-search: dense_vec
semantic nearest → ranked list A
sub-search: sparse_vec
keyword best → ranked list B
fuse
ranker
A+B → final topK

Fusion: RRF / weighted / model reranking

Combining N rankings into one is the soul of hybrid search; this step is rerank (fusion). The hard part: scores from different sub-searches are often incomparable — a dense similarity (say 0.92) and a sparse BM25 score (say 14.7) are simply not the same unit, so adding them is meaningless. Milvus offers three kinds of ranker (config in proxy/rerank_meta.go, parseRankParams).

First, RRF (Reciprocal Rank Fusion): it ignores raw scores and looks only at rank. A document ranked r-th in some list earns 1/(k+r) (k a smoothing constant, default 60); summing these "rank scores" across all lists gives the final score. The beauty: it naturally erases unit differences — ranks are always comparable. When unsure how to weigh the scores, RRF is a robust default. Second, WeightedRanker: when you clearly want one path to matter more (say "semantics 70%, keywords 30%"), give each path a weight and sum the normalized scores. Third, model reranking: hand the candidates to a dedicated rerank model (a cross-encoder, e.g. Cohere, TEI; code in internal/util/function/rerank) to re-score and reorder — highest quality, but it calls an external model and costs the most. The three side by side:

RankerHow it fusesBest for
RRFby rank only: each list adds 1/(k+r) (k default 60), erasing unit differencesincomparable scores / robust default
WeightedRankernormalize scores, then weighted sumwhen you want one path weighted higher
Model rerankinghand candidates to a cross-encoder model to re-scoremaximum relevance, external-call cost acceptable
RRF fusion example (k=60): rank only, not raw scores — so different units still combine
semantic list Adoc D ranked #1→1/(60+1) = 0.0164
keyword list Bdoc D ranked #3→1/(60+3) = 0.0159
RRF total0.0164 + 0.0159=0.0323 ← D's final score

Where it lands in the system

So how do you choose among the three? Another familiar trade-off. RRF is the most carefree and robust — no tuning, no unit worries, friendliest when "I can't really say which path matters more", which is why it's often the default. WeightedRanker gives you control — when business sense tells you "semantics matters more here", you can push a weight in, at the cost of tuning that ratio yourself. Model reranking has the highest quality ceiling — a cross-encoder examines each query–candidate pair closely, far more precise than score fusion, but it runs a model inference per candidate, so latency and cost climb sharply; you therefore usually fine-rank only a small set of already-fused candidates (say the top ~50). A common production combo is coarse then fine: use RRF to fuse the paths into a candidate batch quickly, then model-rerank just that batch — filter the bulk cheaply, polish the tip expensively. This "two-level ranking" is the same layered-convergence wisdom as Lesson 5's ANN (coarse buckets, then exact) and Lesson 28's filter-then-search.

Place hybrid search back on the architecture map and you'll see it's mostly the Proxy's job, with a touch of core help. The Proxy is the conductor: it parses SubReqs, fans each sub-search out (through QueryNode delegators), gathers the N results, and performs fusion at the Proxy layer (RRF/weighted computed by Proxy; model reranking has the Proxy call the external model). That makes sense — fusion needs to see the global results of all paths, and the Proxy is exactly "the point where all results converge" (recall Lesson 29: the Proxy is the top tier of the three-level reduce).

The core side has a related role: RescoresNode in internal/core/src/exec/operator — sibling to Lesson 36's FilterBitsNode, differing in that FilterBitsNode produces a "which rows pass" bitset, while RescoresNode takes an array of offsets, re-scores them, and produces valid results with new scores. It serves cases needing in-segment score adjustment (some rerank/groupby logic pushed down to segcore). But for most hybrid searches, fusion happens at the Proxy layer, since only there are all sub-search results held at once. To wrap up: hybrid search = multiple vector fields (Lesson 6) + parallel sub-searches (reusing Lessons 25–29) + one fusion step (RRF/weighted/model). It lets Milvus answer "both semantically similar and keyword-matching" — the most-used move in modern RAG, recommendation, and multimodal retrieval.

📌 Key points
  • Why hybrid: a single embedding has blind spots — dense excels at semantics, sparse (BM25) at keywords, complementary; a collection can hold multiple vector fields, and hybrid search combines signals (also for multimodal/multi-view).
  • Sub-searches: HybridSearch carries N SubReqs, each a complete plain vector search; the Proxy fans them out, getting a ranked list each (reusing Lessons 25–29, no new engine).
  • Fusion (rerank): scores across paths are incomparable, so a ranker is needed — RRF (rank-only 1/(k+r), erases units), WeightedRanker (normalize then weight), model reranking (cross-encoder, highest quality, highest cost).
  • Where it lands: fusion is mostly done by the Proxy (the convergence point of all results, the top reduce tier of Lesson 29); the core's RescoresNode serves in-segment re-scoring. Common in RAG/recommendation/multimodal.

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. Why hybrid search instead of searching just one vector field?
  1. Because single-vector search is always wrong
  2. A single embedding has blind spots: dense excels at semantics but misses exact keywords, sparse (BM25) hits keywords but ignores synonyms; hybrid search combines multiple vector fields and relevance signals (also for multimodal)
  3. Because hybrid search uses less memory
  4. Purely to make queries slower
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. Dense vectors (L4) excel at semantics — sofa≈couch — but miss exact keywords like 'XJ-200'; sparse/BM25 (L24) nails keyword hits but ignores synonyms. Their blind spots complement. A collection can define multiple vector fields (L6); hybrid search hits several at once, combining signals, also covering multimodal (image+text) and multi-view. It's a key step from toy to real workload (especially RAG, where questions carry both semantics and key terms).
2. How is a HybridSearch executed in the system?
  1. It's a brand-new engine independent of plain search
  2. It carries N SubReqs, each a complete plain vector search; the Proxy fans them out in parallel (reusing the Lesson 25–29 path), gets a ranked list each, then fuses into the final topK with a ranker
  3. It searches in one segment only, no fan-out
  4. It concatenates all vector fields into one big vector and searches once
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. A HybridSearchRequest carries a set of SubReqs, each specifying the vector field, query vector, topK, filter. The Proxy (task_search.go), seeing len(SubReqs)>0, treats each as an independent search, reusing delegator fan-out, filter-then-search, and the three-level reduce (L29), each merging its own topK. So it builds no new engine — 'N plain searches run together + one fusion'. Sub-searches are capped (defaultMaxSearchRequest); each path usually fetches more candidates than the final topK, since fusion reshuffles ranks.
3. The three rankers — RRF, WeightedRanker, model reranking — how does each work and when to use it?
  1. RRF requires calling an external model
  2. All three just add the raw scores from each path
  3. RRF uses rank only (1/(k+r), k default 60, erases units, robust default); WeightedRanker normalizes then weights (to weight a path higher); model reranking uses a cross-encoder (highest quality, highest cost, often only the top few)
  4. Model reranking ignores the query, looking only at candidates
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: C. The hard part: scores across paths are incomparable (dense 0.92 vs BM25 14.7, different units). RRF looks only at rank: r-th in a list earns 1/(k+r), summed across lists, naturally erasing units — a robust default when unsure. WeightedRanker, when you want to favor a path, weights the normalized scores. Model reranking hands candidates to a cross-encoder (Cohere/TEI) for pairwise re-scoring — highest quality but a model run per candidate, high latency/cost, so often only the top few (e.g. top-50). A common 'coarse then fine': RRF fuses fast → model refines — spend costly compute where it counts (like the layered convergence of L5/L28).
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • This lesson says hybrid search is 'score several ways → fuse into one list', fused mainly at the Proxy. Please: (1) design a hybrid search for a RAG scenario (user asks 'the 2023 return policy for XJ-200'): which vector fields, and why is pure-semantic or pure-keyword insufficient? (2) explain why RRF can fuse 'incomparable scores', and why each sub-search often fetches more candidates than the final topK. (3) why is fusion done at the Proxy rather than a QueryNode? How does this echo Lesson 29's 'the top tier of the three-level reduce is the Proxy'?

Quota, rate-limiting & backpressure

Every system has a capacity ceiling. If writes outrun flushing, memory fills with growing segments and bursts; if queries exhaust resources, nodes are dragged down; if data fills the disk, the whole cluster can avalanche. A mature database must protect itself — that's quota, rate-limiting, and backpressure. This lesson shows how Milvus uses QuotaCenter (the central governor) to watch live metrics, compute rate limits, and have the Proxy enforce them at the entrance, actively rejecting requests when needed to rescue the cluster from overload.

🔌 Analogy
Think of the system as a restaurant. The kitchen (the nodes) has finite capacity: too many dishes at once and it flails, plating collapses. So the host (the Proxy entrance) controls the rate of letting guests in based on how busy the kitchen is — not letting a crowd flood in and overwhelm it. The manager (QuotaCenter), with a global view, watches the kitchen's live metrics (burner occupancy, prep stock, plating delay), computes "how many tables per minute we can seat now", and tells the host to follow it. When the kitchen is truly about to blow, the manager issues a harsher order: "fully booked, stop seating" — better to hold new guests at the door (come back later) than let the diners already inside suffer too. Throttling is a gentle brake; rejection is the emergency brake — together they keep the restaurant from collapsing at the peak.
🌍 Big picture
In one line: QuotaCenter (rootcoord/quota_center.go) periodically collectMetrics from nodes (memory water level, disk, TT lag…) → calculateRates for each operation type (DML/DQL/DDL…) → pushes them to all Proxies; the Proxy's RateLimitInterceptor checks before each request and returns ErrServiceRateLimit (retriable) when over; under hard-limit overload, forceDenyWriting/Reading drives rates to 0, rejecting a whole class. Decide centrally, enforce at the edge.

Why self-protect: the essence of backpressure

First a plain truth: a system's rate of digesting requests is finite, but clients won't restrain themselves. Let clients write infinitely fast and query infinitely much, and sooner or later you hit some hard resource ceiling: memory filled by unflushed growing segments, disk burst by binlogs, CPU saturated by queries. And then? Not a graceful slowdown but a crash — nodes OOM-killed, requests timing out en masse, even avalanche-style cascading failure (one node dies, its load shifts to others and crushes them too).

So mature systems hold an iron rule: better to slow down on purpose than be swept away. That's backpressure — when downstream can't keep up, push back on upstream to make it slow down. Like a clog downstream in a pipe: pressure travels back up the pipe, forcing the upstream tap to close partway. For a database, backpressure means: as the system nears its limit, actively reject or slow some new requests, reserving scarce resources for "work already in flight" — trading a little availability for not crashing overall. A classic engineering trade-off: better to say "no" to a few requests than let all of them die together. Grasp this core of "slow down on purpose to survive" and you've got the soul of quota/rate-limiting — it's not to vex users but the system's safety valve.

Worth untangling three terms people often conflate; they're really three links in one chain. Quota is the "allowance" — the per-second ceiling for a class of operation (configurable under quotaAndLimits in configs/milvus.yaml, Lesson 40). Rate limiting is the act of admitting against that allowance — counting at the entrance to decide whether to let a request through. Backpressure is the broader idea of pushing back — when downstream can't keep up, make upstream slow down. Put simply: backpressure is the goal, rate limiting is the means, and quota is the concrete number inside that means. Milvus deliberately lets QuotaCenter compute the quota dynamically from live metrics rather than hardcode a static value, so the safety valve tightens and loosens with the system's load — looser when idle, tighter when busy.

QuotaCenter: the central governor

Who decides "should we slow down now, and by how much"? It's QuotaCenter on RootCoord (internal/rootcoord/quota_center.go). It runs a periodic loop, each round doing three things: collect → compute → distribute. First, collectMetrics(): aggregate live metrics from all nodes — memory water level, disk usage, TimeTick lag (whether consumption keeps up with writes), queue depths. Second, calculateRates(): from these metrics, compute the allowed rate for each operation type right now — writes (DML), queries (DQL), DDL, index building, flush, compaction — each with its own quota. Third, push the computed limits to all Proxies.

Why put this on RootCoord, as a central component? Because rate-limit decisions need a global view. A single Proxy sees only the requests flowing through it; it has no idea "how much memory the whole cluster has left" or "how much write the other Proxies admitted in total". Only a central brain that can survey cluster-wide metrics can correctly decide "what the global limit should be now" — exactly the control plane's job (Lesson 9): QuotaCenter decides, Proxies execute. This again echoes the guide's throughline: converge "decisions needing global info" to the center, spread "acting on decisions" to the edge. The diagram below is QuotaCenter's feedback loop.

node metrics
memory level / disk / TT lag / queue depth
collectMetrics
QuotaCenter (RootCoord)
calculateRates: per-op-type limits
push
all Proxies
receive the latest limits
enforce
allow / reject
over-limit → ErrServiceRateLimit

Two brakes: throttle and force-deny

QuotaCenter's "slowing down" isn't all-or-nothing but two levels of force, matching overload severity. First, throttle (cool-off): when a metric approaches a warning line (say memory at 80%), gradually lower the rate for the matching operation, making requests "queue and go slowly", giving the system room to breathe and digest. A gentle brake — requests still get in, just slower.

Second, force-deny: when a metric breaches a hard limit (memory/disk water level too high, or TT lag dangerously large), QuotaCenter calls forceDenyWriting (drive DML rate straight to 0, reject all writes) or forceDenyReading (DQL to 0, reject all reads). An emergency brake — a whole operation class is temporarily held at the door until the water level recedes to safety. Why such a "harsh" level? Because at the hard limit, admitting more means an OOM/disk-full crash; rather than total wipeout, decisively reject new requests to save the existing ones. Notably, writes and reads are managed separately: under memory/disk pressure, writes (the big resource hog) are often denied first, while reads may still go. The two brakes side by side:

Throttle (gentle)

as a metric approaches the warning line, gradually lower the rate so requests queue and go slowly. Requests still get in, just slower — room to breathe.

Force-deny (emergency)

as a metric breaches a hard limit, forceDenyWriting/Reading drives that rate to 0, rejecting the whole class until levels recede. Reject the new, save the existing.

Enforced at the Proxy: RateLimitInterceptor

Limits are decided by QuotaCenter, but the actual "stopping people" happens at the Proxy entrance. The Proxy has a RateLimitInterceptor (internal/proxy/rate_limit_interceptor.go) — a gRPC interceptor (recall Lesson 38, the Proxy uses interceptors for cross-cutting concerns) that every request passes first: it checks the current rate quota for the request type (write/read/DDL…), admitting if there's quota, rejecting on the spot if not, returning an ErrServiceRateLimit error.

1

Request reaches the Proxy

a write/read/DDL request arrives and first hits the RateLimitInterceptor.

2

Check current quota

by request type, check the latest limit QuotaCenter pushed: is there quota left?

3

Enough → admit

quota available, the request proceeds to normal handling (validate, enqueue, fan out…).

4

Not enough → reject

over limit, return ErrServiceRateLimit (retriable) on the spot; the client backs off and retries.

Here's a subtlety echoing Lesson 44: ErrServiceRateLimit is a retriable (System-class) error — it doesn't say "your request itself is wrong" (that's an Input error, where retry won't help) but "I'm busy now, come back shortly". So a client (SDK) receiving it should back off and retry later, not surface an error to the user. This is backpressure's gentle face: it turns "the system is overloaded" into a signal the client can understand and auto-handle, letting the whole call chain slow down in concert instead of colliding and crashing. To wrap up: QuotaCenter (central — global view, sets limits, force-deny on overload) + RateLimitInterceptor (edge — per-request enforcement, returns a retriable error when over), one central and one edge, together form Milvus's self-protection safety valve. Every piece it uses you've seen — control-plane decisions (Lesson 9), interceptors (Lesson 38), retriable errors (Lesson 44), quota config (Lesson 40), metric monitoring (Lesson 39) — and this lesson twists them into one complete machine for "how a system survives overload".

📌 Key points
  • Why self-protect (backpressure): finite capacity, unrestrained clients; hitting a hard resource ceiling causes crashes, even avalanches; better to slow down on purpose — trade a little availability for not crashing.
  • QuotaCenter (central governor): a RootCoord loop of collectMetrics (memory/disk/TT lag) → calculateRates (per-op-type limits) → push to Proxies; rate decisions need a global view — control-plane work.
  • Two brakes: throttle (near the warning line → gradually lower, queue and go slow) and force-deny (breach a hard limit → forceDenyWriting/Reading to 0, reject the whole class); writes and reads managed separately.
  • Enforced at the Proxy: RateLimitInterceptor checks quota per request and returns ErrServiceRateLimit (retriable, meaning "busy, come back later"); central decision + edge enforcement. Reuses control plane/interceptor/merr/quota/metrics.

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. Why does Milvus need this quota/rate-limiting/backpressure self-protection?
  1. To reduce disk usage
  2. Capacity is finite and clients won't restrain themselves; unbounded writes/queries hit hard ceilings (memory/disk/CPU) causing crashes or avalanches. Better to slow down on purpose — trade a little availability for not crashing
  3. Purely to restrict users and charge more
  4. To make query results more accurate
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. Writes outrunning flush → memory bursts with growing segments; too many queries → resources exhausted; disk full → avalanche-style cascading failure (one node dies, load shifts and crushes others). Hitting a hard ceiling is usually not a graceful slowdown but a crash (OOM-kill, mass timeouts). Backpressure pushes back on upstream when downstream can't keep up, reserving scarce resources for in-flight work — trading a little availability for not crashing. It's a safety valve, not user-vexing.
2. What's QuotaCenter's role, and why is it on RootCoord?
  1. It executes vector search
  2. It's just a static config file
  3. It's the central governor: periodically collectMetrics (memory/disk/TT lag)→calculateRates (per-op-type limits)→push to Proxies. Rate decisions need a global view — control-plane work
  4. It decides limits independently on each QueryNode
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: C. QuotaCenter (rootcoord/quota_center.go) runs a periodic loop: collectMetrics aggregates cluster-wide live metrics (memory level, disk, TimeTick lag, queue depth), calculateRates computes the allowed rate for each op type (DML/DQL/DDL/index/flush/compaction) now, then pushes to all Proxies. It's on RootCoord because rate decisions need a global view — a single Proxy only sees its own traffic, not the whole cluster's remaining memory. That's the control plane's job (L9): decide centrally, enforce at the edge.
3. About the 'two brakes' and Proxy-side enforcement, which is correct?
  1. Throttling is decided by the Proxy alone, independent of QuotaCenter
  2. ErrServiceRateLimit is a non-retriable input error; the client should just error out
  3. Only one level: all-pass or all-stop
  4. Throttle (near the warning line → gradually lower, queue slowly) and force-deny (breach a hard limit → forceDenyWriting/Reading to 0, reject the whole class); the Proxy's RateLimitInterceptor checks per request and returns the retriable ErrServiceRateLimit when over
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: D. Two graded levels: throttle (cool-off) gradually lowers the rate as a metric approaches the warning line, queueing requests (gentle — they still get in, just slower); force-deny, on breaching a hard limit, uses forceDenyWriting/forceDenyReading to drive that rate to 0, rejecting the whole class (emergency — reject the new, save the existing), writes/reads managed separately. Enforced at the Proxy: RateLimitInterceptor (a gRPC interceptor) checks quota per request by type and returns ErrServiceRateLimit when over — it's retriable (System-class, L44), meaning 'busy, come back later'; the client should back off and retry, not error to the user. Central decision + edge enforcement.
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • This lesson says good self-protection is 'not a rigid wall but a rein that tightens smoothly with pressure'. Please: (1) describe QuotaCenter's 'collect→compute→push' loop in your own words, and why rate decisions must sit at a center with a global view (tie to Lesson 9's control plane). (2) Explain why 'two brakes' are needed — what goes wrong with only throttle, or only deny? (3) ErrServiceRateLimit is designed as 'retriable (System)' not 'non-retriable (Input)' — what does that mean for client behavior, and how does it echo Lesson 44's merr Input/System split?

Advanced features tour

To close Part 11, this lesson is a quick tour of several features that are "common in production but not covered before": RBAC (permissions), resource groups (node isolation), databases (multi-tenancy), iterators (paging), TTL (data expiry), and Function (server-side processing). Each gets a "what it is, what it solves, where it lives" — most aren't core engine, but they show up one by one as you put Milvus to real use. Learn them all and your Milvus map extends from the kernel all the way to the production edge.

🔌 Analogy
Think of these as an office building's facility services. An access-control system decides who enters which floor and opens which door (RBAC); floors are zoned and rented to different companies without interference (resource groups); each company has its own business registration with separate books (databases / multi-tenancy); the archive room lets you browse page by page rather than haul everything out at once (iterators); expired files are auto-shredded on schedule (TTL); and the front desk can receive and process deliveries on the spot (Function, e.g. turning text into vectors right there). None of this is "constructing the building" (the core engine); it's the facility power that makes the building actually rentable to many tenants, safely and effortlessly. However well built, without these a building can't be leased or managed.
🌍 Big picture
In one line: RBAC (users/roles/privileges, rootcoord) governs "who can do what"; resource groups (querycoordv2/meta/resource_group.go) partition QueryNodes for isolation; databases (CreateDatabase) add a multi-tenancy layer above collections; search/query iterators page through large result sets; Collection TTL (CollectionTTLConfigKey) auto-expires old data; the Function module (util/function, BM25 / text embedding) turns data into vectors server-side. This is the layer that turns an engine into an operable service.

Security: RBAC (users / roles / privileges)

A real database can't let "anyone who connects do anything". Milvus offers full RBAC (role-based access control): there are users (with credentials), privileges (create collection, insert, search, delete…) are bundled into roles, and roles are granted to users. So managing permissions isn't "configure user by user" but "define a few roles and deal them out" — a typical reader role can only search, an admin role can do everything. The capabilities live in rootcoord (CreateRole / OperatePrivilege / CreateCredential, see ddl_callbacks_rbac_credential.go), with privilege groups bundling a set of privileges and a built-in public role.

privileges
create coll/insert/search/delete…
bundled into
role
e.g. reader (read-only) / admin (full)
granted to
user
with credentials, gets privileges by role

A worthwhile detail: permission metadata is metadata too, so it travels the same Lesson 14 path of "coordinator → Catalog → etcd" and takes effect consistently across the cluster via DDL callbacks — you needn't learn a separate mechanism for RBAC; it's embedded in the metadata picture you already know. In a line: RBAC answers "who can do what to what", the first gate you can't skip whenever many people or workloads share one cluster.

Isolation & multi-tenancy: resource groups + databases

When many workloads share one cluster, two questions arise immediately: will they fight over compute? will they see each other's data? Milvus answers each with a tool. Resource Groups solve compute isolation: they partition QueryNodes into groups (querycoordv2/meta/resource_group.go, each with a request/limit capacity config), so different workloads' collections load onto different node groups — workload A's big query can't crush workload B's nodes. When needed, TransferNode moves nodes between groups for elastic allocation.

Resource groups exist to tame a very real "noisy neighbor" problem. Picture no resource groups: workload A's and workload B's collections share the same QueryNodes, and one day A runs a huge-range batch search that devours those nodes' CPU and memory — so B's online queries slow down or time out, even though B did nothing wrong. That's the noisy neighbor: when resources are shared, one tenant's behavior spills over onto another. The fix is direct — carve out dedicated nodes for the important workload and physically isolate it from everyone else's load. It's the same layer as Lesson 13's QueryCoord assigning segments to nodes, just with the added constraint that "these nodes serve only this group". That's why serious multi-tenant deployments almost always reach for resource groups — isolation isn't a luxury but a precondition for tenants coexisting.

Databases solve naming and data isolation: they add another layer above collections. Recall Lesson 6's hierarchy "collection → partition → segment"; databases extend it upward into "database → collection → partition → segment". Different tenants use different databases, so collection names don't clash and permissions can be granted per database (CreateDatabase, default DB default). Resource groups govern "who gets compute", databases govern "whose data is whose"; together they let one Milvus cluster serve many tenants gracefully. The layered diagram lays out the multi-tenancy hierarchy.

databaseDatabase (the outermost multi-tenancy layer)
different tenants use different DBs; collection names don't clash, permissions/quota per DB (default DB: default)
coll·partCollection → Partition (Lesson 6)
tables and partitioning within a DB; Segment is the physical unit below
res groupResource Group (compute isolation, an orthogonal layer)
partition QueryNodes so different workloads' loads/queries land on different node groups, no compute contention

Convenient retrieval: iterators & TTL

Two small, practical features for "big results" and "old data".
